# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lab-on-a-Chip (LOC) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab-on-a-Chip (LOC)** is a miniaturized device that integrates one or several laboratory functions (mixing, reaction, separation, and detection) onto a single integrated circuit (IC) — compatible microchip of only millimetres to a few square centimetres in footprint. It belongs to the broader field of **Microfluidics** and **Micro Total Analysis Systems (µTAS)**.

The original term **µTAS (Miniaturized Total Chemical Analysis System)** was coined by Andreas Manz in 1990. Later, LOC became the more general, application-driven nomenclature, encompassing biochemical, biomedical, and chemical synthesis applications.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Credit-Card Sized Laboratory"

Imagine a complete pathology laboratory — with beakers, centrifuges, spectrometers, and reaction chambers — shrunk down to the size of a **credit card or even a fingernail**. That is essentially a Lab-on-a-Chip. A drop of blood placed at one end of the chip is automatically drawn through microchannels (by capillary action or electrokinetic pumping), mixed with reagents, and analysed by a tiny sensor that prints out a digital reading. All of this happens without a human technician pipetting anything — the chip itself acts as the laboratory assistant.

---

## 1.2 Biochemical Sensor (in LOC context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Biochemical Sensor / Biosensor** is an analytical device that converts a biological recognition event (e.g., enzyme-substrate reaction, antibody-antigen binding, DNA hybridization) into a measurable electrical or optical signal, comprising a **bioreceptor**, a **transducer**, and a **signal processor**.

The three classic components of a biosensor are:
1. **Bioreceptor (Biological Element)** — Enzyme, antibody, nucleic acid, cell, or tissue that recognizes the analyte specifically.
2. **Transducer (Physical Element)** — Converts the biochemical interaction into an electrical/optical signal (electrochemical, optical, piezoelectric, thermometric).
3. **Signal Processor (Electronic Element)** — Amplifies, filters, and displays the signal in user-friendly form.

In an LOC, the transducer is often co-integrated with the microfluidic channels on the same substrate (glass, silicon, PDMS, or paper).

---

## 1.3 Miniaturized Systems for (Bio) Chemical Analysis and Synthesis

> [!NOTE]
> **Miniaturized (Bio) Chemical Analysis Systems** refer to engineered micro-scale platforms that perform complete chemical assays, synthesis, and detection protocols using sub-millilitre (often sub-microlitre) reagent volumes. They are characterized by **low reagent consumption, fast analysis times, low cost, and high portability**.

These systems leverage:
- **Microfluidic principles** — controlled fluid motion in channels of 1–1000 µm cross-section.
- **Surface-area-to-volume ratio (SA:V) advantage** — microchannels have SA:V 10–100× higher than beakers, leading to faster heat/mass transfer.
- **Laminar flow regimes** — characterized by very low Reynolds numbers (Re < 1), making flow predictable and diffusion-controlled.

---

## 1.4 Glucometer — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Glucometer (Glucose Meter)** is a portable, hand-held **Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) device** used to measure the **concentration of glucose in blood** (typically capillary whole blood from a finger-prick), giving a digital readout in **mg/dL** or **mmol/L** within 5–10 seconds.

### Working Principle Snapshot

The dominant commercial principle is **Electrochemical (Amperometric) Biosensing** using the enzyme **Glucose Oxidase (GOx)** or **Glucose Dehydrogenase (GDH)**:

1. A **test strip** with a capillary inlet is touched to a blood drop (~0.3–1.0 µL).
2. Blood wicks into the strip by **capillary action**.
3. Glucose reacts with GOx:
   - Glucose + O₂ → Gluconolactone + H₂O₂
4. The H₂O₂ produced is oxidized at the working electrode at a fixed potential (~+0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl).
5. The resulting current (nA to µA) is proportional to glucose concentration.
6. The meter's microprocessor converts current → concentration using a calibration curve.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sugar Detective in Your Pocket"

Think of the glucometer as a tiny courtroom judge: the **blood drop is the witness**, the **enzyme on the strip is the detective** that interrogates the witness for "glucose" (the suspect), and the **electrode is the stenographer** that records a measurable confession (current) proportional to how much "glucose suspect" was found. The judge (microprocessor) then translates that confession into a number — your sugar level.

---

## 1.5 Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Point-of-Care Testing (POCT)** refers to medical diagnostic testing performed at or near the **site of patient care** (bedside, home, ambulance, rural clinic), outside the central laboratory, providing **rapid results (typically < 30 minutes)** that enable immediate clinical decision-making.

### WHO "ASSURED" Criteria for an Ideal POCT Device

| Criterion | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **A**ffordable | Cost-effective for low-resource settings |
| **S**ensitive | Low false-negative rate |
| **S**pecific | Low false-positive rate |
| **U**ser-friendly | Minimal training required |
| **R**apid & Robust | Results in minutes, works in harsh environments |
| **E**quipment-free | No cold chain, no mains power needed ideally |
| **D**eliverable | Reaches end-users in remote areas |

Common POCT devices include: **Glucometer, Pulse Oximeter, Pregnancy Test, Troponin Cardiac Marker, HbA1c, Blood Gas Analyzer, Lateral Flow Assays (COVID-19 antigen tests)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "ATM for Diagnostics"

If a central hospital lab is a full bank branch (slow, comprehensive, expert-dependent), then a **POCT device is an ATM** — fast, automated, and placed right where the user is. You swipe (place a sample), get a result, and walk away with actionable information within minutes.

---

## 1.6 Comparison Table — LOC vs Conventional Lab

| Parameter | Conventional Lab | Lab-on-a-Chip |
|---|---|---|
| Sample volume | mL | nL–µL |
| Analysis time | Hours–Days | Seconds–Minutes |
| Reagent cost | High | Reduced 10–1000× |
| Portability | Bench-bound | Pocket-sized |
| Automation | Manual multi-step | Fully integrated |
| Cost per test | Moderate–High | Low (post-fabrication) |
| Throughput | Moderate | High (parallelization) |

---

## 1.7 Important Metric Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard physical/analytical constants used in LOC/POCT designs:
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C: µ ≈ 0.89 mPa·s (0.89 cP)**
> - **Density of water: ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Surface tension of water at 25 °C: γ ≈ 0.072 N/m**
> - **Diffusion coefficient of glucose in water: D ≈ 6.7 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s**
> - **Standard finger-prick blood volume for glucometer: ~ 0.3–1.0 µL**

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microfluidic Foundations for LOC

### 2.1.1 Reynolds Number & Flow Regime

In microchannels, fluid flow is almost always **laminar** because characteristic lengths (l) are very small (1–500 µm), driving Re ≪ 100 (typically **Re < 1**).

> **Re = (ρ · v · D_h) / µ**

Where:
- ρ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- v = mean velocity (m/s)
- D_h = hydraulic diameter (m) = 4A/P (cross-section / wetted perimeter)
- µ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

At **Re < 2300** → Laminar flow; **Re > 4000** → Turbulent flow.

### 2.1.2 Capillary Action (Passive Pumping)

Spontaneous filling of microchannels by capillary force is governed by the **Young–Laplace equation**:

> **ΔP = (2 γ · cos θ) / r**

Where:
- ΔP = pressure drop across the meniscus (Pa)
- γ = surface tension (N/m)
- θ = contact angle between fluid and channel wall
- r = radius of the capillary / equivalent channel radius (m)

For θ < 90° (hydrophilic surfaces like glass, PDMS treated with O₂ plasma) → cos θ > 0 → ΔP > 0 → fluid is **pulled in**.

### 2.1.3 Hagen–Poiseuille Law (Volumetric Flow in Microchannels)

For a circular microchannel of radius r and length L, with pressure drop ΔP:

> **Q = (π · r⁴ · ΔP) / (8 · µ · L)**

For rectangular microchannels (width w, height h, w > h):

> **Q = (w · h³ · ΔP) / (12 · µ · L) · [1 − (192·h / (π⁵·w)) · Σ (tanh terms)...]**

The simpler approximation for w ≫ h is:
> **Q ≈ (w · h³ · ΔP) / (12 · µ · L)**

This is **why microchannels are made shallow (small h)**: the h³ dependence makes flow highly sensitive to depth.

### 2.1.4 Electrokinetic Flow (EOF)

When a microchannel wall carries a surface charge (e.g., glass → SiO⁻), an applied axial electric field E drags the diffuse ionic cloud, dragging the bulk fluid with it:

> **v_EOF = (ε · ζ · E) / µ**

Where:
- ε = permittivity of the medium
- ζ = zeta potential of the channel wall (V)
- E = applied electric field (V/m)
- µ = viscosity (Pa·s)

EOF is **plug-flow** (flat velocity profile), unlike the **parabolic** Hagen–Poiseuille profile — advantageous for sample plug dispersion control.

---

## 2.2 Electrochemical Glucose Sensing — Core Equations

### 2.2.1 Amperometric Sensor (Most Common in Glucometers)

For the GOx enzymatic reaction:
> **Glucose + O₂ →(GOx)→ Gluconolactone + H₂O₂**

At the working electrode (held at +0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl):
> **H₂O₂ → 2H⁺ + O₂ + 2e⁻**

The resulting **diffusion-limited current** at a planar microelectrode follows the **Cottrell equation**:

> **i(t) = (n · F · A · C · √D) / (√(π · t))**

Where:
- i(t) = current at time t (Amperes)
- n = number of electrons transferred (n = 2 for H₂O₂)
- F = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**
- A = electrode area (m²)
- C = bulk concentration of electroactive species (mol/m³)
- D = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- t = time (s)

> **At fixed time t, the current is directly proportional to glucose concentration C → linear calibration curve**.

### 2.2.2 Colorimetric Glucose Sensor (Alternative)

Some glucometers and POCT strips use **color change** (e.g., o-toluidine, glucose oxidase–peroxidase–o-dianisidine chemistry). The intensity of the colored product is read by a photodiode, obeying the **Beer–Lambert Law**:

> **A = ε · c · l**

Where:
- A = absorbance (dimensionless)
- ε = molar absorptivity (L · mol⁻¹ · cm⁻¹)
- c = concentration (mol/L)
- l = optical path length (cm)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-stop Cheat Sheet** for numerical problems on LOC / Glucometer / POCT.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Re = ρ v D_h / µ | Reynolds number | ρ (kg/m³), v (m/s), D_h (m), µ (Pa·s) |
| 2 | ΔP = 2γ cos θ / r | Young–Laplace capillary pressure | γ (N/m), θ (°), r (m) |
| 3 | Q = π r⁴ ΔP / (8 µ L) | Hagen–Poiseuille flow (circular) | Q (m³/s) |
| 4 | Q ≈ w h³ ΔP / (12 µ L) | Hagen–Poiseuille flow (rectangular) | w,h (m) |
| 5 | v_EOF = ε ζ E / µ | Electroosmotic velocity | ε (F/m), ζ (V), E (V/m) |
| 6 | i(t) = n F A C √D / √(π t) | Cottrell current (amperometry) | i (A), A (m²), C (mol/m³), t (s) |
| 7 | A = ε c l | Beer–Lambert absorbance | A (—), c (mol/L), l (cm) |
| 8 | t_diff = L² / (2D) | Diffusion time across length L | t (s), L (m), D (m²/s) |
| 9 | t_filling = 8 µ L² / (r² ΔP) | Capillary filling time | L (m), r (m) |
| 10 | Pe = v L / D | Péclet number (advection vs diffusion) | v (m/s), L (m), D (m²/s) |
| 11 | C_Plasma (mg/dL) ≈ C_WholeBlood × 1.15 | Plasma correction factor for glucometer | mg/dL |

---

## 2.4 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering

- **Microfluidic engineers** use Hagen–Poiseuille to design channel dimensions for desired flow rates at low pressure (avoiding pump complexity).
- **Biomedical engineers** use Cottrell to size electrodes and time windows for accurate glucose readings in handheld meters.
- **Diagnostic device manufacturers** use Beer–Lambert to calibrate colorimetric test strips (HbA1c, pregnancy, COVID antigen).
- **POCT designers** apply WHO ASSURED criteria to develop devices deployable in rural/field settings (no centrifuge, no cold chain).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille Flow in a Rectangular Microchannel

This is the most common microchannel geometry in LOC devices (fabricated by soft lithography in PDMS or by wet etching in glass).

### Given

- Rectangular channel of width **w**, height **h**, length **L**
- Pressure drop **ΔP** applied across the length
- Incompressible, Newtonian fluid of viscosity **µ**
- Steady-state, fully-developed laminar flow (Re < 1)
- No-slip at walls, w ≫ h (so we can treat it as 2D between two parallel plates separated by h)

### Derivation

Start with the Navier–Stokes equation in the x-direction (along channel length), with the flow only depending on z (height):

$$
\frac{d^{2} v_{x}(z)}{dz^{2}} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

This is a second-order ODE. Integrate once:

$$
\frac{d v_{x}}{dz} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot z + C_{1}
$$

Integrate again:

$$
v_{x}(z) = \frac{1}{2 \mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot z^{2} + C_{1} z + C_{2}
$$

Apply **no-slip boundary conditions** at the walls z = ±h/2:
- v_x(+h/2) = 0
- v_x(−h/2) = 0

By symmetry, C₁ = 0. Apply v_x(h/2) = 0:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{2 \mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} + C_{2}
$$

$$
C_{2} = -\frac{\Delta P \, h^{2}}{8 \mu L}
$$

Therefore the **parabolic velocity profile** is:

$$
v_{x}(z) = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ z^{2} - \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} \right] = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( z^{2} - \frac{h^{2}}{4} \right)
$$

The **volumetric flow rate** is the integral of v_x across the cross-section:

$$
Q = w \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} v_{x}(z) \, dz
$$

$$
Q = \frac{w \, \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \left( z^{2} - \frac{h^{2}}{4} \right) dz
$$

$$
Q = \frac{w \, \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{z^{3}}{3} - \frac{h^{2} z}{4} \right]_{-h/2}^{+h/2}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{w \, \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \left(\frac{h^{3}}{24} - \frac{h^{3}}{8}\right) - \left(-\frac{h^{3}}{24} + \frac{h^{3}}{8}\right) \right]
$$

$$
Q = \frac{w \, \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ -\frac{h^{3}}{12} - \frac{h^{3}}{12} \right]
$$

$$
Q = -\frac{w \, h^{3} \, \Delta P}{24 \mu L}
$$

The negative sign simply means we defined ΔP opposite to flow direction. Taking magnitudes:

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{w \, h^{3} \, \Delta P}{12 \, \mu \, L}}
$$

### Physical Insight
The **h³ dependence** is critical: halving the channel height reduces flow by **8×**. This is why microchannels are designed with h in the 10–100 µm range — too deep = uncontrollable flow, too shallow = clogging and very high resistance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Filling Time of a Microchannel

### Given
- Rectangular microchannel (width w, height h, length L)
- Capillary pressure ΔP_c from Young–Laplace
- Fluid of viscosity µ

### Derivation
By Young–Laplace:
$$
\Delta P_{c} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{h}} \quad \text{where } r_{h} = \frac{h}{2} \text{ (for a flat channel)}
$$

So:
$$
\Delta P_{c} = \frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{h}
$$

Substitute into Hagen–Poiseuille (rectangular):
$$
Q = \frac{w h^{3}}{12 \mu L} \cdot \frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{h} = \frac{w h^{2} \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \mu L}
$$

The **linear filling velocity** v = Q / (w h):
$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{h \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \mu L_{fill}(t)}
$$

Note: L_fill(t) is the instantaneous filled length, which grows with time. So:
$$
L_{fill} \, dL_{fill} = \frac{h \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \mu} \, dt
$$

Integrate from 0 to L (filling the full channel in time t_fill):
$$
\frac{L^{2}}{2} = \frac{h \gamma \cos\theta}{3 \mu} \, t_{fill}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{fill} = \frac{3 \mu L^{2}}{2 h \gamma \cos\theta}}
$$

### Numerical Example (Typical LOC)
- µ = 1 mPa·s = 10⁻³ Pa·s
- L = 10 mm = 10⁻² m
- h = 50 µm = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m
- γ = 0.072 N/m
- cos θ ≈ 1 (very hydrophilic, e.g., plasma-treated PDMS)

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{3 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (10^{-2})^{2}}{2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.072 \cdot 1} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-7}}{7.2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.0417 \, s
$$

→ **Capillary filling of a 1 cm microchannel occurs in ~40 milliseconds**. This is why LOC sample loading is essentially instantaneous.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cottrell Equation for Amperometric Glucose Detection

### Given
- Planar microelectrode of area **A** in a semi-infinite diffusion layer
- Step potential applied at t = 0; electroactive species H₂O₂ at bulk concentration **C**
- Diffusion coefficient **D**, n electrons transferred per molecule

### Derivation
Fick's second law in 1D (x = distance from electrode):
$$
\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C(x,t)}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

**Initial condition:** C(x,0) = C₀ (uniform)
**Boundary condition 1:** C(∞,t) = C₀ (far from electrode)
**Boundary condition 2:** C(0,t) = 0 (concentration of H₂O₂ at electrode surface is zero because it's instantaneously consumed)

This is a classic semi-infinite diffusion problem. The solution (using error function expansion) is:
$$
C(x,t) = C_{0} \, \text{erf}\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \right)
$$

The flux at the electrode surface (x = 0):
$$
J(0,t) = -D \left. \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{C_{0} \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi t}}
$$

The current (charge per unit time per electron × n):
$$
i(t) = n F A J(0,t)
$$

$$
\boxed{i(t) = \frac{n F A C_{0} \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi t}}}
$$

### Inverse Calibration Form
For a fixed sampling time **t_s** (the meter's ADC reads current at a defined time), the current is **linear in C₀**:
$$
i = k \cdot C_{0} \quad \text{where } k = \frac{n F A \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi t_{s}}}
$$

This **k** is the meter's calibration factor (typically determined per batch using reference glucose solutions at 50, 100, 200, 400 mg/dL).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — LOC vs µTAS vs BioMEMS vs POCT

| Parameter | Lab-on-a-Chip (LOC) | µTAS | BioMEMS | POCT Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Integration of multi-step assay | Continuous flow analysis | Mechanical + biological micro-devices | End-user clinical decision |
| **Typical Material** | PDMS, Glass, Paper, Silicon | Glass, Silicon | Silicon, Polymer | Plastic, Paper, Polymer |
| **Sample Volume** | nL–µL | µL | Variable | µL–drop |
| **Throughput** | High (parallel) | Continuous | Variable | Single test |
| **User Skill** | Trained technician | Lab researcher | Specialized | Lay user / home |
| **Power Required** | Often yes (pumps) | Yes (detector) | Yes | Battery / solar / none |
| **Example** | Multi-organ-on-chip, droplet PCR | Capillary electrophoresis chip | Microneedle array, micro-pump | Glucometer, pregnancy test |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Glucometer Calibration

**Problem:** A glucometer uses a planar gold microelectrode of area A = 0.5 mm². At a fixed readout time t = 5 s after the sample is applied, the diffusion-limited current for a reference 100 mg/dL glucose solution is i_ref = 1.85 µA. Calculate the calibration factor k and predict the current for a patient sample reading 180 mg/dL.

**Solution:**

Concentration 100 mg/dL glucose:
- Molecular weight of glucose = 180 g/mol
- 100 mg/dL = 1 g/L = 1 / 180 mol/L = 5.56 × 10⁻³ mol/L = 5.56 mol/m³

Assuming linear response: i = k·C

$$
k = \frac{i_{ref}}{C_{ref}} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^{-6} \, A}{5.56 \, mol/m^{3}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-7} \, A \cdot m^{3}/mol
$$

For 180 mg/dL = 1.8 × 5.56 = 10.0 mol/m³:

$$
i_{patient} = k \cdot C_{patient} = 3.33 \times 10^{-7} \times 10.0 = 3.33 \, \mu A
$$

Expected display: **180 mg/dL** ✓

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample]**
**Define Lab-on-a-Chip (LOC) and list any four advantages over conventional laboratory analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Lab-on-a-Chip (LOC)** is a miniaturized device that integrates one or more laboratory functions — including sample preparation, fluid handling, reaction, separation, and detection — onto a single microchip of millimetre-to-centimetre footprint, typically using microchannel dimensions of 1–500 µm. It is a subset of the broader field of **Microfluidics** and the historical concept of **Micro Total Analysis Systems (µTAS)**.

**Four advantages (any four, ½ mark each):**
1. **Reduced sample/reagent volume** — nanolitres to microlitres vs millilitres (×100–1000 saving).
2. **Faster analysis time** — diffusion-limited transport over small distances; results in seconds to minutes vs hours/days.
3. **Lower cost per test** — inexpensive mass-fabricated chips (similar to semiconductor industry).
4. **Portability & POCT suitability** — pocket-sized, battery-powered, deployable in field.
5. **Higher throughput** — easy parallelization (many channels on one chip).
6. **Better analytical performance** — high surface-to-volume ratio improves reaction kinetics, sensitivity.

**Examiner tip:** A clean definition (1 mark) + 4 distinct points (½ × 4 = 2 marks) is the standard KTU marking pattern.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]**
**Explain the working principle of an electrochemical glucometer with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** A glucometer is an amperometric biosensor that uses the enzyme **glucose oxidase (GOx)** immobilized on the working electrode of a disposable test strip.

Reaction sequence:
1. Capillary action draws ~0.5 µL of capillary blood onto the strip.
2. **Enzymatic reaction:** Glucose + O₂ →(GOx)→ Gluconolactone + H₂O₂.
3. **Electro-oxidation at working electrode (held at ~+0.4 V vs Ag/AgCl reference):**
   H₂O₂ → 2H⁺ + O₂ + 2e⁻
4. The resulting current (nA–µA) follows the **Cottrell equation**: i(t) ∝ C_glucose / √t.
5. The microprocessor samples current at a fixed time t and converts it to mg/dL using a stored calibration curve.

**Sketch (1 mark):** Draw a test strip showing: capillary inlet → reagent layer (GOx) → working electrode + reference electrode → wire contacts to meter. Label the three-electrode configuration.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks on these questions by: (a) writing only definitions without diagrams, (b) skipping the chemical equations, (c) failing to mention the transducer type, and (d) not comparing LOC vs POCT explicitly when asked. Always: define → diagram → principle → equation → example → advantages.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) device** for blood glucose estimation. Discuss the role of each functional block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Cottrell equation** for amperometric sensing and show how it forms the basis of glucose quantification in a handheld glucometer. A planar gold microelectrode of area 0.8 mm² is used in a glucometer. The diffusion coefficient of H₂O₂ in the reagent layer is D = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s. At t = 4 s, the current is measured as 2.4 µA for a reference glucose solution of 90 mg/dL. Calculate the calibration factor (in µA·m³/mol) and the meter-display reading when the same patient has a current of 3.6 µA. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q. A(a) — 7 marks:**

**POCT Glucometer Block Architecture** (block diagram: 2 marks; explain blocks 5 × 1 mark = 5 marks)

| Functional Block | Role |
|---|---|
| **1. Sample Collection Unit (Capillary Inlet)** | Touch the strip to a finger-prick blood drop; capillary wicks in 0.3–1.0 µL automatically. |
| **2. Reagent Layer (Bioreceptor)** | Contains immobilized GOx enzyme + redox mediator (ferrocyanide or ferrocene). Glucose is selectively oxidized. |
| **3. Transducer (Electrodes)** | Three-electrode setup — Working (Au/Carbon), Counter, Reference (Ag/AgCl). Converts biochemical reaction to electrical current. |
| **4. Potentiostat Circuit** | Maintains the working electrode at a fixed potential (+0.4 V) vs reference. |
| **5. Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA)** | Converts small current (nA–µA) into a measurable voltage. |
| **6. ADC + Microcontroller** | Digitizes voltage; applies temperature compensation and linearization (calibration curve). |
| **7. Display & Optional Connectivity** | LCD shows mg/dL; Bluetooth/USB sends to phone/cloud for tracking. |

The flow: **Sample → Reagent → Transducer → Electronics → Display**.

---

**Model Solution for Q. A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Cottrell Derivation (5 marks):** Refer to Section 3.3 above. The Cottrell equation is:

$$
i(t) = \frac{n F A C \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi t}}
$$

For H₂O₂ oxidation: n = 2, F = 96485 C/mol.

**Numerical Part (2 marks):**

- A = 0.8 mm² = 0.8 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- D = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s
- t = 4 s
- i_ref = 2.4 µA = 2.4 × 10⁻⁶ A
- C_ref = 90 mg/dL = 0.9 g/L = 0.9/180 mol/L = 5 × 10⁻³ mol/L = 5 mol/m³

Calibration factor k = i_ref / C_ref = 2.4 × 10⁻⁶ / 5 = **4.8 × 10⁻⁷ A·m³/mol** (or 0.48 µA·L/mol).

For i_patient = 3.6 µA:
- C_patient = 3.6 × 10⁻⁶ / 4.8 × 10⁻⁷ = **7.5 mol/m³** = 7.5 mmol/L
- In mg/dL: 7.5 × 180 / 10 = **135 mg/dL**

→ **The meter will display 135 mg/dL** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a)** What is a **Micro Total Analysis System (µTAS)**? Compare and contrast it with a conventional clinical chemistry analyzer. Discuss the role of **microfluidic principles** — capillary action, electroosmotic flow, and laminar diffusion — in enabling LOC functionality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular PDMS microchannel of width w = 200 µm, height h = 50 µm, length L = 5 cm is filled by capillary action. The fluid has surface tension γ = 0.072 N/m, viscosity µ = 1 mPa·s, and the contact angle θ = 30° (after O₂ plasma treatment). Calculate **(i)** the capillary pressure driving the flow, **(ii)** the volumetric flow rate, and **(iii)** the time to fill the channel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q. B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** µTAS (Manz, 1990) integrates **all steps of a chemical analysis — sample injection, transport, reaction, separation, detection — onto a single microchip**, with channel cross-sections typically 10–500 µm.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | µTAS / LOC | Conventional Lab Analyzer |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | nL–µL | mL |
| Time | Seconds–Minutes | Hours |
| Cost/test | Low (mass-produced chips) | High (reagents, technician) |
| Portability | Pocket-sized | Bench / central lab |
| Automation | Fully integrated | Multi-step manual + automated |
| Cross-contamination | Minimal (disposable) | Possible |

**Microfluidic Principles (3 marks):**
1. **Capillary action** — provides passive, pump-free sample loading; governed by Young–Laplace equation; depends on surface tension γ and contact angle θ.
2. **Electroosmotic flow (EOF)** — enables precise plug-flow pumping without moving parts; useful for electrophoretic separation on chip.
3. **Laminar diffusion** — at low Reynolds numbers, mixing is purely by molecular diffusion; can be exploited for H-filter extractors and gradient generators.

---

**Model Solution for Q. B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** w = 200 µm = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m; h = 50 µm = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m; L = 5 cm = 0.05 m; γ = 0.072 N/m; µ = 10⁻³ Pa·s; θ = 30° → cos 30° = √3/2 ≈ 0.866.

**(i) Capillary Pressure (2 marks):** For a rectangular channel, r_h = h/2:

$$
\Delta P_{c} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{h/2} = \frac{4 \gamma \cos\theta}{h}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{c} = \frac{4 \times 0.072 \times 0.866}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.2494}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4988 \approx 4990 \, Pa
$$

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate (2 marks):**

$$
Q = \frac{w h^{3} \Delta P}{12 \mu L} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(5 \times 10^{-5})^{3}(4988)}{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(1.25 \times 10^{-13})(4988)}{6 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.247 \times 10^{-13}}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.08 \times 10^{-10} \, m^{3}/s \approx 0.208 \, \mu L/s
$$

**(iii) Filling Time (3 marks):** From derived formula in Section 3.2:

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{3 \mu L^{2}}{2 h \gamma \cos\theta} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.05)^{2}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.072 \times 0.866}
$$

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-6}}{6.24 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.20 \, s
$$

**Verification using average velocity:**

$$
v_{avg} = \frac{Q}{w h} = \frac{2.08 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.08 \times 10^{-2} \, m/s
$$

$$
t = \frac{L}{v_{avg}} = \frac{0.05}{0.0208} = 2.40 \, s
$$

(Slight discrepancy is because the constant-flow Q was calculated using fully-developed steady-state assumption, while the actual filling is a transient; the derived formula gives the exact transient result of ~1.2 s — more accurate).

→ **The channel fills in approximately 1.2 seconds** — a perfect illustration of the speed of passive microfluidic loading.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 LOC / Glucometer / POCT**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **LOC** = Miniaturized lab on a microchip using µL–nL volumes; rooted in **µTAS** (Manz, 1990).
- ☐ **POCT** = Diagnostic testing at patient site; results in minutes; meets WHO **ASSURED** criteria.
- ☐ **Glucometer** = Most widely used POCT device; amperometric biosensor with **GOx enzyme**.

### Key Equations (Memorize)
- ☐ Re = ρvD_h / µ (laminar when Re < 2300; in microchannels Re ≪ 1)
- ☐ ΔP_capillary = 2γ cos θ / r
- ☐ Q (rectangular) = wh³ΔP / (12µL)
- ☐ v_EOF = ε ζ E / µ
- ☐ i_Cottrell = n F A C √D / √(π t) — linear in concentration
- ☐ A = ε c l (Beer–Lambert for colorimetric sensors)

### Microfluidic Phenomena
- ☐ **Capillary action** (passive pumping)
- ☐ **Electroosmotic flow** (EOF, plug profile)
- ☐ **Laminar flow** (no turbulence in microchannels)
- ☐ **Dominant diffusion** (high SA:V ratio)
- ☐ **h³ scaling** of flow resistance (shallow channels are highly resistive)

### Glucometer Specifics
- ☐ Working electrode potential ≈ **+0.4 V vs Ag/AgCl**
- ☐ Blood sample volume: **0.3–1.0 µL**
- ☐ Reaction: **Glucose + O₂ →(GOx)→ Gluconolactone + H₂O₂**; H₂O₂ electro-oxidized.
- ☐ Current is **directly proportional to glucose concentration** (Cottrell).
- ☐ Plasma correction: C_plasma ≈ **1.15 × C_whole_blood** (lab vs meter).
- ☐ Measurement time: **5–10 seconds**.

### POCT Device Categories to Know
- ☐ Glucometer, Pulse oximeter, Pregnancy test, Troponin test, HbA1c, COVID antigen LFA, Blood gas analyzer.

### Common Numerical Conversions
- ☐ 1 mg/dL glucose = **0.0555 mmol/L**
- ☐ 1 mmol/L = **18 mg/dL**
- ☐ M.W. glucose = **180 g/mol**

### Examiner Favourites
- ☐ "Explain LOC with block diagram" — define + diagram + advantages.
- ☐ "Working of glucometer" — must include **Cottrell equation** for full marks.
- ☐ "Capillary filling time" derivation in microchannels.
- ☐ Comparison: LOC vs conventional lab, LOC vs µTAS vs POCT.
- ☐ "POCT + WHO ASSURED criteria" — frequently asked as 5–7 mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrocardiograph: Pre-amplifiers, Filters, Isolation Circuits, RL Driven Circuit, Protection Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electrocardiograph: Pre-amplifiers, Filters, Isolation Circuits, RL Driven Circuit, Protection Circuits # MODULE 3 — ELECTROCARDIOGRAPH (ECG) FRONT-END ARCHITECTURE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Electrocardiograph? An **Electrocardiograph (ECG / EKG)** is a bioelectric signal acquisition system that records the electrical activity of the heart by sensing skin-surface potentials generated by myocardial depolarization and repolarization. The measured biopotential is in the range of **0.5 mV to 5 mV** with a frequency content from **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**. The ECG front-end must perform four critical engineering tasks: 1. **Amplify** the weak differential signal riding on a large common-mode voltage (up to **±1.5 V**). 2. **Filter** baseline wander (low-frequency motion artifact) and high-frequency EMG/noise. 3. **Isolate** the patient from any possibility of hazardous leakage current (max allowable **10 µA** through patient per IEC 60601-1). 4. **Protect** the sensitive input electronics from defibrillator shocks (up to **5000 V**) and electro-surgical (ESU) interference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Students must clearly distinguish the roles of *Pre-amplification*, *Filtering*, *Isolation*, *Driven-Right-Leg (DRL)*, and *Protection*. These are **separate functional blocks**, often tested as independent 7-mark questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microphone for the Heart" Imagine trying to hear a whisper in a noisy stadium. The ECG problem is mathematically identical: - The **whisper** = the differential ECG signal (~1 mV). - The **stadium roar** = the common-mode 50 Hz mains hum and electrode DC offset. - The **pre-amplifier** = your eardrum with selective attention. - The **filters** = noise-cancelling headphones. - The **isolation barrier** = a glass window that prevents stadium electricity from shocking the listener. - The **RL Driven Circuit** = a smart "anti-loudspeaker" that cancels out the stadium roar before it reaches your ears. > [!NOTE] > **Standard ECG Frequency Range:** 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz (diagnostic) or 0.5 Hz – 40 Hz (monitoring mode). The notch filter at **50 Hz** (India/Europe) or **60 Hz** (USA) is *additional*, not a substitute for proper shielding. ### 1.3 Block Diagram of ECG Front-End (Textual Overview) The standard architecture is: **Electrodes → Protection → Pre-amp (Instrumentation Amp) → Filters (HPF + LPF + Notch) → Isolation Barrier → Output Stage → Display/ADC**. Every block has a non-negotiable engineering specification. The pre-amp is always an **instrumentation amplifier (INA)** because no other topology simultaneously achieves the required **CMRR ≥ 80 dB**, **input impedance ≥ 10 MΩ**, and **adjustable gain**. ### 1.4 Key Specifications (Boilerplate for All ECG Front-Ends) | Parameter | Required Value | Reason | |---|---|---| | Differential Gain | **1000** (≈ 60 dB) | Lift 1 mV ECG to ~1 V ADC input | | CMRR | **≥ 80 dB** | Reject 50 Hz mains coupled equally on both leads | | Input Impedance | **≥ 10 MΩ** | Avoid loading the skin-electrode interface | | Leakage Current | **≤ 10 µA** | Patient safety (IEC 60601) | | Isolation Voltage | **≥ 5000 V** | Defibrillator + mains breakdown protection | | Noise | **< 40 µV p-p** | Below ECG signal of interest | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Instrumentation Pre-Amplifier (INA) The pre-amp is the heart of the ECG. The industry-standard topology is the **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** shown conceptually below: - Two input op-amps act as **buffer-and-gain** stages sharing a single gain-setting resistor $R_G$. - The third op-amp is a **unity-gain subtractor** that converts the differential output to a single-ended output and rejects common-mode voltage. ### 2.1.1 Differential Gain Derivation Let the inputs be $V_1$ and $V_2$ applied to the two non-inverting inputs of the input buffers. The single shared resistor $R_G$ sets the gain. **KTU Formula Sheet:** | Formula | Expression | Use | |---|---|---| | Differential Gain | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Total INA gain | | CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection metric | | Common-Mode Gain | $A_{cm} = 0$ (ideal) | Should be minimized via matched resistors | | Input Impedance | $Z_{in} \approx 2Z_{in,\text{opamp}}$ (very high) | Avoids electrode loading | | Noise (INA) | $e_n \approx e_{n,\text{opamp}}\sqrt{2}$ | Total referred-to-input | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks "Why use an INA and not a simple op-amp differential amplifier?" The answer is *high input impedance on both inputs* and *single-resistor gain control*. A bare op-amp differencer has unbalanced input impedances, which destroys CMRR when driven through electrode impedances. ### 2.2 Filter Subsystem Three filters are typically cascaded: **a) High-Pass Filter (HPF) — 0.05 Hz cutoff** Removes DC electrode offset and baseline wander. First-order RC HPF: $$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$ **b) Low-Pass Filter (LPF) — 150 Hz cutoff** Limits bandwidth to suppress EMG, RF, and thermal noise. First-order RC LPF uses the same equation. Higher-order filters (Butterworth, Bessel) are used for sharp roll-off. **c) Notch Filter — 50 Hz (India)** Twin-T or active Wien-bridge topology to suppress mains interference without attenuating the 50 Hz ECG harmonics (since ECG spectrum contains useful 50 Hz energy). **Notch Filter Formula:** $$f_{notch} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$ For symmetric design $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$: $$f_{notch} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$ The quality factor $Q$ governs the notch depth and bandwidth: $$Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 / C_2}}{R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_1 + R_2 C_2(1 - R_1/R_2)}$$ ### 2.3 Isolation Circuit An isolation barrier breaks any direct electrical path between the patient and the mains-powered side. Two dominant techniques exist: **1. Transformer Isolation** — analog signal is modulated, passed through a transformer with **≥ 5000 V dielectric rating**, then demodulated. Used in older analog ECG machines. **2. Optocoupler Isolation** — LED on patient side illuminates a phototransistor on the output side; signal is encoded as light pulses. Modern preference for digital ECG. > [!IMPORTANT] > The barrier must withstand both **continuous mains voltage** (230 V) and **transient defibrillator pulses** (~5000 V). Insulation resistance must be **≥ 10 MΩ**, and creepage/clearance distances follow IEC 60601-1. ### 2.4 Right-Leg Driven (RLD) Circuit — Common-Mode Feedback A conventional INA rejects common-mode voltage *passively* through finite CMRR. The **Driven Right-Leg (DRL)** circuit *actively cancels* the common-mode voltage at the body. **Working Principle:** 1. The common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ is sensed from the two electrode leads (averaged through resistors). 2. $V_{cm}$ is inverted and amplified by an auxiliary op-amp. 3. The inverted signal is fed back to the **third electrode** (Right Leg, RL). 4. This drives the patient's body toward the amplifier reference potential, forcing $V_{cm} \approx 0$. **KTU Formula:** The effective common-mode voltage is reduced by a factor equal to the loop gain $A_{loop}$ of the DRL amplifier: $$V_{cm,\text{effective}} = \dfrac{V_{cm,\text{original}}}{1 + A_{loop}}$$ This boosts the effective CMRR by orders of magnitude (often **> 100 dB** combined with the INA's intrinsic CMRR). > [!CAUTION] > The DRL current must be **limited to ≤ 10 µA** (or ≤ 50 µA with respect to ground) by a current-limiting resistor to comply with patient safety standards. A typical value is **1 MΩ** in series with the RL electrode. ### 2.5 Protection Circuits **a) Input Protection (Defibrillator-Proof)** - **Gas discharge tubes (GDT)** or **Transient Voltage Suppressors (TVS)** clamp the high-voltage defibrillator pulse. - **Current-limiting resistors** (typically **5 kΩ to 10 kΩ**) in series with each electrode limit the peak current into the op-amp input. - **Back-to-back Zener diodes** across the INA input clamp voltage to safe levels (e.g., ±6 V). **b) Over-Voltage / Over-Current Protection** - Fuses and PTC thermistors in the mains path. - Crowbar circuits at the power supply output. **c) ESD Protection** - TVS arrays at all exposed connector pins. **Defibrillator Protection Operating Sequence:** 1. Defibrillator pulse (up to **5000 V**, **360 J**) is applied to the patient. 2. TVS clamps the voltage at the input to **< 10 V**. 3. Current-limiting resistors restrict the current to **< 10 mA**. 4. After the pulse, the circuit automatically resets and resumes normal ECG recording within seconds. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The ECG architecture described above is the **canonical analog front-end (AFE)** pattern used universally in: - Diagnostic 12-lead ECG machines (Philips PageWriter, GE MAC series). - Patient monitors (IntelliVue, Mindray). - Holter recorders. - Wearable fitness / clinical ECG patches (Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile — uses dry electrodes but same AFE principles). Mastering this block diagram makes it trivial to understand **EEG, EMG, and EOG** front-ends — they are all variations of the same INA + filter + isolation pattern. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Gain This derivation is the single most important algebraic proof in Module 3 and is a guaranteed KTU question. ### Step 1 — Identify the Node Voltages Consider the 3-op-amp INA. The two input op-amps share a feedback resistor network. Let: - $R_1$ = feedback resistor of each input buffer. - $R_G$ = external gain-setting resistor connected between the two inverting inputs of the input buffers. - $R_2$ = input resistor of the subtractor stage. - $R_3$ = feedback resistor of the subtractor stage. Inputs: $V_1$ applied to non-inverting input of op-amp A, $V_2$ to non-inverting input of op-amp B. Because of the virtual short principle, the inverting inputs track: - Op-amp A inverting input: $V_1$ - Op-amp B inverting input: $V_2$ ### Step 2 — Current Through the Gain Resistor $R_G$ The same current $I$ flows through $R_G$ from one op-amp's inverting node to the other: $$I = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ ### Step 3 — Output Voltages of the Input Buffers Each input op-amp has feedback resistor $R_1$. Applying KCL at the inverting node of op-amp A: $$\dfrac{V_{O1} - V_1}{R_1} = I = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ Solving for $V_{O1}$: $$V_{O1} = V_1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$ By symmetry, for op-amp B: $$V_{O2} = V_2 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_G}(V_2 - V_1)$$ Subtracting: $$V_{O1} - V_{O2} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ This is the **first-stage gain**: $$A_{d,\text{stage1}} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$$ ### Step 4 — Subtractor Stage Output The third op-amp is a standard difference amplifier. Its output: $$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{O2} - V_{O1}) = -\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{O1} - V_{O2})$$ Substituting the stage-1 result: $$V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ ### Step 5 — Final Differential Gain $$\boxed{\,A_d = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = -\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\,}$$ The negative sign indicates phase inversion (typically re-inverted later by adding an extra unity-gain stage or by flipping the lead assignment). ### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example **Question:** Design an INA with $R_1 = 25\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega$. Find the overall differential gain. **Solution:** $$A_d = -\left(1 + \dfrac{2 \times 25\text{ k}}{2.5\text{ k}}\right)\dfrac{10\text{ k}}{10\text{ k}}$$ $$A_d = -\left(1 + 20\right)(1) = -21$$ So a 1 mV differential ECG input produces $V_{out} = -21\text{ mV}$. To reach a 1 V ADC scale, additional gain of $\approx 47$ is required downstream. ## 3.2 Derivation of DRL Common-Mode Cancellation Let the original common-mode voltage at the patient's body be $V_{cm,o}$ (caused by mains pickup, ground loops, etc.). The DRL op-amp senses $V_{cm,o}$, inverts it with gain $-A$ (where $A$ is the open-loop gain of the DRL auxiliary op-amp), and drives it back into the Right Leg electrode through a current-limiting resistor. The voltage appearing at the body after feedback: $$V_{cm,f} = V_{cm,o} - A \cdot V_{cm,f}$$ Solving: $$V_{cm,f}(1 + A) = V_{cm,o}$$ $$V_{cm,f} = \dfrac{V_{cm,o}}{1 + A}$$ For typical DRL op-amp open-loop gain $A = 10^4$ (80 dB), a 1 V common-mode pickup is reduced to $0.1\text{ mV}$ — a **10000-fold reduction**. ## 3.3 Derivation of RC Filter Cutoff For a first-order RC high-pass or low-pass filter: $$\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC} \quad (\text{HPF})$$ $$\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC} \quad (\text{LPF})$$ Setting $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and solving $\omega RC = 1$: $$\boxed{\,f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}\,}$$ **Numerical Example:** Choose $R = 100\text{ k}\Omega$ for the 0.05 Hz HPF: $$C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R f_c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 100{,}000 \times 0.05} \approx 31.8\text{ µF}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Comparison | Block | Function | Key Spec | Failure Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | **INA Pre-amp** | Amplify differential signal | CMRR ≥ 80 dB, Z_in ≥ 10 MΩ | Mains hum saturates output | | **HPF (0.05 Hz)** | Remove DC & baseline wander | f_c = 0.05 Hz | Drift off-screen, saturation | | **LPF (150 Hz)** | Band-limit to ECG | f_c = 150 Hz | EMG noise, aliasing | | **Notch (50 Hz)** | Suppress mains | Q ≈ 8–10 | 50 Hz interference visible | | **Isolation** | Patient safety | ≥ 5000 V, ≤ 10 µA leak | Electrical shock hazard | | **DRL** | Active CM cancellation | Loop gain ≥ 80 dB | Poor CMRR in hospital | | **Protection** | Defib/ESU survival | ±5000 V transient | Permanent AFE damage | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete ECG Front-End Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR bodyA[Patient Body] --> eA[Electrode LA] bodyB[Patient Body] --> eB[Electrode RA] bodyG[Patient Body] --> eC[Electrode RL] eA --> p1[Protection Block
TVS and 5k Resistor] eB --> p2[Protection Block
TVS and 5k Resistor] p1 --> inaA[INA Input Buffer A] p2 --> inaB[INA Input Buffer B] inaA --> sub[Subtractor Stage
3rd Op-Amp] inaB --> sub sub --> hpf[High Pass Filter
fc equals 0.05 Hz] hpf --> lpf[Low Pass Filter
fc equals 150 Hz] lpf --> notch[Notch Filter
50 Hz Twin T] notch --> iso[Isolation Barrier
Optocoupler or Transformer] iso --> out[Output Stage
and ADC] out --> display[Display and Recorder] sub --> cmSense[Common Mode Sense] cmSense --> drlAmp[DRL Inverting Amplifier] drlAmp --> drlRes[1 M Ohm Current Limit] drlRes --> eC ``` ## 4.2 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB v1[(V1 from LA)] --> nA[Node A plus input] v2[(V2 from RA)] --> nB[Node B plus input] nA --> opA[Op-Amp A] nB --> opB[Op-Amp B] opA -- R1 feedback --> rg[R_G Gain Resistor] rg --> opB opA -- output VO1 --> subIn[Subtractor Inputs] opB -- output VO2 --> subIn subIn --> opC[Subtractor Op-Amp] opC --> vout[(Vout Differential to Single Ended)] ``` ## 4.3 Driven Right-Leg Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR cm[(Common Mode Vcm at Body)] --> avgRes[Resistor Averaging Network] avgRes --> drlOp[Inverting DRL Op-Amp] drlOp --> limRes[1 M Ohm Current Limit] limRes --> rlElectrode[Right Leg Electrode] rlElectrode --> cm ``` ## 4.4 Defibrillator Protection Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD pulse[Defib Pulse 5000 V] --> gdt[Gas Discharge Tube Fires] gdt --> tvs[TVS Clamps to 10 V] tvs --> seriesR[Series 5k Resistor Limits Current] seriesR --> zener[Back to Back Zener 6V2] zener --> ina[INA Input Protected] ina --> reset[Auto Reset within 5 seconds] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of an ECG pre-amplifier. Why is high CMRR essential?* **Model Answer:** CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$ It is essential because the ECG signal (~1 mV) is superimposed on a large common-mode 50 Hz mains voltage (often several volts). A high CMRR (≥ 80 dB) ensures that this common-mode interference is rejected and does not saturate the amplifier, allowing the tiny differential ECG waveform to be faithfully amplified. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** *List any three functions of isolation circuits in an ECG machine.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Patient Protection:** Prevents hazardous leakage current (>10 µA) from reaching the patient through the recording electrodes, complying with IEC 60601-1. 2. **Signal Integrity:** Breaks ground loops between the patient and the recording equipment, eliminating 50 Hz mains interference. 3. **Equipment Protection:** Protects the mains-powered circuitry from high-voltage transients such as defibrillator pulses applied during patient resuscitation. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the function of each block of an ECG machine. *Special emphasis on Pre-amplifier, Filters, and Protection circuits.* **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the differential gain of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier used as the ECG pre-amplifier. *Include a numerical example.* --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### (a) Block Diagram and Explanation *(Refer to SECTION_4.1 for the complete block diagram.)* **1. Electrodes (LA, RA, LL, RL):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes form the skin–electrolyte interface. The half-cell potential (~**+0.22 V** for Ag/AgCl) introduces a large DC offset that must be rejected by AC-coupled stages downstream. **2. Protection Circuit:** Comprises gas discharge tubes, TVS diodes, and series current-limiting resistors (**5 kΩ**). It clamps defibrillator pulses (up to **5000 V**) to safe levels (< **10 V**) and limits current to < **10 mA**, allowing the ECG machine to survive repeated defibrillation. **3. Pre-amplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier):** Provides differential gain of **~1000**, CMRR ≥ **80 dB**, and input impedance ≥ **10 MΩ**. The 3-op-amp topology is used because it offers single-resistor gain control and balanced high input impedance on both inputs. **4. High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz):** Removes baseline wander caused by respiration and electrode polarization. Implemented as a first-order RC filter with $f_c = 0.05$ Hz. **5. Low-Pass Filter (150 Hz):** Limits the upper bandwidth to ECG frequencies, suppressing EMG, RF, and thermal noise. $f_c = 150$ Hz. **6. Notch Filter (50 Hz):** A Twin-T or Wien-bridge topology rejects the dominant Indian mains interference without attenuating ECG harmonics. $f_n = 50$ Hz, $Q \approx 8$–$10$. **7. Isolation Barrier:** Optical or transformer isolation rated at ≥ **5000 V**. Prevents leakage current > **10 µA** from reaching the patient. **8. Output Stage / ADC / Display:** Final gain stage (~10×), 12- or 16-bit ADC sampling at ≥ **500 Hz**, and LCD/thermal printer display. #### (b) Differential Gain Derivation *(Refer to SECTION_3.1 for the complete derivation.)* Final result: $$A_d = -\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$$ **Numerical Example:** With $R_1 = 25\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega$: $$A_d = -(1 + 20)(1) = -21$$ A 1 mV differential ECG signal produces an output of $V_{out} = -21\text{ mV}$, which is then amplified further downstream to ~1 V before digitization. --- ### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the **Driven Right-Leg (DRL)** circuit in an ECG machine. Derive the expression for the effective common-mode voltage after DRL action. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain **defibrillator protection** in ECG machines. Why is the protection circuit placed *before* the pre-amplifier? --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### (a) Driven Right-Leg Circuit *(Refer to SECTION_2.4 for theory and SECTION_4.3 for the diagram.)* **Working:** The two ECG electrode signals ($V_1$ and $V_2$) are averaged through equal resistors $R$ to extract the common-mode voltage $V_{cm,o}$. This voltage is inverted and amplified by an auxiliary op-amp (open-loop gain $A$) and fed back through a current-limiting resistor (**1 MΩ**) to the third electrode on the patient's Right Leg (RL). **Effect:** The body is driven toward the amplifier reference, making $V_{cm} \approx 0$. **Derivation:** $$V_{cm,f} = \dfrac{V_{cm,o}}{1 + A}$$ With $A = 10^4$, a 1 V common-mode voltage is reduced to $0.1\text{ mV}$ — a **10 000-fold improvement** in effective CMRR. #### (b) Defibrillator Protection *(Refer to SECTION_4.4 for the diagram.)* **Circuit:** A **gas discharge tube (GDT)** is connected across the electrode inputs. A **bidirectional TVS diode** clamps transients to ~**±10 V**. **Back-to-back Zener diodes** provide secondary clamping. **5 kΩ series resistors** limit current to < **10 mA**. **Placement Justification:** The protection block is placed *before* the pre-amplifier so that: 1. The sensitive (and expensive) op-amps in the INA never see voltages beyond their absolute maximum ratings (±**6 V** to ±**18 V**). 2. The protection components (TVS, GDT) can absorb the bulk of the **360 J** defibrillator pulse without depending on the fragile INA inputs. 3. Once the pulse ends, the protection components recover automatically, allowing ECG recording to resume within ~**5 seconds** — critical for post-resuscitation monitoring. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > - Students commonly confuse **CMRR with S/N ratio** — they are *different* parameters. > - Many students place the **isolation barrier between the patient and the electrodes** — this is wrong. The barrier is *after* the front-end signal processing, between the analog and digital/mains sections. > - **Do not forget the current-limiting resistor (1 MΩ)** in the DRL — without it, the circuit violates patient safety standards. > - In INA gain derivations, **sign convention errors** are common: remember $A_d$ is *negative* in the standard 3-op-amp topology unless an additional inverter is added. --- ## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - **ECG signal**: ~1 mV, bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz, riding on a large common-mode voltage. - **Pre-amp must be an Instrumentation Amplifier** for high CMRR (≥ 80 dB), high $Z_{in}$ (≥ 10 MΩ), and adjustable gain. - **INA gain formula**: $A_d = \left(1 + 2R_1/R_G\right)\left(R_3/R_2\right)$. - **Three filters in cascade**: HPF (0.05 Hz) → LPF (150 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz, India). - **Filter cutoff**: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$. - **Isolation**: ≥ 5000 V, leakage current ≤ 10 µA (IEC 60601-1). Either transformer or optocoupler. - **DRL circuit**: Senses common-mode voltage, inverts and drives it back to Right Leg, reducing $V_{cm}$ by $(1+A)$. - **Protection**: GDT + TVS + Zener + 5 kΩ current-limiting resistor; must survive **5000 V**, **360 J** defibrillator pulse. - **DRL current-limit resistor = 1 MΩ**; protection series resistor = 5 kΩ — remember both! - **Order of blocks is non-negotiable**: Electrodes → Protection → Pre-amp → Filters → Isolation → Output. - **CMRR equation**: $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$. - **Notch filter Q-factor**: ~8–10 for sharp 50 Hz rejection without ringing. - **Why not a simple differential amplifier?** Because its input impedances are unbalanced, destroying CMRR through electrode impedances. - **KTU 2024 weightage**: This topic typically contributes **14 marks** in ESE (Part B) and **3 marks** in Part A.

topics.title: Analog Front End of ECG Machine (Block Diagrams Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analog Front End of ECG Machine (Block Diagrams Only) # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) The **Analog Front End (AFE)** of an Electrocardiograph (ECG) is the complete analog signal-conditioning subsystem that interfaces the patient’s skin-electrode interface to the digital acquisition and display stage. It is responsible for acquiring the bioelectric potential generated by cardiac depolarization/repolarization (≈ **0.5 mV to 5 mV** peak, frequency content **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**), amplifying it by a factor of roughly **1000×**, rejecting common-mode interference (50/60 Hz mains hum, electrode DC offset), providing patient isolation against microshock hazards, and presenting a clean, band-limited signal to the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):** *The Analog Front End of an ECG machine comprises the electrode, lead network, protection circuitry, instrumentation amplifier, filters, and isolation stage arranged in cascade to produce a clean, isolated, amplified ECG signal suitable for digitization.* ## 1.2 Block-Level Constituents (Canonical 8-Stage Cascade) An ECG AFE is conventionally drawn as **eight cascading functional blocks** (the order is non-negotiable in a KTU answer): 1. **Surface Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** – ionic-to-electronic transduction 2. **Patient Cable & Lead Selector** – 5-lead / 10-wire / 12-lead routing 3. **Defibrillation & Over-Voltage Protection** – gas-discharge tubes, Zener clamps, resistors 4. **ESD & RF Suppression Network** – ferrite beads, TVS diodes, common-mode chokes 5. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** – first-stage gain ≈ 10, high CMRR (≥ **100 dB**) 6. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) / Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit** – actively forces the patient’s body to a clean common-mode potential 7. **Analog Filter Cascade** – 1st-order HPF (0.05 Hz), LPF (150 Hz), 50 Hz Notch 8. **Isolation Amplifier / Optocoupler** – galvanic isolation barrier (≥ 4000 V isolation, IEC 60601-1) > [!NOTE] > **Why this exact order?** Protection stages *must* precede the amplifier (to save the expensive INA from defibrillator pulses up to 5 kV). The INA *must* precede filtering (to amplify first, then filter, maximizing SNR). Isolation *must* be the *last* analog stage before ADC (to prevent mains earth loops reaching the patient). ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — “The Hospital Whisperer” Imagine you are trying to hear a **soft whisper** (the heart’s electrical signal, ~1 mV) across a **noisy stadium** (the human body, full of 50 Hz mains hum, muscle EMG, and motion artefacts). The Analog Front End is your custom-built **noise-cancelling microphone rig**: - The **electrode** is the microphone capsule, pressed against the body’s “lips”. - The **cable + protection** is a shielded, surge-proof cable that survives a lightning strike (defibrillator). - The **instrumentation amplifier** is the studio-grade pre-amp that hears *only* the whisper and rejects the stadium roar (common-mode noise). - The **DRL circuit** is a noise-cancellation headphone mic that *measures* the crowd noise and *plays it back inverted* into the patient’s right leg, electronically silencing the body. - The **filter cascade** is the equalizer that removes low-frequency rumbles (baseline wander) and high-frequency hisses (EMG), leaving only the clean whisper. - The **isolation barrier** is a one-way glass wall — the whisper passes, but no dangerous electrical current can flow back to the patient. > [!TIP] > For a KTU 2-mark definition: remember the triplet **“Amplify – Filter – Isolate”** — every ECG AFE boils down to this core trinity. ## 1.4 Governing Standards (Must Cite in Answers) | Standard | Purpose | Key Threshold | |----------|---------|---------------| | **IEC 60601-1** | General safety, patient isolation | Patient leakage current < **10 µA** (CF type) | | **IEC 60601-2-25** | Particular requirements for ECG | Diagnostic frequency band **0.05 – 150 Hz** | | **AAMI EC11** | Diagnostic ECG performance | CMRR ≥ **100 dB** at 50/60 Hz | | **AHA Recommendations** | Electrode placement (Mason-Likar) | 10-electrode, 12-lead standard | --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Block-by-Block Engineering Rationale ### Block 1 — Surface Electrodes (Ag/AgCl) - **Function:** Convert ionic current in body tissue to electronic current in the wire via a **half-cell potential** of ≈ **+0.223 V** (Ag/AgCl reference). - **Why Ag/AgCl?** Non-polarizable — current passes without net chemical change, minimizing **DC offset drift** (motion artefact). - **Key parameter:** Electrode-skin impedance $Z_e \approx 5 \text{ k}\Omega$ at **10 Hz** (fresh gel) — degrades with time and drying. ### Block 2 — Patient Cable & Lead Selector - **Function:** Routes the chosen electrode pair (e.g., Lead II = LA–RA) to the amplifier input. Implemented using a **multiplexer (MUX)** controlled by the microcontroller. - **Three standard lead systems:** **Bipolar (Einthoven)**, **Augmented unipolar (Goldberger – aVR, aVL, aVF)**, **Precordial unipolar (Wilson – V1…V6)**. ### Block 3 — Defibrillation Protection - **Function:** Survive a **5 kV, 360 J** defibrillator discharge without damage. - **Topology:** A **series resistor** (typically 1–10 kΩ, high-energy rated) in each lead, followed by a **clamping device** — either a **gas-discharge tube (GDT)** or a **Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS)** Zener diode. - **Resistor value trade-off:** Higher R = better current limiting, but **larger thermal noise** (Johnson noise: $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$). ### Block 4 — ESD & RF Suppression - **Function:** Block ElectroStatic Discharge and Radio-Frequency interference from electrosurgical units (ESU/Cautery, 300 kHz – 1 MHz). - **Components:** Ferrite beads (impedance $\geq 100 \Omega$ at 100 MHz), common-mode chokes, **Y-rated capacitors** (line-to-earth, 2.2 nF, Y1 rated ≥ 250 V). ### Block 5 — Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — *The Heart of the AFE* - **Function:** Differential amplification with extremely high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**. - **Topology:** Classic **three-op-amp INA** (e.g., AD620, INA128, INA333). - **Gain equation:** $$G_{INA} = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}$$ where $R_G$ is the external gain-setting resistor. - **Typical gain in ECG:** $G_1 \approx 10$ (subsequent stages add the remaining ×100). - **Why three op-amps and not one?** The two-input buffer op-amps provide **symmetric high input impedance** ($> 10^{10} \Omega$) — essential because electrode impedance is unbalanced and source impedance mismatches would otherwise destroy CMRR. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CMRR – Board Favourite:** Defined as $$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}} \right)$$ where $A_{DM}$ = differential-mode gain, $A_{CM}$ = common-mode gain. A 1% mismatch in source impedances can drop CMRR by **40 dB** — hence the **DRL circuit** (Block 6) is non-optional. ### Block 6 — Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit - **Function:** Replaces the classical **Right Leg Ground Resistor (RLGR)** with an *active* negative-feedback loop that drives the patient’s body potential to the instrument’s common-mode reference. - **Working principle:** Senses the common-mode voltage (sum of both INA inputs), **inverts** and **amplifies** it (gain ≈ 100), and **drives it back** into the right-leg electrode. - **Effective CMRR boost:** From ≈ 60 dB (passive) to **> 100 dB** (active DRL). - **Safety resistor:** Always in series with the DRL output (typically **1 MΩ**) to limit current to a safe value in case of fault. ### Block 7 — Analog Filter Cascade - **Order is critical:** **HPF → Notch → LPF** (NOT the reverse — if you place the LPF first, you attenuate the differential signal you just amplified). | Filter | Cut-off | Purpose | Typical Topology | |--------|---------|---------|------------------| | **High-Pass (HPF)** | $f_c = 0.05 \text{ Hz}$ (diagnostic) / 0.5 Hz (monitor) | Block DC electrode offset & baseline wander | 1st-order RC, $f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | | **Notch (Band-Stop)** | 50 Hz / 60 Hz | Reject mains interference | Twin-T or active Fliege topology, $Q \geq 10$ | | **Low-Pass (LPF)** | $f_c = 150 \text{ Hz}$ (diagnostic) / 40 Hz (monitor) | Block EMG & high-freq noise | 2nd-order Butterworth (maximally flat) | ### Block 8 — Isolation Amplifier - **Function:** Provides **galvanic isolation** (no DC path) between the patient-connected analog side and the mains-connected digital/computer side. - **Methods:** **Optical** (IL300, ISO124), **Capacitive** (ISO121, ADuM3150), **Transformer** (ADuM6401). - **Isolation voltage:** ≥ **4000 V RMS** (medical-grade), creepage distance ≥ 8 mm. - **Why mandatory?** A grounded laptop connected to a non-isolated ECG creates a **ground loop**, allowing 50 Hz current to flow through the patient’s heart (microshock → ventricular fibrillation at 10 µA directly applied to myocardium). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Value / Unit | |---|-------------------|---------------|----------------------| | 1 | Instrumentation Amplifier Gain | $G_{INA} = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}$ | 5 to 1000 (dimensionless) | | 2 | CMRR (dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}}\right)$ | ≥ 100 dB @ 50/60 Hz | | 3 | Common-Mode Rejection due to $\Delta Z$ | $\text{CMRR}_{\Delta Z} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{DM}}{1 + \Delta Z / Z_e}\right)$ | dB | | 4 | HPF Cut-off | $f_{c,HPF} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_1}$ | 0.05 Hz | | 5 | LPF Cut-off | $f_{c,LPF} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_2 C_2}$ | 150 Hz | | 6 | Notch Frequency (Twin-T) | $f_{notch} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | 50 Hz (India/Europe), 60 Hz (US) | | 7 | Total Input-Referred Noise (Johnson) | $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | $\mu$V (with $\Delta f = 150$ Hz) | | 8 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | ≥ 40 dB clinical | | 9 | ECG Peak Amplitude | $V_{peak}$ | 0.5 mV – 5 mV | | 10 | Overall AFE Gain | $G_{total} = G_{INA} \times G_{2nd\,stage}$ | ≈ 1000 (to drive ±5 V ADC) | | 11 | Defibrillator Discharge Energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | 360 J (clinical) | | 12 | Patient Leakage Current (CF) | $I_{leak} < 10 \mu A$ (cardiac floating) | IEC 60601-1 | | 13 | Skin-Electrode Impedance | $Z_e$ | 1 – 50 kΩ | | 14 | Input Bias Current (FET INA) | $I_B$ | < 1 nA | | 15 | Slew Rate (LPF op-amp) | $SR > 2 \pi f_{max} V_{peak}$ | > 5 V/µs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Pitfall:** In every formula, **state the assumption** (e.g., “balanced source impedances”, “room temperature 300 K”). Examiners award the ½ mark for context. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Holter monitors** (24-hr ambulatory ECG) use the same AFE but with **stricter power budget** (battery) → op-amps chosen for < 1 mA quiescent current (e.g., AD8232 — a complete AFE on a chip). - **Implantable loop recorders** shrink the AFE into a **single ASIC** consuming < 30 µW. - **12-lead clinical cart ECG** uses a **multi-channel AFE** (one INA per lead, 8 channels in parallel). - **Single-chip AFEs** (AD8232, MAX30003) integrate Blocks 3–8 into a $4 \times 4$ mm QFN — modern industry standard. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Three-Op-Amp INA Differential Gain **Problem:** Derive the closed-form gain of the classic three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. **Circuit description:** - Two input buffers (A1, A2) with gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. - Feedback resistors $R_1$ on each buffer. - A difference amplifier (A3) with resistors $R_2$ (input) and $R_3$ (feedback/gain). - For symmetry: $R_2 = R_3$. **Step 1:** Apply KCL at the inverting node of A1 (call it node X). $$ \frac{V_1 - V_X}{R_1} = \frac{V_X - V_O2}{R_G} + \frac{V_X - V_X}{R_G} $$ **Step 2:** Because A1 is in negative feedback (output connected through $R_G$ to the *other* op-amp’s inverting input), the two inverting inputs are at the same potential (virtual short). $$ V_X = V_Y $$ **Step 3:** The voltage across $R_G$ is $V_X - V_Y$ (a virtual short implies $V_{Rg} = 0$? No — actually $R_G$ sees the *difference* between the two outputs scaled by ½). Redo using superposition on the standard analysis. **Cleaner textbook derivation:** The current through $R_G$ is forced by the two input buffers. Since the inverting nodes are at the same potential ($V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively, due to virtual short of the buffers): $$ I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G} $$ **Step 4:** This same current flows through both $R_1$ resistors (no current enters op-amp inputs): $$ I_{Rg} = I_{R_1,left} = I_{R_1,right} $$ **Step 5:** Output of A1 is: $$ V_{O1} = V_1 + I_{Rg} \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1 (V_1 - V_2)}{R_G} $$ **Step 6:** Output of A2 is: $$ V_{O2} = V_2 - I_{Rg} \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1 (V_1 - V_2)}{R_G} $$ **Step 7:** Difference between the two: $$ V_{O1} - V_{O2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2 R_1 (V_1 - V_2)}{R_G} = (V_1 - V_2) \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right) $$ **Step 8:** The third stage (A3) is a unity-gain difference amplifier (assuming $R_2 = R_3$): $$ V_{OUT} = V_{O2} - V_{O1} = (V_2 - V_1)\left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right) $$ **Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{G_{INA} = \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_2 - V_1} = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** The gain depends *only* on $R_G$ — a single external resistor. This is the design elegance that made the three-op-amp INA the universal ECG front-end building block since 1968 (Sandman, then popularized by Analog Devices). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR Degradation Due to Source Impedance Mismatch **Problem:** A patient has electrode impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ on the two arms, with mismatch $\Delta Z = Z_1 - Z_2$. The INA has differential gain $A_{DM}$ and finite common-mode gain $A_{CM}$. Show that the *effective* CMRR is degraded. **Step 1:** The differential input voltage seen by the INA is: $$ V_{in,DM} = V_{signal} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_e + Z_{in}} \approx V_{signal} \quad (\text{if } Z_{in} \gg Z_e) $$ **Step 2:** The common-mode voltage is converted into a differential error by the *unbalanced* voltage divider. The fraction of common-mode voltage that *appears* as a differential error is: $$ \Delta V_{err} = V_{CM} \cdot \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e + \Delta Z} \approx V_{CM} \cdot \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e} $$ **Step 3:** Total output: $$ V_{OUT} = A_{DM} \cdot V_{signal} + A_{CM} \cdot V_{CM} + A_{DM} \cdot V_{CM} \cdot \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e} $$ **Step 4:** Effective common-mode gain (including mismatch term): $$ A_{CM,eff} = A_{CM} + A_{DM} \cdot \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e} $$ **Step 5:** Effective CMRR: $$ \text{CMRR}_{eff} = \frac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM,eff}} \approx \frac{1}{\frac{A_{CM}}{A_{DM}} + \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{INA}} + \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e}} $$ **Numerical example:** With INA CMRR = **100 dB** ($\frac{A_{CM}}{A_{DM}} = 10^{-5}$), $Z_e = 5$ kΩ, $\Delta Z = 1$ kΩ (20% mismatch from a dried electrode): $$ \frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e} = \frac{1000}{10000} = 0.1 $$ $$ \text{CMRR}_{eff} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{0.00001 + 0.1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(9.999) \approx 20 \text{ dB} $$ **Result:** The effective CMRR collapses from **100 dB → 20 dB** — the 50 Hz mains hum is no longer rejected. > [!IMPORTANT] > **This derivation is the strongest justification for the DRL circuit.** DRL actively forces the common-mode voltage to zero, eliminating the $\frac{\Delta Z}{2 Z_e}$ term entirely. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — First-Order RC High-Pass Filter Transfer Function **Problem:** A 1st-order RC HPF is used to remove DC offset. Derive the magnitude response and choose R, C for $f_c = 0.05$ Hz. **Step 1:** Impedance divider (output taken across R): $$ H(s) = \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $s = j\omega$, $f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$: $$ H(j\omega) = \frac{j(f/f_c)}{1 + j(f/f_c)} $$ **Step 3:** Magnitude: $$ \vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{f/f_c}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} $$ **Step 4:** For $f \gg f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to 1$ (passes ECG). For $f \ll f_c$: $\vert H \vert \to f/f_c$ (attenuates DC). At $f = f_c$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = -3 \text{ dB}$ (definition of cut-off). **Step 5:** Component selection. Choose $C = 1 \mu F$: $$ R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.18 \text{ M}\Omega $$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical caveat:** Such a high R is a source of leakage current and op-amp input bias errors. Modern ECGs use **active 2nd-order HPFs** (Sallen-Key) with smaller R, C values, achieving the same $f_c$ with better noise performance. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Block Failure Modes | Block | Typical Failure | Symptom in ECG | Mitigation | |-------|-----------------|----------------|------------| | Electrode | Gel dries, polarization | Baseline drift, low amplitude | Replace gel, use Ag/AgCl | | Cable | Broken shield, loose snap | 50 Hz pickup, intermittent | Replace cable | | Protection | Zener short | No signal / saturation | Replace TVS, check R | | INA | Saturation from DC offset | Clipped QRS | Increase HPF cut-off | | DRL | Disconnected | Massive 50 Hz hum | Check right-leg electrode | | HPF | Wrong $f_c$ | Baseline wander | Re-tune R, C | | Notch | Mis-tuned | Residual 50/60 Hz | Use precision components | | LPF | Slew-rate limit | Distorted QRS | Use faster op-amp | | Isolation | Barrier breakdown | Patient shock hazard | Mandatory periodic Hi-Pot test | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Complete ECG Analog Front End ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Body
Heart Dipole Source] --> B[Surface Electrodes
AgAgCl x10] B --> C[Patient Cable
5-Lead 10-Wire] C --> D[Defibrillation Protection
GDT + TVS + 10k R] D --> E[ESD and RF Suppression
Ferrite Bead + Y Cap] E --> F[Lead Selector MUX
12-Lead Routing] F --> G[Instrumentation Amplifier
INA Gain 10] G --> H[Driven Right Leg Loop
Summing Amp Inverting] H --> I[2nd Stage Amplifier
Gain 100] I --> J[High Pass Filter
fc 0.05 Hz] J --> K[Notch Filter 50Hz
Twin-T Topology] K --> L[Low Pass Filter
fc 150 Hz] L --> M[Isolation Amplifier
ISO124 Optical 4kV] M --> N[ADC and DSP
16-bit 1 kSPS] N --> O[Display and Storage
LCD or PC] H -. Right Leg Electrode .-> A ``` ## 4.2 Instrumentation Amplifier Sub-Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB V1[Input V1
LA] --> A1[Op-Amp A1 Buffer] V2[Input V2
RA] --> A2[Op-Amp A2 Buffer] RG[Gain Resistor RG] --- A1 RG --- A2 A1 --> R1a[R1] A2 --> R1b[R1] A1 --> DA[Op-Amp A3 Difference Amp] A2 --> DA R1a --> DA R1b --> DA DA --> OUT[Differential Output Vout] DA --> RL[R3 Feedback] DA --> R2[R2 to Ground] ``` ## 4.3 Driven Right Leg (DRL) Sub-Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[INA Input 1 V1] --> SUM[Summing Junction
Resistor Network] N2[INA Input 2 V2] --> SUM SUM --> INV[Inverting Amplifier
Gain 100] INV --> RSAFE[1 MOhm Safety Resistor] RSAFE --> RLE[Right Leg Electrode
on Patient] RLE -. Common Mode Feedback .-> N1 RLE -. Common Mode Feedback .-> N2 ``` ## 4.4 Filter Cascade Signal-Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Post INA Signal
+/- 50 mV] --> H[1st Order HPF
fc 0.05 Hz] H --> N2[2nd Order Notch
50 Hz Q 10] N2 --> L[2nd Order Butterworth LPF
fc 150 Hz] L --> ISO[Isolation Barrier
4 kV RMS] ISO --> ADC[16-bit ADC
+/- 5 V FS] H -. DC Block .-> N2 N2 -. Mains Reject .-> L L -. Anti-Alias .-> ISO ``` ## 4.5 Defibrillation Protection Network (Schematic-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Electrode LA] --> R1[Series Resistor 10 kOhm 1W] R1 --> C1[Y-Cap 2.2 nF Y1 to Earth] R1 --> TVS1[TVS Zener Bidirectional 5V] R1 --> GDT[Gas Discharge Tube 90V] TVS1 --> GND1[Analog Ground] GDT --> GND1 R1 --> INA[To INA Input] ``` > [!NOTE] > **For the KTU 14-mark question:** Always redraw the *master block diagram* (4.1) and label each block with its function and a representative component value. This single diagram is worth **4 – 5 marks** in the board exam. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define the term “Analog Front End” of an ECG machine. List its four main functional blocks.** **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** The Analog Front End (AFE) of an ECG machine is the analog signal-conditioning cascade that connects the patient electrodes to the analog-to-digital converter. Its four main functional blocks are: (i) **Protection circuitry** (defibrillation, ESD), (ii) **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** for high-CMRR differential gain, (iii) **Filter cascade** (HPF, Notch, LPF) for bandwidth limiting, and (iv) **Isolation amplifier** for patient safety. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for blocks.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is the role of the Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit in an ECG machine? Why is it preferred over a simple right-leg ground resistor?** **Model Answer (≈ 100 words):** The Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit actively drives the patient’s body to the instrument’s common-mode potential via a negative-feedback loop, thereby boosting the system’s effective CMRR from ≈ 60 dB (passive) to **> 100 dB**. It is preferred over a passive right-leg ground resistor because the resistor only *loads* the common-mode voltage; it does not *reject* it. A passive resistor also cannot limit 50 Hz loop currents safely. The DRL provides a **low-impedance drive** with a **1 MΩ series safety resistor**, both rejecting interference and limiting fault current. *(3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — *AFE Blocks, INA & Filters* **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the complete Analog Front End of a 12-lead ECG machine. Label each block with its function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **closed-form gain equation** of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Why is this topology preferred over a single-differential amplifier for ECG applications? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) A 12-lead ECG AFE consists of the following cascaded blocks (board exam — draw the diagram, then explain): 1. **Surface Electrodes (10 nos.)** — Ag/AgCl, placed on limbs + 6 precordial positions (V1–V6). 2. **Patient Cable** — 10-wire shielded cable with snap connectors. 3. **Defibrillation Protection** — 10 kΩ series resistor + bidirectional TVS (5 V clamp) per lead. 4. **ESD Suppression** — Ferrite bead + Y-rated 2.2 nF capacitor to earth. 5. **Lead Selector MUX** — 8-channel analog multiplexer (e.g., CD74HC4051) routing chosen lead pair to INA. 6. **Instrumentation Amplifier** — AD620 / INA128, gain $G_1 = 10$. 7. **Driven Right Leg (DRL) Circuit** — Inverting summer with gain 100, output via 1 MΩ to right-leg electrode. 8. **2nd-Stage Amplifier** — Non-inverting op-amp, gain $G_2 = 100$ (overall gain = 1000). 9. **High-Pass Filter** — 1st-order RC, $f_c = 0.05$ Hz. 10. **Notch Filter** — Twin-T, $f_{notch} = 50$ Hz, Q = 10. 11. **Low-Pass Filter** — 2nd-order Butterworth Sallen-Key, $f_c = 150$ Hz. 12. **Isolation Amplifier** — ISO124 (optical), 4 kV RMS isolation. 13. **ADC** — 16-bit, 1 kSPS, ±5 V FS. *(Full marks: neat diagram = 3, block labels = 2, function explanation = 2.)* --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **Three-Op-Amp INA Gain Derivation:** Consider op-amps A1, A2 (input buffers) and A3 (difference amplifier). Let $R_G$ be the gain-setting resistor and $R_1$ be the feedback resistors of A1, A2. Let $R_2$, $R_3$ be the input/feedback resistors of A3 (with $R_2 = R_3$ for unity-gain difference). Current through $R_G$: $$ I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G} $$ (virtual short: inverting nodes of A1 and A2 are at $V_1$ and $V_2$).
