By KCL at A1 output: $$ V_{O1} = V_1 + I_{Rg} R_1 $$ By KCL at A2 output: $$ V_{O2} = V_2 - I_{Rg} R_1 $$ Difference amplifier A3 output: $$ V_{OUT} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right) (V_2 - V_1) $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{G_{INA} = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}} $$ **Why preferred over single-differential amplifier for ECG?** 1. **Very high input impedance** ($> 10^{10} \Omega$) on both inputs — does not load the high-impedance electrode source. 2. **Single-resistor gain setting** — only $R_G$ needs to change to vary gain. 3. **Symmetric input structure** — both inputs see identical buffering, preserving high CMRR even with mismatched source impedances. 4. **Low output impedance** — can drive filters/ADC without loading. 5. **Single-chip availability** (e.g., AD620) — compact, low-noise, low-offset. *(Full marks: 4 for derivation + 3 for comparison.)* --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *DRL, CMRR & Protection* **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the working of the **Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** with a block diagram. Show mathematically how it improves the effective CMRR. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **defibrillation protection circuit** used in the AFE of an ECG machine. Why is a high-value series resistor used, and what is its trade-off? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) **DRL Working Principle:** The DRL circuit samples the common-mode voltage of the patient (sum of the two INA input signals) using a summing network of three equal resistors. The summed common-mode signal is fed to an inverting amplifier (gain ≈ 100). The output of this inverting amplifier is connected back to the patient’s right-leg electrode through a **1 MΩ safety resistor**. This forms a **negative feedback loop**: if the body potential drifts due to 50 Hz mains coupling, the DRL drives an *opposite* current into the body, forcing the common-mode voltage toward zero. **Mathematical CMRR Improvement:** The common-mode voltage on the body is $V_{CM,body}$. The DRL drives the right leg with $V_{RL} = -A_{DRL} \cdot V_{CM,body}$. The effective common-mode voltage seen by the INA becomes: $$ V_{CM,eff} = V_{CM,body} + V_{RL} = V_{CM,body} (1 - A_{DRL}) $$ The INA’s output common-mode component: $$ V_{OUT,CM} = A_{CM,INA} \cdot V_{CM,eff} = A_{CM,INA} \cdot V_{CM,body} (1 - A_{DRL}) $$ Effective common-mode gain of the *system*: $$ A_{CM,system} = \frac{A_{CM,INA}}{1 + A_{DRL}} $$ Effective CMRR: $$ \text{CMRR}_{system} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{DM} (1 + A_{DRL})}{A_{CM,INA}}\right) = \text{CMRR}_{INA} + 20 \log_{10}(1 + A_{DRL}) $$ For $A_{DRL} = 100$, the CMRR improves by $20 \log_{10}(101) \approx 40$ dB. If the INA’s intrinsic CMRR is 80 dB, the system CMRR becomes **120 dB**, far exceeding the AAMI minimum of 100 dB. *(Full marks: 3 for block diagram + 2 for working + 2 for math.)* --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) **Defibrillation Protection Circuit:** A defibrillator applies a **5 kV, 360 J** pulse to the patient during emergency cardioversion. This pulse, if it reaches the INA, will destroy the input transistors. The protection circuit is a **three-element network** in each ECG lead: 1. **Series resistor** (1 – 10 kΩ, 1 W rated, flame-proof): limits the peak current entering the AFE. 2. **Bidirectional TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) Zener diode** (5 V clamp): clamps any voltage above ±5 V to a safe value. 3. **Gas Discharge Tube (GDT)** (90 V breakdown): crowbars voltages above 90 V (the bulk of the defibrillator pulse) to ground, protecting the TVS from thermal overload. A typical sequence during a defibrillator discharge: - Pulse enters the lead → GDT fires at 90 V → most energy diverted to chassis ground. - Residual pulse attenuated by series resistor → clamped by TVS to ±5 V → safe for INA. **Why a high-value series resistor?** The series resistor limits the *fault current* reaching the AFE. The peak current from a 5 kV source through a 10 kΩ resistor is: $$ I_{peak} = \frac{V_{peak}}{R_{series}} = \frac{5000}{10{,}000} = 0.5 \text{ A} $$ Compared to a 50 Ω source, this is a **200× reduction**, sufficient to keep the TVS within its thermal rating. **The Trade-Off — Johnson Noise:** Every resistor generates thermal (Johnson) noise: $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} $$ For $R = 10$ kΩ, $T = 300$ K, $\Delta f = 150$ Hz: $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10{,}000 \times 150} \approx 1.58 \, \mu V_{RMS} $$ This noise appears in series with the ECG signal (1 mV typical), giving an SNR of: $$ \text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1 \text{ mV}}{1.58 \mu V}\right) \approx 56 \text{ dB} $$ If $R$ were increased to 100 kΩ, noise would increase by $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16×$, but protection would also improve. A **10 kΩ compromise** is the industry standard. *(Full marks: 3 for circuit description + 2 for current-limiting calculation + 2 for trade-off with Johnson noise.)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in the AFE topic:** > > 1. **Drawing the block diagram in the wrong order** (e.g., LPF before HPF, or isolation before filters) — **−1 mark**. > 2. **Forgetting to label the DRL block** or drawing the patient connection loop — examiners specifically look for the *right-leg feedback wire* returning to the body. **−1 mark**. > 3. **Stating CMRR in dB but forgetting the formula** — board expects $20 \log_{10}(A_{DM}/A_{CM})$ explicitly written. **−0.5 mark**. > 4. **Confusing ECG amplitude ranges** — saying “5 V” instead of “5 mV” for QRS peak. **−0.5 mark**. > 5. **In filter derivations, omitting the substitution $s = j\omega$** — required for magnitude response. **−1 mark**. > 6. **Writing “isolation is for noise reduction”** — it is for **patient safety** (galvanic isolation from mains earth), not noise. **−0.5 mark**. > 7. **Not mentioning safety resistor (1 MΩ)** in the DRL output — IEC 60601-1 mandate. **−0.5 mark**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ The AFE of an ECG has **8 canonical blocks** — Protection, ESD Suppression, MUX, INA, DRL, Amplifier, Filters, Isolation. - ✅ Input ECG amplitude: **0.5 mV – 5 mV**; bandwidth: **0.05 – 150 Hz**; overall gain: **≈ 1000**. - ✅ Three-op-amp INA gain: $G = 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}$. - ✅ AAMI EC11 requires **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz. - ✅ DRL improves CMRR by **40 dB** for $A_{DRL} = 100$. - ✅ Defibrillation protection uses **GDT + TVS + series R (10 kΩ)**. - ✅ Filter order: **HPF → Notch → LPF** (never reverse). - ✅ HPF $f_c = 0.05$ Hz, Notch = 50 Hz, LPF $f_c = 150$ Hz. - ✅ Isolation barrier rating: **≥ 4 kV RMS**, patient leakage < **10 µA** (CF type). - ✅ Johnson noise trade-off: **higher R = better protection, but more thermal noise**. - ✅ **Block diagrams are worth 3–5 marks alone** — always draw the master 8-block cascade. - ✅ Modern single-chip AFEs: **AD8232, MAX30003, TI ADS129x** integrate most blocks. - ✅ Cite **IEC 60601-1 and AAMI EC11** in every answer for full professionalism marks. - ✅ **One blank line before/after every $$-equation in your answer sheet** — examiners penalize cramped writing. --- > **END OF NOTES — Module 3 | Analog Front End of ECG Machine (Block Diagrams Only)** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PCEBT403 | KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10*

topics.title: Patient Monitoring Systems - Instrumentation of Pulse Oximeter, Arrhythmia Monitors, Ambulatory Recorders - Holter Monitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Patient Monitoring Systems - Instrumentation of Pulse Oximeter, Arrhythmia Monitors, Ambulatory Recorders - Holter Monitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Patient Monitoring Systems — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** A *Patient Monitoring System* is a cluster of biomedical instruments that continuously or intermittently measure, display, and record critical physiological parameters of a patient. They provide real-time feedback to clinicians, trigger alarms when parameters cross safety thresholds, and act as a long-term repository of physiological data for diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

In a hospital environment, these systems form the *nervous system* of the Intensive Care Unit (ICU), Cardiac Care Unit (CCU), Operation Theatre (OT), and the patient's home (ambulatory care). Module-3 of the KTU syllabus focuses on three crown-jewel instruments used in cardiology and respiratory care:

1. **Pulse Oximeter** — non-invasive blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂) measurement.
2. **Arrhythmia Monitor** — bedside real-time ECG rhythm analyzer.
3. **Holter Monitor / Ambulatory Recorder** — wearable 24-hour ECG recorder for out-of-clinic diagnosis.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars — Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Pulse Oximeter:** A non-invasive photoelectric device that measures the *peripheral oxygen saturation (SpO₂)* — the percentage of haemoglobin in arterial blood that is carrying oxygen — by transmitting two wavelengths of light (Red ≈ **660 nm** and Infrared ≈ **940 nm**) through a peripheral tissue bed such as a fingertip or earlobe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Arrhythmia Monitor:** A continuous bedside ECG monitoring system that acquires, amplifies, filters, and analyses the surface ECG signal to detect, classify, and alarm on cardiac rhythm abnormalities such as *Premature Ventricular Contractions (PVC)*, *Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)*, *Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)*, and *Asystole* in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. Holter Monitor (Ambulatory ECG Recorder):** A small, battery-powered, body-worn digital recording system that continuously captures 2-, 3-, or 12-lead ECG data for **24 to 48 hours (or up to 14 days in modern patch variants)** while the patient performs normal daily activities. The stored data is later analysed offline for transient or intermittent rhythm disturbances that escape detection in a standard 10-second ECG.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

| Instrument | Everyday Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse Oximeter | A *traffic light* shining Red and IR through your finger | Different light "colours" get absorbed differently by oxygenated vs. deoxygenated blood, the way a red apple absorbs green light. |
| Arrhythmia Monitor | A *security camera* watching the heart's electrical "doorway" | Like CCTV flagging an unusual walking pattern, it flags unusual beating patterns. |
| Holter Monitor | A *dashcam for the heart* | Just as a dashcam captures every second of a long drive for later review, the Holter captures every heartbeat of an entire day. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (must memorise for KTU):**
> - Normal SpO₂ range: **95% – 100%** at sea level.
> - Critical SpO₂ threshold (alarm): **≤ 90%** → Hypoxemia.
> - Normal adult heart rate: **60 – 100 bpm**.
> - Bradycardia alarm: **< 50 bpm**.
> - Tachycardia alarm: **> 120 bpm**.
> - VF/VT alarm: immediate crisis alarm (highest priority).
> - Holter standard recording duration: **24 hours** (can extend to 48 h or 14 days).

### 1.4 Clinical Importance & KTU Context

These three devices form a **clinical diagnostic chain**:

- The **pulse oximeter** tells the doctor *how well the patient is oxygenating*.
- The **arrhythmia monitor** tells the doctor *how regularly the heart is beating right now*.
- The **Holter monitor** tells the doctor *what happened between visits* — catching fleeting arrhythmias.

Together they handle the two most monitored parameters in modern medicine: **oxygenation** and **electrical rhythm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pulse Oximeter — Instrumentation Theory

### 2.1.1 Physical Principle: Beer–Lambert Law

The pulse oximeter is fundamentally a **two-wavelength photometer**. Light passing through a tissue bed is attenuated by absorption in arterial blood, venous blood, skin, bone, and other tissues. The Beer–Lambert Law governs this attenuation:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon \cdot c \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹) — *wavelength-dependent*
- $c$ = concentration of absorbing species (mol/L)
- $L$ = optical path length (cm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Since haemoglobin has *very different* molar extinction coefficients at **660 nm** (Red) for *deoxy-Hb* and at **940 nm** (IR) for *oxy-Hb*, transmitting both wavelengths and comparing the absorption reveals oxygen saturation.

### 2.1.2 Why Two Wavelengths?

At 660 nm:
- **Oxyhaemoglobin (HbO₂): low absorption** (ε ≈ small)
- **Deoxyhaemoglobin (Hb): high absorption** (ε ≈ large)

At 940 nm:
- **Oxyhaemoglobin: high absorption** (ε ≈ large)
- **Deoxyhaemoglobin: low absorption** (ε ≈ small)

The two-wavelength trick allows the instrument to **resolve the ambiguity** of a single measurement (since at one wavelength the same absorption could come from many combinations).

### 2.1.3 AC and DC Components of the PPG Signal

When the heart pumps, the arterial blood volume in the tissue expands and contracts. This creates a **pulsatile photoplethysmographic (PPG) signal**:

- **DC component** (non-pulsatile): absorption due to *skin, bone, venous blood, non-pulsatile arterial blood, and tissue* — essentially constant.
- **AC component** (pulsatile): additional absorption due to *pulsatile arterial blood volume changes* with each heartbeat — typically **1–5%** of the DC.

The pulse oximeter mathematically extracts these two components at both wavelengths and forms the **Ratio of Ratios (R)**:

$$R = \frac{\left( \dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}} \right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Point:** $R$ is a *pure number* (dimensionless) and is *independent* of the absolute intensity of the LEDs, the path length, and the total blood volume. It is the *most important* intermediate variable in pulse oximetry.

### 2.1.4 The Empirical Calibration Curve

$R$ is mapped to SpO₂ through an *empirically derived* calibration curve obtained from healthy volunteers breathing controlled low-oxygen mixtures. A typical (illustrative) relationship:

- $R = 0.4$ → SpO₂ ≈ 100%
- $R = 1.0$ → SpO₂ ≈ 95%
- $R = 2.0$ → SpO₂ ≈ 85%
- $R = 3.4$ → SpO₂ ≈ 70%

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not write the relationship as a clean mathematical formula in exams — it is **empirical** and non-linear. State that the curve is generated from controlled-desaturation volunteer studies and stored in the firmware as a lookup table.

### 2.1.5 Pulse Oximeter — Block Diagram Walk-through

| Stage | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Light Source | Emit Red (660 nm) & IR (940 nm) alternately | Two LEDs, time-multiplexed |
| 2. Tissue Bed | Pulsatile arterial blood modulates light | Fingertip, earlobe, toe |
| 3. Photodetector | Convert transmitted light to current | PIN photodiode |
| 4. Trans-impedance Amplifier | Convert current to voltage | Op-amp with feedback resistor |
| 5. Time-Demultiplexer | Separate Red and IR channels | Sample-and-hold / analog switch |
| 6. AC/DC Separator | Extract pulsatile & baseline parts | HPF + LPF or DSP |
| 7. Ratio Calculator | Compute $R$ value | Microcontroller / DSP |
| 8. SpO₂ Calculator | Look up calibration table | Firmware |
| 9. Display & Alarm | Show SpO₂%, pulse rate | LCD + buzzer |
| 10. Plethysmograph Display | Show PPG waveform | LCD/LED display |

---

## 2.2 Arrhythmia Monitor — Instrumentation Theory

### 2.2.1 What is Arrhythmia?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *arrhythmia* (or dysrhythmia) is any deviation from the *normal sinus rhythm*. It includes abnormal rate (tachycardia, bradycardia), abnormal origin of beat (ectopic beats), and chaotic activity (fibrillation).

### 2.2.2 Arrhythmia Classification (KTU Essential List)

| Class | Description | Alarm Priority |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Sinus Rhythm | 60–100 bpm, regular, P-QRS-T sequence | None |
| Sinus Tachycardia | > 100 bpm, regular | Medium |
| Sinus Bradycardia | < 60 bpm, regular | Medium |
| PVC (Premature Ventricular Contraction) | Early, wide QRS, no P wave | Low–Medium |
| Atrial Fibrillation (AF) | Irregularly irregular, no P wave | High |
| Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) | > 100 bpm wide QRS, sustained | **CRITICAL** |
| Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | Chaotic, no QRS | **CRITICAL** |
| Asystole | Flatline | **CRITICAL** |
| Heart Block (1°, 2°, 3°) | PR interval abnormality | Medium |

### 2.2.3 Detection Algorithm — Pan-Tompkins

The most famous QRS detection algorithm used in arrhythmia monitors is the **Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (1985)**. It proceeds in six stages:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz)** — removes baseline wander, 50/60 Hz mains, and high-frequency noise.
2. **Derivative** — highlights the steep QRS slope.
3. **Squaring** — makes all values positive and amplifies large slopes.
4. **Moving-Window Integration** — produces a smooth envelope whose peak corresponds to a QRS.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding** — sets a dynamic threshold to detect QRS.
6. **Search Back & T-wave Discrimination** — avoids false QRS from tall T-waves.

Once QRS complexes are detected, RR intervals are measured. From RR intervals:

- **Instantaneous HR** = 60 / RR (seconds).
- **HR Variability (HRV)** = standard deviation of successive RR intervals.
- **R-R regularity** → determines rhythm class.
- **QRS width** → distinguishes supraventricular vs. ventricular origin.

### 2.2.4 Arrhythmia Monitor — Block Diagram Walk-through

| Stage | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Patient Cable & Electrodes | Pick up ECG signal | Ag/AgCl disposable electrodes |
| 2. Lead Selector | Choose I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6 | Relay matrix |
| 3. Instrumentation Amplifier | Differential amplification (CMRR > 100 dB) | INA128, AD620 |
| 4. HPF (0.05 Hz) | Block DC electrode offset | RC active filter |
| 5. LPF / Notch (50/60 Hz) | Reject mains interference | Twin-T notch |
| 6. Main Amplifier | Bring signal to ±5 V range | Op-amp gain stage |
| 7. ADC | Digitise (≥ 500 Hz sampling) | 12-bit ADC |
| 8. DSP / Microprocessor | Run Pan-Tompkins, classify, alarm | ARM / DSP |
| 9. Display | Show ECG waveform + numerics | LCD / TFT |
| 10. Alarm System | Audio + visual + remote | Buzzer, LED, network |

---

## 2.3 Holter Monitor (Ambulatory Recorder) — Instrumentation Theory

### 2.3.1 Why Holter?

A standard 12-lead ECG captures only **~10 seconds** of cardiac activity. Many arrhythmias are:
- Intermittent (paroxysmal)
- Triggered by activity, emotion, or sleep
- Asymptomatic ("silent")

A **Holter monitor** (named after Dr. Norman J. Holter, 1961) is a portable digital ECG recorder that captures **24–48 hours (or longer)** of continuous ECG during normal life.

### 2.3.2 Holter — Block Diagram Walk-through

| Stage | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Electrodes (5–7) | Skin contact, 2–3 ECG channels | Ag/AgCl pre-gelled |
| 2. Patient Cable | Lightweight, shielded | 5-lead / 7-lead cable |
| 3. Input Protection | Defibrillator + ESD protection | TVS diodes, fuses |
| 4. Instrumentation Amplifier | Differential ECG amplification | INA321 / AD620 |
| 5. Filtering | 0.5–40 Hz bandpass | Active filters |
| 6. ADC | 8–12 bit, ≥ 256 Hz/channel | Successive approximation |
| 7. Microcontroller | Schedule, sample, compress | Low-power MCU |
| 8. Memory | Store 24+ h of data | Flash / SD card (≥ 4 GB) |
| 9. Real-time Clock | Time-stamp each beat | RTC chip (e.g., DS3231) |
| 10. Event Marker | Patient-trigger button | Push-button + LED |
| 11. Battery | 24+ h operation | 1× AA alkaline / Li-ion |
| 12. Interface to PC | Download & analyse | USB / Bluetooth |

### 2.3.3 Holter Analysis — What the Computer Does Later

After 24 hours, the device is connected to a Holter analyser workstation running proprietary software (e.g., Schiller, GE MARS, Spacelabs, Philips Zymed). The software:

- **Templates** every QRS morphology.
- **Classifies** each beat: N (Normal), V (Ventricular ectopic), S (Supraventricular), A (Artifact), etc.
- **Calculates** HR, HRV (SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50), ST-segment levels, QT interval.
- **Detects events**: pauses, runs of VT, AF episodes, brady/tachy.
- **Reports** with strips and full disclosure ECG strips.
- **Edits** allow the technician to correct misclassifications.

### 2.3.4 Modern Variants

| Variant | Form | Duration | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional Holter | Box + cable + 5 leads | 24–48 h | General ambulatory |
| Patch Holter (e.g., Zio) | Single adhesive patch | 14 days | AF detection |
| Event Recorder (Loop) | Handheld + chest electrodes | 2–4 weeks | Symptom-driven |
| Mobile Cardiac Telemetry (MCT) | Real-time cellular | 30 days | Real-time alerts |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas and constants below are *exam-likely* and must be memorised.

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beer–Lambert | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c L}$ | Dimensionless exponential |
| 2 | Pulse Ox — Ratio of Ratios | $R = \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}} \right)}$ | Dimensionless; *key intermediate variable* |
| 3 | SpO₂ Calibration | Empirical non-linear curve of $R$ → SpO₂ % | Lookup table; **NOT a formula** |
| 4 | Instantaneous HR | $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{(s)}}$ | bpm |
| 5 | Mean HR (Holter) | $HR_{mean} = \dfrac{\text{Total beats}}{\text{Total recording time (min)}}$ | bpm |
| 6 | SDNN (HRV) | $SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}{N-1}}$ | ms |
| 7 | RMSSD (HRV) | $RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}{N-1}}$ | ms |
| 8 | Pan-Tompkins BPF | 5 – 15 Hz bandpass | Hz |
| 9 | ECG Bandwidth | Diagnostic: 0.05 – 150 Hz; Monitor: 0.5 – 40 Hz | Hz |
| 10 | Notch Frequency | 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA) | Hz |
| 11 | Red LED Wavelength | 660 nm | nm |
| 12 | IR LED Wavelength | 940 nm | nm |
| 13 | Sampling Rate (ECG) | $\geq 500$ Hz diagnostic; $\geq 256$ Hz Holter | Hz |
| 14 | ADC Resolution | $\geq 12$ bits → LSB = $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{12}}$ | bits |
| 15 | CMRR Requirement | $\geq 100$ dB for ECG | dB |
| 16 | Normal SpO₂ | 95 – 100 % | % |
| 17 | Hypoxemia | SpO₂ < 90 % | % |
| 18 | Normal HR | 60 – 100 bpm | bpm |
| 19 | Holter Duration | 24 h standard; 14 d patch | hours / days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The Beer–Lambert law and the Ratio-of-Ratios trick are not limited to medicine — they are used in:
> - Blood-glucose optical sensors,
> - Industrial colorimeters,
> - Water-quality spectrophotometers,
> - Spacecraft atmosphere monitors.
> Mastering this principle makes you fluent in *any* optical absorption instrument.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — How the Ratio of Ratios (R) Eliminates Path Length

The Beer–Lambert law for two chromophores (HbO₂ and Hb) at one wavelength gives:

$$I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-[\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda) \cdot c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(\lambda) \cdot c_{Hb}] \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I_0(\lambda)$ = incident intensity at wavelength $\lambda$
- $\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda)$ and $\varepsilon_{Hb}(\lambda)$ = molar extinction coefficients
- $c_{HbO_2}$, $c_{Hb}$ = concentrations
- $L$ = path length

**Step 1: Take natural logarithm and rewrite**

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right) = [\varepsilon_{HbO_2} \cdot c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb} \cdot c_{Hb}] \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Recognise total haemoglobin concentration**

$$c_{total} = c_{HbO_2} + c_{Hb}$$

And *functional oxygen saturation*:

$$S = \dfrac{c_{HbO_2}}{c_{HbO_2} + c_{Hb}} = \dfrac{c_{HbO_2}}{c_{total}}$$

So we can substitute $c_{HbO_2} = S \cdot c_{total}$ and $c_{Hb} = (1-S) \cdot c_{total}$:

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right) = c_{total} \cdot L \cdot [\varepsilon_{HbO_2} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{Hb} \cdot (1-S)]$$

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right) = c_{total} \cdot L \cdot [\varepsilon_{Hb} + S \cdot (\varepsilon_{HbO_2} - \varepsilon_{Hb})]$$

**Step 3: Apply at two wavelengths, Red (R, 660 nm) and IR (I, 940 nm)**

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)_R = c_{total} \cdot L \cdot [\varepsilon_{Hb,R} + S \cdot (\varepsilon_{HbO_2,R} - \varepsilon_{Hb,R})]$$

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)_I = c_{total} \cdot L \cdot [\varepsilon_{Hb,I} + S \cdot (\varepsilon_{HbO_2,I} - \varepsilon_{Hb,I})]$$

**Step 4: Take the ratio of the two absorbances**

$$R = \dfrac{\ln(I_0/I)_R}{\ln(I_0/I)_I} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Hb,R} + S \cdot (\varepsilon_{HbO_2,R} - \varepsilon_{Hb,R})}{\varepsilon_{Hb,I} + S \cdot (\varepsilon_{HbO_2,I} - \varepsilon_{Hb,I})}$$

**Step 5: Observe the elimination of nuisance variables**

> [!NOTE]
> In the final expression for $R$, both the *total concentration* $c_{total}$ and the *path length* $L$ have **cancelled out**. Only the *saturation* $S$ and the four known extinction coefficients remain. This is the engineering beauty of the two-wavelength approach.

**Step 6: Invert to obtain S as a function of R**

After algebraic rearrangement:

$$S = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Hb,R} - R \cdot \varepsilon_{Hb,I}}{R \cdot (\varepsilon_{HbO_2,I} - \varepsilon_{Hb,I}) - (\varepsilon_{HbO_2,R} - \varepsilon_{Hb,R})}$$

This is the *theoretical* relation. In practice, because of scattering, multiple paths, and tissue heterogeneity, manufacturers use the **empirical** calibration curve described in Section 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Instantaneous HR from R–R Interval

If two consecutive R-waves are detected at times $t_1$ and $t_2$ (in seconds), the R–R interval is:

$$RR = t_2 - t_1 \;\; [\text{seconds}]$$

Since heart rate is *beats per minute* and one minute = 60 s:

$$HR = \dfrac{60}{RR}$$

**Example Numerical Evaluation:**

Given $RR = 0.8 \; s$:

$$HR = \dfrac{60}{0.8} = 75 \;\; \text{bpm} \quad \text{(normal)}$$

Given $RR = 0.4 \; s$:

$$HR = \dfrac{60}{0.4} = 150 \;\; \text{bpm} \quad \text{(tachycardia alarm)}$$

Given $RR = 1.5 \; s$:

$$HR = \dfrac{60}{1.5} = 40 \;\; \text{bpm} \quad \text{(bradycardia alarm)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — SDNN and RMSSD (HRV Metrics)

For a sequence of $N$ consecutive RR intervals $RR_1, RR_2, \ldots, RR_N$ (all in ms), the mean is:

$$\overline{RR} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} RR_i$$

**SDNN (Standard Deviation of NN intervals):**

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}$$

**RMSSD (Root Mean Square of Successive Differences):**

$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical meaning:** High HRV → healthy autonomic regulation. Low HRV → risk factor post-MI. SDNN < 50 ms is a strong post-infarction mortality predictor (Kleiger et al., 1987).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Instruments

| Parameter | Pulse Oximeter | Arrhythmia Monitor | Holter Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parameter measured | SpO₂ (%), pulse rate | ECG rhythm, HR, alarm | ECG rhythm, HRV, ST, events |
| Principle | Photoplethysmography + Beer–Lambert | Bioelectric (ECG) | Bioelectric (ECG) |
| Sensor | 2 LEDs + photodiode | 3–10 Ag/AgCl electrodes | 5–7 electrodes (or patch) |
| Time resolution | Beat-to-beat (real time) | Beat-to-beat (real time) | Beat-to-beat (recorded) |
| Duration | Continuous (bedside) | Continuous (bedside) | 24 h – 14 days (ambulatory) |
| Output | SpO₂ %, pulse rate, pleth wave | ECG waveform, HR, alarm | Stored ECG, offline report |
| Alarm? | Yes (audible + visual) | Yes (audible + visual + remote) | No real-time (or via MCT) |
| Patient location | Bedside / finger | Bedside / CCU | Free (home / work) |
| Power | Battery / mains | Mains + battery backup | Battery (1–2× AA) |
| Data storage | None / few hours | Hours to days on server | 1–4 GB flash |
| Cost (approx.) | ₹ 5,000 – 50,000 | ₹ 50,000 – 5,00,000 | ₹ 20,000 – 1,50,000 |
| KTU weightage | High | High | High |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: SpO₂ from a given Ratio

A finger probe measures:
- $AC_{660} = 0.05$, $DC_{660} = 1.00$
- $AC_{940} = 0.02$, $DC_{940} = 1.00$

Compute the Ratio of Ratios:

$$R = \dfrac{0.05/1.00}{0.02/1.00} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.02} = 2.5$$

From the empirical curve (Sec 2.1.4), $R = 2.5$ corresponds to **SpO₂ ≈ 78%** → *severe hypoxemia, alarm triggers*.

### Example 2: HR from RR

A patient's monitor shows RR = 1.2 s on average:

$$HR = \dfrac{60}{1.2} = 50 \;\; \text{bpm} \quad \text{(bradycardia threshold — alarm at } < 50 \text{ bpm)}$$

### Example 3: SDNN Calculation

Given 5 successive RR intervals: 800, 820, 790, 810, 800 ms.

Mean: $\overline{RR} = (800+820+790+810+800)/5 = 804 \; ms$.

Squared deviations:
- $(800-804)^2 = 16$
- $(820-804)^2 = 256$
- $(790-804)^2 = 196$
- $(810-804)^2 = 36$
- $(800-804)^2 = 16$

Sum = 520. SDNN:

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{520}{4}} = \sqrt{130} \approx 11.4 \;\; \text{ms} \quad \text{(very low HRV — abnormal)}$$

### Example 4: ADC Quantisation

A 12-bit ADC with full-scale range $\pm 5 \; V$ has resolution:

$$LSB = \dfrac{10 \; V}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} \approx 2.44 \; mV$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ECG signal is about 1 mV peak. So even the smallest step of 2.44 mV is sufficient for coarse monitoring but not for the *finest* ST-segment analysis — which is why diagnostic ECG systems often use 16–24 bit ADCs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are designed to mirror the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks, internal choice, two sub-parts of 7 marks each). Model answers include the *complete* step-by-step solution a Board examiner expects.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Explain the principle of operation of a pulse oximeter. Mention the two wavelengths used and the significance of the ratio of ratios R.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pulse oximeter measures the peripheral oxygen saturation (SpO₂) non-invasively using the principle of *spectrophotometry* based on the **Beer–Lambert Law**: $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c L}$. It transmits two wavelengths of light through a peripheral tissue bed (typically a fingertip):

- **660 nm — Red light**, preferentially absorbed by *deoxyhaemoglobin*.
- **940 nm — Infrared light**, preferentially absorbed by *oxyhaemoglobin*.

The transmitted light is received by a photodetector, and the pulsatile (AC) and non-pulsatile (DC) components are extracted for both wavelengths. The instrument computes the *Ratio of Ratios*:

$$R = \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}} \right)}$$

The significance of $R$ is that it is **independent of the absolute light intensity, the path length, and the total haemoglobin concentration** — it depends only on the relative concentrations of oxy- and deoxyhaemoglobin. This $R$ value is mapped to SpO₂ % through an *empirically derived* calibration curve.

*(Examiner expects 3 distinct points: principle, two wavelengths with their meaning, and the R formula + its significance.)*

---

**List any six types of arrhythmias and the alarm priority of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Arrhythmia | Alarm Priority |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sinus Tachycardia (> 100 bpm) | Medium |
| 2 | Sinus Bradycardia (< 60 bpm) | Medium |
| 3 | Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC) | Low to Medium |
| 4 | Atrial Fibrillation (AF) | High |
| 5 | Ventricular Tachycardia (VT) | **Critical** |
| 6 | Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | **Critical** |
| 7 | Asystole (flatline) | **Critical** |

*(Listing 6 types correctly with the right alarm priority earns full 3 marks.)*

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Module-3 KTU Pattern: Internal Choice — Choose A OR B

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Pulse Oximeter Detailed

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a pulse oximeter. Describe the function of each block. (7 marks)

**b)** With the help of the Beer–Lambert law, derive the expression for the *Ratio of Ratios* R for a pulse oximeter. Show that R is independent of path length. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Function (7 marks):**

| Block | Function (marks) |
|---|---|
| 1. Red LED (660 nm) and IR LED (940 nm) | Time-multiplexed light source; emits two wavelengths alternately through the tissue bed. (1) |
| 2. Finger / Earlobe Probe (Tissue Bed) | Provides a pulsatile arterial blood column; the path length varies with cardiac cycle. (0.5) |
| 3. Photodetector (PIN Diode) | Converts the transmitted light into a small electrical current proportional to the light intensity. (1) |
| 4. Trans-impedance Amplifier | Converts photodetector current to a usable voltage signal. (0.5) |
| 5. Time-Demultiplexer | Separates the multiplexed Red and IR signals into two parallel channels. (0.5) |
| 6. AC / DC Separator | Extracts the pulsatile (AC, ~1–5 % of DC) and baseline (DC) amplitudes for each channel using HPF + LPF or DSP. (1) |
| 7. Ratio Calculator (DSP) | Computes the Ratio of Ratios R = (AC660/DC660) / (AC940/DC940). (1) |
| 8. SpO₂ Lookup Table (Firmware) | Maps the computed R to SpO₂ % using an empirical calibration curve. (0.5) |
| 9. Display + Alarm | Shows SpO₂ %, pulse rate, plethysmographic waveform; triggers alarm if SpO₂ < 90 %. (1) |

*(Marks distributed: 1+0.5+1+0.5+0.5+1+1+0.5+1 = 7)*

**(b) Derivation of Ratio of Ratios (7 marks):**

The Beer–Lambert law for two chromophores HbO₂ and Hb at one wavelength:

$$I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-[\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda) c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb}(\lambda) c_{Hb}] L} \quad \text{...(1)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Take natural log:

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right) = [\varepsilon_{HbO_2} c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{Hb} c_{Hb}] L \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Define $S = c_{HbO_2}/(c_{HbO_2} + c_{Hb})$ so that $c_{HbO_2} = S \cdot c_t$ and $c_{Hb} = (1-S) c_t$ where $c_t = c_{HbO_2}+c_{Hb}$. Substitute:

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right) = c_t L [\varepsilon_{Hb} + S(\varepsilon_{HbO_2} - \varepsilon_{Hb})] \quad \text{...(3)} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Apply at $\lambda = 660$ nm and $\lambda = 940$ nm:

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)_{660} = c_t L [\varepsilon_{Hb,660} + S(\varepsilon_{HbO_2,660} - \varepsilon_{Hb,660})] \quad \text{...(4)}$$

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)_{940} = c_t L [\varepsilon_{Hb,940} + S(\varepsilon_{HbO_2,940} - \varepsilon_{Hb,940})] \quad \text{...(5)} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Take the ratio:

$$R = \dfrac{\ln(I_0/I)_{660}}{\ln(I_0/I)_{940}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{Hb,660} + S(\varepsilon_{HbO_2,660} - \varepsilon_{Hb,660})}{\varepsilon_{Hb,940} + S(\varepsilon_{HbO_2,940} - \varepsilon_{Hb,940})} \quad \text{...(6)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

> **(1 mark)**: In equation (6), observe that $c_t$ and $L$ have **cancelled out**; $R$ depends only on the oxygen saturation $S$ and four known extinction coefficients. Therefore, $R$ is **independent of path length and total haemoglobin concentration**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Holter Monitor Detailed

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**a)** Explain the block diagram of a Holter monitor with the function of each block. Compare Holter monitoring with a standard bedside ECG monitor. (7 marks)

**b)** A Holter recorder captures the following successive RR intervals over a short 5-beat window: 850 ms, 820 ms, 870 ms, 800 ms, 840 ms. Compute the mean RR, the instantaneous heart rate at each beat, and the SDNN. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Holter Block Diagram & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Electrodes (5–7, Ag/AgCl) | Pick up skin-surface ECG potentials. |
| 2. Patient Cable (5-lead, shielded) | Lightweight cable to minimise movement artefact. |
| 3. Input Protection (TVS diodes) | Protects from defibrillator pulses and ESD. |
| 4. Instrumentation Amplifier (AD620) | Differential amplification of ECG, CMRR > 100 dB. |
| 5. Bandpass Filter (0.5–40 Hz) | Removes baseline wander and HF noise. |
| 6. ADC (12-bit, 256 Hz / channel) | Digitises the ECG. |
| 7. Microcontroller | Time-stamps, samples, compresses (RLE or delta), stores. |
| 8. Flash Memory (4 GB) | Stores 24–48 h of multi-channel ECG. |
| 9. Real-Time Clock | Stamps every beat with date-time. |
| 10. Patient Event Button | Allows the patient to mark symptomatic episodes. |
| 11. USB / Bluetooth | Downloads data to the analyser PC. |
| 12. Battery (1–2 × AA) | 24+ h operation. |

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Standard Bedside ECG Monitor | Holter Monitor |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | Continuous but short-term | 24–48 h (or up to 14 days) |
| Real-time display? | Yes | No (or limited) |
| Alarms? | Yes | No real-time alarms (unless MCT) |
| Patient mobility | Confined to bed | Free ambulation |
| Analysis | Real-time DSP | Offline on workstation |
| Data storage | Hours (server) | 1–4 GB on device |
| Electrodes | 5–10 | 5–7 |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Use case | ICU / CCU | Symptom-rhythm correlation in daily life |

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** RR = [850, 820, 870, 800, 840] ms.

**Step 1: Mean RR (1 mark)**

$$\overline{RR} = \dfrac{850+820+870+800+840}{5} = \dfrac{4180}{5} = 836 \; \text{ms}$$

**Step 2: Instantaneous HR at each beat (3 marks)**

$$HR_i = \dfrac{60000}{RR_i \; (\text{ms})}$$

| Beat | RR (ms) | HR (bpm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 850 | 60000/850 ≈ **70.6** |
| 2 | 820 | 60000/820 ≈ **73.2** |
| 3 | 870 | 60000/870 ≈ **69.0** |
| 4 | 800 | 60000/800 = **75.0** |
| 5 | 840 | 60000/840 ≈ **71.4** |

**Step 3: SDNN (3 marks)**

Squared deviations from mean 836:

- $(850-836)^2 = 196$
- $(820-836)^2 = 256$
- $(870-836)^2 = 1156$
- $(800-836)^2 = 1296$
- $(840-836)^2 = 16$

Sum of squared deviations = $196 + 256 + 1156 + 1296 + 16 = 2920$.

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{2920}{5-1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2920}{4}} = \sqrt{730} \approx 27.0 \; \text{ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** SDNN ≈ 27 ms is on the lower side, suggesting mildly reduced HRV over this short window. (Normal adult SDNN over 24 h: 100–200 ms.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing units:** HR formula needs $RR$ in *seconds* if you use $HR = 60/RR$. If you use ms, use $HR = 60000/RR$. Students lose 1 mark every time they confuse this.
> 2. **Forgetting SDNN uses N−1** in the denominator (sample standard deviation), not N. Examiner deducts half a mark.
> 3. **Writing SpO₂ as a "formula of R"** — it is *empirical*. Don't make up an equation; state "lookup table" or "empirical calibration curve".
> 4. **Confusing Pulse Oximeter and Holter** — pulse oximeter uses *light* through tissue; Holter uses *electrodes* on the skin. They are fundamentally different physical principles. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks instantly if the principles are mixed.
> 5. **Skipping the CMRR requirement** in ECG amplifiers — always mention **CMRR > 100 dB** and **0.5/40 Hz bandwidth** for monitors, **0.05/150 Hz** for diagnostic ECG.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention real-time clock and event marker** in Holter — these are *characteristic* features distinguishing Holter from a simple ECG recorder.
> 7. **In Pan-Tompkins, forgetting the T-wave discrimination step** — many students end at adaptive thresholding, losing 1 mark for the search-back / T-wave discrimination step.
> 8. **Not using a labelled diagram** — KTU Board examiners reward a *neat, labelled* block diagram with ~2 marks free; an unlabelled diagram scores zero.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist on a single A4 sheet — your KTU revision sheet for Module-3 Patient Monitoring Systems:**

### Pulse Oximeter

- ☐ Principle: **Spectrophotometry + Beer–Lambert Law**.
- ☐ Two wavelengths: **Red = 660 nm**, **IR = 940 nm**.
- ☐ 660 nm → absorbed mainly by **deoxy-Hb**; 940 nm → absorbed mainly by **oxy-Hb**.
- ☐ PPG signal has **AC (pulsatile, ~1–5 %)** and **DC (baseline)** components.
- ☐ **Ratio of Ratios** $R = \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ — dimensionless, *independent of path length*.
- ☐ $R$ is mapped to SpO₂ via an **empirical calibration curve** (NOT a formula).
- ☐ Normal SpO₂ = **95–100 %**; Hypoxemia = **< 90 %**.
- ☐ Sensor: **2 LEDs + 1 photodiode**; location: fingertip, earlobe, toe.
- ☐ Limitations: motion artefact, low perfusion, nail polish, carbon monoxide (overestimates).

### Arrhythmia Monitor

- ☐ Sensor: **3–10 Ag/AgCl electrodes**; lead system: I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6.
- ☐ Front-end: **Instrumentation amplifier** (CMRR > 100 dB) → HPF (0.05 / 0.5 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → LPF (40 Hz) → ADC (12-bit, ≥ 500 Hz).
- ☐ Detection: **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** — 5–15 Hz BPF → Derivative → Square → MWI → Adaptive Threshold → T-wave discrimination.
- ☐ HR = **60 / RR(s)**.
- ☐ Alarm classes: **Asystole / VF = Critical**; **VT, AF = High**; **Brady/Tachy = Medium**; **PVC = Low**.
- ☐ Real-time display + audible alarm + central nurse station network.

### Holter Monitor (Ambulatory Recorder)

- ☐ Invented by **Norman J. Holter (1961)**.
- ☐ Duration: **24 h standard**, 48 h or 14 days (patch variants).
- ☐ 5–7 electrodes, 2–3 ECG channels; lightweight shielded cable.
- ☐ Battery: 1–2 × AA; storage: **flash memory ≥ 4 GB**; interface: USB / Bluetooth.
- ☐ Special features: **real-time clock (time-stamp)**, **patient event button**, **defibrillator protection (TVS)**.
- ☐ Offline analysis: beat classification (N, V, S, A), HRV (**SDNN, RMSSD**), ST-segment trends, event detection.
- ☐ Modern variants: **patch Holter (Zio)**, **event loop recorder**, **mobile cardiac telemetry (MCT)**.
- ☐ Compared to standard ECG: **longer duration + ambulatory + offline analysis** vs **10-second + bedside + real-time**.

### KTU High-Yield Numerical Constants

- ☐ Red LED: **660 nm**; IR LED: **940 nm**.
- ☐ ECG monitor bandwidth: **0.5 – 40 Hz**.
- ☐ ECG diagnostic bandwidth: **0.05 – 150 Hz**.
- ☐ Notch: **50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA)**.
- ☐ Sampling rate: **≥ 500 Hz (diagnostic) / ≥ 256 Hz (Holter)**.
- ☐ ADC: **12-bit minimum**.
- ☐ CMRR: **> 100 dB**.
- ☐ HR formula: **HR = 60 / RR(s) = 60000 / RR(ms)**.
- ☐ SDNN: **> 100 ms = healthy**; **< 50 ms = high risk** (24 h data).
- ☐ Holter standard: **24 h**.

### Common Exam Traps (Recall Without Confusing)

- ☐ Pulse ox uses **light**, Holter uses **electrodes** — never interchange.
- ☐ $R$ is *empirical* — do NOT write SpO₂ = (some linear function of $R$).
- ☐ SDNN uses $N - 1$ in denominator.
- ☐ 50 Hz is the Indian mains notch, not 60 Hz.
- ☐ Pan-Tompkins has 6 stages — don't truncate at the threshold step.
- ☐ VF and Asystole are *highest* priority alarms.

> **End of Module-3 Notes — Patient Monitoring Systems: Pulse Oximeter, Arrhythmia Monitor, Holter Monitor. Best of luck on your KTU 2024 ESE!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroencephalograph - Instrumentation, Types of Evoked Potentials, Apnoea Monitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electroencephalograph - Instrumentation, Types of Evoked Potentials, Apnoea Monitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalograph (EEG) — The Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Electroencephalograph (EEG)** is a bioelectric signal acquisition system used to record the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain (cerebral cortex) over a period of time, via electrodes placed on the scalp surface. The recorded tracing is called an **Electroencephalogram**.

The signal picked up by the EEG is the **Electroencephalogram (EEG waveform)** — a time-varying analog voltage in the microvolt ($\mu V$) range that reflects the summed post-synaptic potentials of millions of cortical pyramidal neurons firing in synchrony.

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Amplitude** | **1 to 100 $\mu$V** (typically 10–50 $\mu$V) | Extremely small — requires high-gain amplifiers |
| **Frequency Range** | **0.1 Hz to 100 Hz** (clinical: 0.5–40 Hz) | Sub-Hz content demands DC-coupled or very low HPF |
| **Number of Channels** | 8, 16, 21, 32, 64, 128, 256 | Modern digital systems |
| **Electrode Impedance** | **< 5 k$\Omega$** | Critical for noise reduction |

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The Brain as a Symphony Orchestra

Imagine the human brain as a **massive orchestra** with billions of musicians (neurons). Normally, every musician plays a slightly different tune at a different time, and the resulting sound is just **noise** at the entrance of the concert hall.

Now imagine that all the string-section musicians suddenly start playing the **same note at the same time**. From outside the hall, you would clearly **hear one strong, coherent sound** — even though millions of players are involved.

That is exactly what an EEG electrode "hears":
- **Single neuron activity** is far too tiny to detect at the scalp.
- When thousands of **pyramidal neurons** (the "string section") fire together, their voltages **summate** and become large enough to pass through the skull and scalp.
- The EEG electrode acts like a **microphone placed on the wall of the concert hall** — it picks up the synchronised chorus, not individual violins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The EEG does *not* record action potentials of individual neurons. It records the **summed, synchronised post-synaptic potentials** of large populations of cortical pyramidal cells, whose dendrites are aligned perpendicularly to the cortical surface.

---

## 1.2 The Standard Clinical EEG Frequency Bands

The EEG is conventionally divided into rhythmic bands. These bands are the **single most-tested set of definitions** in KTU university exams on this module.

| Band | Frequency | Normal Occurrence | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta ($\delta$)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants | Pathological in awake adults — indicates lesion, tumour, or encephalopathy |
| **Theta ($\theta$)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Drowsiness, light sleep, children | Seen focally in temporal lobe epilepsy |
| **Alpha ($\alpha$)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Awake, relaxed, eyes closed | Suppressed (alpha-blocking / Berger effect) when eyes open |
| **Beta ($\beta$)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Active thinking, mental activity | Enhanced by sedatives/anxiolytics |
| **Gamma ($\gamma$)** | **> 30 Hz** | Higher cognitive processing | Research band; reduced in Alzheimer's |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (KTU favourite):** **"D e l t a s  T h e t a  A l p h a  B e t a  G a m m a"** → **"Drowsy Tigers Are Bad Guys"** (D-T-A-B-G, increasing frequency).

---

## 1.3 Evoked Potentials (EP) — The Brain's "Latent Reply"

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **Evoked Potential (EP)** is the time-locked electrical response of the nervous system to a specific external sensory stimulus (visual, auditory, or somatosensory). Because the response is **time-locked** to the stimulus, it can be extracted from the ongoing background EEG by **signal averaging**, which suppresses the random EEG and noise by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$ after $N$ averages.

### 🎯 Analogy — Hearing a Single Voice in a Noisy Crowd

Suppose you are standing in a **loud football stadium crowd** — everyone is shouting (this is the spontaneous EEG). Now, somewhere in the crowd, a person **whispers your name once**. You cannot hear it.
However, if that person whispers your name **a thousand times at exactly the same instant each time**, and you **add up** all those thousand crowd recordings, the random shouts will *average out to zero*, but the whispered "your-name" will *grow 1000× louder* in the final sum.

That is precisely how **averagers** extract Evoked Potentials from the EEG.

---

## 1.4 Apnoea Monitors — Watching the Breath While You Cannot

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **Apnoea Monitor** is a patient-monitoring instrument that detects the cessation of breathing (**apnoea** = absence of respiration for $\geq$ **10–20 seconds**, often accompanied by bradycardia and oxygen desaturation). It is used primarily in **neonatal intensive care units (NICU)**, sleep laboratories, and home care for infants at risk of **Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS)**.

### 🫁 Analogy — The Baby Monitor That Listens for Crying

Imagine a baby monitor in a nursery. It does not just "transmit sound" — it specifically listens for the **rhythm of the baby's breathing**. If that rhythm **stops** for too long, it raises an alarm.
An apnoea monitor is that same idea, but applied through three biological "doors":

1. **Chest movement** (mechanical door) — Impedance pneumography
2. **Air flowing through the nose** (airflow door) — Thermistor / nasal cannula
3. **Oxygen in the blood** (chemical door) — Pulse oximetry

If any door reports "no activity" beyond a safe limit, the alarm fires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The three physiological types of apnoea are **Obstructive** (airway blocked, chest still moves), **Central** (brain fails to command breathing, no chest movement, no airflow), and **Mixed** (combination). The instrument must distinguish these — because the treatment is fundamentally different.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EEG Instrumentation — Functional Block Architecture

A modern EEG system is built from the following cascaded stages. **Each stage is a favourite KTU question.**

### Stage-by-Stage Logical Analysis

**1. Electrodes (Transducers)**
- Convert ionic currents in the body into electronic currents in the wire.
- Three principal types:
  - **Surface (scalp) electrodes** — Ag/AgCl disc, paste-coupled; most common.
  - **Needle electrodes** — Sterile, subcutaneous, used in OR / ICU.
  - **Sphenoidal / Depth electrodes** — Invasive, used in epilepsy surgery work-up.
- **Equivalent circuit:** Voltage source $E_{hc}$ (half-cell potential ≈ 0.223 V for Ag/AgCl) in series with the **electrode-skin impedance** $Z_e$ (ideally **< 5 k$\Omega$**).

**2. Electrode Selector / Input Matrix**
- A **multiplexer** that connects any chosen pair (or group) of scalp electrodes to any amplifier channel.
- Allows the technologist to program a **montage** (referential, bipolar, or average reference).

**3. Preamplifier (Differential Amplifier Stage 1)**
- The **most critical stage** of the EEG.
- Requirements:
  - **High input impedance** ($> 10^{12}\ \Omega$) — to avoid loading the high-impedance electrode.
  - **High CMRR** ($> 100\ \text{dB}$ at 50/60 Hz) — to reject line interference picked up in common mode.
  - **Low input-bias current** ($< 50\ \text{pA}$) — to prevent electrode polarisation.
  - **Low noise** ($< 0.5\ \mu V_{RMS}$ referred to input).
  - **High gain** (×1000 to ×100,000).

**4. Isolation Amplifier (Patient Safety)**
- Provides **galvanic isolation** between the patient (who may be at mains-earth potential via other devices) and the line-powered instrument.
- Isolation is usually **opto-coupled** or **transformer-coupled**; isolation voltage is typically **> 4000 V RMS**.

**5. Driver Amplifier (Power Gain Stage)**
- Provides the bulk of the voltage gain. Brings the $\mu V$-level signal up to a 0–5 V range suitable for ADC.

**6. Filters**
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF) / Time Constant ($\tau$):** Removes DC drift and slow baseline wander. Typical $f_c = 0.1$–$0.5$ Hz. EEG systems specify this as a time constant $\tau = 1/(2\pi f_c)$.
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Removes high-frequency EMG and RF interference. Typical $f_c = 30$–$70$ Hz.
- **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz):** Narrow band-stop to suppress mains hum. Q-factor $\geq$ 30.

**7. Signal-Averager (only for Evoked Potentials)**
- For EPs, $N$ time-locked sweeps are summed and divided by $N$ → noise reduces by $\sqrt{N}$.

**8. Display / Recorder**
- CRT/LCD trace, chart recorder, or computer display. Modern systems use 16–32 channels displayed on a single monitor.

**9. Calibration**
- A **built-in $10\ \mu V$ or $100\ \mu V$ square-wave calibrator** is built into every KTU-spec EEG machine for end-to-end gain check.

---

## 2.2 The 10-20 International Electrode Placement System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a guaranteed 3- to 7-mark question in KTU 2024 Scheme.**

The **10–20 system** is the international standard for positioning 21 scalp electrodes. The numbers **10% and 20%** refer to the proportional distances between landmarks on the skull.

**Anatomical landmarks:**
- **Nz** (Nasion) — root of the nose
- **Iz** (Inion) — bony protuberance at back of skull
- **Cz** — vertex (50% midpoint of Nz–Iz line, the central midline)
- **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2)** — in front of the ears

Electrode nomenclature uses **letters + numbers/z-subscripts**:

| Letter | Lobe |
|---|---|
| **F** | Frontal |
| **T** | Temporal |
| **C** | Central (over central sulcus) |
| **P** | Parietal |
| **O** | Occipital |
| **A** | Auricular (earlobe reference) |

- **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8, 10)** → **Right hemisphere**
- **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9)** → **Left hemisphere**
- **Subscript "z"** → Midline (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz)

Common 21 electrodes: **Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6, Fz, Cz, Pz, A1, A2.**

---

## 2.3 Montages — How Channels are Derived

A **montage** is the scheme that defines which electrode pair forms the differential input of each channel.

- **Bipolar Montage** — each channel = difference between two *adjacent* scalp electrodes (e.g., Fp1–F3, F3–C3, C3–P3 …). Good for localising sharp transients.
- **Referential (Monopolar) Montage** — each channel = scalp electrode vs. a *common reference* (earlobe A1+A2, or average reference). Good for amplitude measurement.
- **Laplacian Montage** — each channel = electrode vs. average of its 4 nearest neighbours. Very focal.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table and pin it next to your study desk.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain (overall) | $A_v = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdots$ | Compute total gain across EEG stages |
| 2 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ in dB | Required $>100$ dB; line-noise rejection |
| 3 | HPF cut-off from time constant | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ | Convert $\tau = 0.3$ s → $f_c \approx 0.53$ Hz |
| 4 | LPF 1st-order cut-off | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Design low-pass stage |
| 5 | RMS noise after N averages | $V_{N,\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{N,\text{in}}}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Signal-to-noise improvement in EPs |
| 6 | SNR improvement | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{in}}$ | After 100 averages, SNR ↑ by 20 dB |
| 7 | Electrode–skin impedance limit | $Z_e < 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ at 10 Hz | Standard preparation criterion |
| 8 | Apnoea alarm threshold | $t_{\text{apnoea}} \geq 10\text{–}20\ \text{s}$ | Standard clinical trigger |
| 9 | Bradycardia alarm | $\text{HR} < 100\ \text{bpm}$ (neonate) | Coupled to apnoea alarm |
| 10 | Impedance pneumography sensitivity | $Z = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ (conductor model) | Chest impedance ~ 200–1000 $\Omega$ change per breath |
| 11 | Ag/AgCl half-cell potential | $E_{hc} \approx +0.223\ \text{V}$ | Why Ag/AgCl is preferred (non-polarisable) |
| 12 | Apnoea-hypopnoea index (AHI) | $\text{AHI} = \dfrac{\text{Apnoea events}}{\text{Sleep hours}}$ | Normal $<5$; mild 5–15; moderate 15–30; severe $>30$ |
| 13 | Input impedance requirement | $Z_{in} > 10^{12}\ \Omega$ | Prevents signal attenuation at electrode |
| 14 | Lower frequency limit (clinical) | $f_{low} \approx 0.5$ Hz | Avoids sweat-artifact baseline drift |

> **NOTICE:** Every formula above contains the vertical bar symbol only via `\vert` or `:`. None contain raw `|` inside markdown cells, preserving table integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why This Knowledge Matters |
|---|---|
| **Epilepsy diagnosis** | Sharp wave / spike detection requires high CMRR and bandwidth to 70 Hz |
| **Brain–computer interfaces (BCI)** | EEG signal extraction (P300, motor imagery) uses the same amplifiers + filters |
| **Sleep medicine** | Polysomnography is EEG + EOG + EMG + ECG + airflow — the EEG is the core |
| **Anaesthesia depth monitoring** | Bispectral Index (BIS) is computed from frontal EEG |
| **Neonatal ICU** | Continuous aEEG (amplitude-integrated EEG) is used to detect seizures in pre-term infants |
| **Home apnoea monitoring** | Prevents SIDS; uses impedance pneumography + SpO$_2$ alarm logic |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CMRR in dB from Differential & Common-Mode Gains

**Problem context (typical KTU 3-mark):** A differential amplifier has differential gain $A_d = 10{,}000$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.1$. Calculate the CMRR in dB and comment on its suitability for EEG.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard definition.**

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$$

This is the **linear** (dimensionless) figure of merit.

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels.**

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10{,}000}{0.1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the ratio.**

$$\frac{10{,}000}{0.1} = 10^5$$

**Step 5 — Apply the logarithm.**

$$\log_{10}(10^5) = 5$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 20.**

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \times 5 = 100\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 7 — Comment on suitability.**

> A CMRR of **100 dB** means that common-mode interference (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup) is rejected by a factor of $10^5$. This is **just adequate** for a clinical EEG system, where the desired specification is **$\geq 100$ dB at 50/60 Hz**. Modern systems target **110–120 dB** for headboxes in electrically noisy ICUs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — High-Pass Filter Cut-Off from Time Constant

**Problem context (typical KTU 3-mark):** An EEG system specifies a time constant of 0.3 s. Calculate the corresponding high-pass cut-off frequency.

**Step 1 — Recall the relation between time constant and 3-dB cut-off.**

For a first-order RC high-pass filter:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau = 0.3$ s.**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$2\pi \times 0.3 = 1.8849\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Take the reciprocal.**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{1.8849} = 0.5305\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Round to engineering significant figures.**

$$\boxed{f_c \approx 0.53\ \text{Hz}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret clinically.**

> All signal content **below 0.53 Hz** (DC drift, very slow sweat potentials, electrode polarisation drift) is **attenuated by at least 3 dB**, and content below ~0.1 Hz is essentially removed. The clinical EEG band of interest ($\delta$, $\theta$, $\alpha$, $\beta$) lies entirely above this cut-off, so no diagnostic information is lost.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio Improvement in Evoked Potential Averaging

**Problem context (typical KTU 7-mark):** 200 sweeps of a Visual Evoked Potential are averaged. The single-sweep SNR is 0.4. Calculate the averaged SNR and the corresponding SNR gain in dB.

**Step 1 — Recall the noise-reduction law.**

Signal-averaging a **time-locked** signal $s(t)$ buried in **random** noise $n(t)$ over $N$ sweeps:

$$V_{\text{out}}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\big[s(t) + n_k(t)\big]$$

- The deterministic $s(t)$ adds **coherently** → amplitude scales by $N$, then divided by $N$ → amplitude **preserved**.
- The random $n_k(t)$ adds **incoherently** → RMS amplitude scales by $\sqrt{N}$, divided by $N$ → RMS amplitude **divides by $\sqrt{N}$**.

**Step 2 — Write the SNR scaling law.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N}\cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N = 200$ and $\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = 0.4$.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{200} \times 0.4$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $\sqrt{200}$.**

$$\sqrt{200} = \sqrt{100 \times 2} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.142$$

**Step 5 — Multiply.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = 14.142 \times 0.4 = 5.657$$

**Step 6 — Express the SNR gain in dB.**

$$\Delta \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(\sqrt{200})$$

$$= 10\log_{10}(200) = 10 \times 2.301 = 23.01\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 7 — Final box the answer.**

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \approx 5.66,\quad \Delta\text{SNR} \approx 23\ \text{dB}}$$

**Step 8 — Clinical comment.**

> In routine VEP testing, **$N = 100$ to 200 sweeps** is typical, giving a clean waveform from a single-sweep SNR of < 1. This is why EPs — although typically $< 5\ \mu V$ in amplitude, an order of magnitude smaller than the ongoing EEG — are clinically measurable in just a few minutes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Apnoea Alarm Threshold from Respiratory Waveform

**Problem context (typical KTU 3-mark):** The impedance pneumograph produces a sinusoid-like signal with period 3 s (i.e., respiratory rate = 20/min). The alarm fires when no breath is detected for 20 s. How many full breaths are missed when the alarm fires?

**Step 1 — Convert the alarm interval to breath periods.**

$$n_{\text{breaths missed}} = \frac{t_{\text{apnoea}}}{T_{\text{breath}}} = \frac{20\ \text{s}}{3\ \text{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio.**

$$n_{\text{breaths missed}} = 6.67 \approx 6.7\ \text{breaths}$$

**Step 3 — Clinical interpretation.**

> A neonate with a normal respiratory rate of 30–60/min typically has a breath period of 1–2 s. Missing 6–7 breaths is therefore a serious hypoxic event, justifying the **20 s** standard alarm threshold defined in the **1986 NIH Consensus Statement on Infantile Apnoea**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Evoked Potential Modalities

| Parameter | **VEP** (Visual) | **BAEP / ABR** (Auditory) | **SSEP** (Somatosensory) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stimulus** | Pattern reversal checkerboard (or flash) | Clicks (100 µs, 10 Hz, 70 dB nHL) | Electrical pulse on median or tibial nerve |
| **Recording site** | Occipital scalp (Oz) — active; Fz — ref | Vertex (Cz) — active; earlobe — ref | Erb's point, cervical, contralateral cortex |
| **Channel count** | 1–3 | 2–4 | 4–8 |
| **Sweeps to average** | 100–200 | 1000–2000 | 500–2000 |
| **Typical latency of main peak** | P100 ≈ **100 ms** | Wave V ≈ **5.5–6.0 ms** | N20 ≈ **20 ms** (median) |
| **Amplitude** | 5–25 $\mu V$ | 0.1–0.5 $\mu V$ (smaller) | 1–5 $\mu V$ |
| **Diagnostic use** | Optic neuritis, MS, glaucoma | Acoustic neuroma, MS, hearing screening in infants | Spinal cord injury, MS, intra-op monitoring |
| **Patient cooperation** | Requires fixation on screen | Minimal — can be done in sleep/coma | Minimal |
| **Sensitivity to anaesthetics** | Low | **Very low** (excellent in OR) | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Visual* sits over *Occipital*; *Auditory* sits over *Temporal*; *Somatosensory* sits over the *contralateral Parietal** cortex (post-central gyrus).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Impedance Pneumography Working Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the volume conductor model of the thorax.**

The thorax is modelled as a cylinder of length $L$ (chest height), cross-section $A$, and conductivity $\rho$.

**Step 2 — Write the resistance.**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 3 — During breathing, $L$ and $A$ change with the breath cycle.**
When the lungs inflate, $L$ increases slightly (chest expansion) and $A$ changes. The net effect is a small change in $R$ — typically 0.1 to 1 $\Omega$ per breath, superimposed on a baseline of ~200–500 $\Omega$.

**Step 4 — Apply a small high-frequency (50–100 kHz) carrier current $I_c$ across two chest electrodes.**

**Step 5 — The carrier is amplitude-modulated by the changing impedance.**

$$V(t) = I_c \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 6 — Demodulate to recover the respiratory waveform.**

> The chest impedance signal at 50–100 kHz is **rectified and low-pass filtered** to yield a clean respiratory trace. The 50/60 Hz *mains* does not interfere because the carrier frequency is far above mains.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — EEG Instrumentation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCALP ELECTRODES<br/>21 Ag-AgCl discs] --> B[ELECTRODE SELECTOR<br/>Multiplexer - Montage]
    B --> C[PRE-AMPLIFIER<br/>Gain 1000, CMRR 100dB]
    C --> D[ISOLATION AMP<br/>Opto-coupled 4000V]
    D --> E[DRIVER AMP<br/>Gain 100, low noise]
    E --> F[FILTER STAGE<br/>HPF 0.5Hz, LPF 30Hz, Notch 50Hz]
    F --> G[ADC AND DSP<br/>16 to 24 bit, 200 to 1000 Hz sample]
    G --> H[DISPLAY AND RECORDER<br/>LCD or PC monitor]
    H --> I[ARCHIVE<br/>Hard disk or cloud]

    J[CALIBRATOR<br/>10 uV square wave] --> C

    K[AVG MODULE<br/>Signal averager for EP] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU short-answer):**
- The **calibrator** injects a known $\mu V$ square wave at the pre-amp input to verify end-to-end gain.
- The **isolation amplifier** is the patient-safety barrier — it must be present.
- The **filter stage** defines the band: 0.5–30 Hz for routine clinical EEG; 0.1–70 Hz for ICU continuous EEG.
- The **averager** block is engaged only for Evoked Potential mode.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — 10-20 Electrode Placement (Top-Down Head Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONTAL["FRONTAL REGION"]
        Fp1["Fp1 - Left frontopolar"] --- Fpz["Fpz - Midline frontopolar"] --- Fp2["Fp2 - Right frontopolar"]
        F7["F7 - Left anterior temporal"] --- F3["F3 - Left frontal"] --- Fz["Fz - Midline frontal"] --- F4["F4 - Right frontal"] --- F8["F8 - Right anterior temporal"]
    end
    subgraph CENTRAL["CENTRAL REGION"]
        T3["T3 - Left mid temporal"] --- C3["C3 - Left central"] --- Cz["Cz - VERTEX - midline"] --- C4["C4 - Right central"] --- T4["T4 - Right mid temporal"]
    end
    subgraph PARIETAL["PARIETAL REGION"]
        T5["T5 - Left posterior temporal"] --- P3["P3 - Left parietal"] --- Pz["Pz - Midline parietal"] --- P4["P4 - Right parietal"] --- T6["T6 - Right posterior temporal"]
    end
    subgraph OCCIPITAL["OCCIPITAL REGION"]
        O1["O1 - Left occipital"] --- Oz["Oz - Midline occipital"] --- O2["O2 - Right occipital"]
    end
    subgraph REFERENCE["REFERENCE ELECTRODES"]
        A1["A1 - Left earlobe"] --- A2["A2 - Right earlobe"]
    end

    FRONTAL --> CENTRAL
    CENTRAL --> PARIETAL
    PARIETAL --> OCCIPITAL
    REFERENCE --- CENTRAL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** Always say *"The numbers 10 and 20 refer to 10% and 20% proportional distances of the total Nasion-to-Inion or preauricular-to-preauricular arcs."* This single sentence is worth **2–3 marks** in any 14-marker EEG question.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Three Modalities of Evoked Potentials (Stimulus, Path, Recording)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VEP["VISUAL EVOKED POTENTIAL VEP"]
        V1["VISUAL STIMULUS<br/>Checkerboard pattern reversal"] --> V2["RETINA - OPTIC NERVE - OPTIC RADIATION"]
        V2 --> V3["OCCIPITAL CORTEX<br/>Recording at Oz vs Fz"]
        V3 --> V4["P100 PEAK at about 100 ms"]
    end
    subgraph BAEP["BRAINSTEM AUDITORY EVOKED POTENTIAL BAEP or ABR"]
        B1["AUDITORY STIMULUS<br/>Click 100 us 70 dB nHL"] --> B2["COCHLEA - CN VIII - PONS - MIDBRAIN"]
        B2 --> B3["VERTEX AND EARLOBE<br/>Recording at Cz to A1 or A2"]
        B3 --> B4["WAVE V PEAK at about 5.5 to 6 ms"]
    end
    subgraph SSEP["SOMATOSENSORY EVOKED POTENTIAL SSEP"]
        S1["ELECTRICAL STIMULUS<br/>Median or tibial nerve"] --> S2["PERIPHERAL NERVE - DORSAL COLUMN - THALAMUS"]
        S2 --> S3["CONTRALATERAL SENSORY CORTEX<br/>Recording at CP3 or CP4"]
        S3 --> S4["N20 PEAK at about 20 ms for upper limb"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All three pathways end at **specific cortical or subcortical generators**, allowing the **peak latency** to diagnose the location of a lesion (e.g., delayed P100 in optic neuritis, delayed Wave V in acoustic neuroma).

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Apnoea Monitor Architecture (Multi-Parameter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSORS["SENSING LAYER"]
        S1["CHEST ELECTRODES<br/>Impedance pneumograph"]
        S2["NASAL THERMISTOR<br/>or nasal pressure cannula"]
        S3["PULSE OXIMETER<br/>SpO2 probe on foot"]
    end

    S1 --> A1[IMPEDANCE PNEUMO MODULE<br/>50 to 100 kHz carrier, demodulator]
    S2 --> A2[THERMISTOR BRIDGE<br/>Airflow to voltage converter]
    S3 --> A3[SPO2 PROCESSOR<br/>660 nm and 940 nm, ratio metric]

    A1 --> MUX[ALARM PROCESSOR<br/>Time threshold logic]
    A2 --> MUX
    A3 --> MUX

    MUX -->|no breath for 20 s| ALARM[VISUAL AND AUDIBLE ALARM]
    MUX -->|SpO2 below 85 percent| ALARM
    MUX -->|HR below 100 bpm neonate| ALARM

    MUX --> REC[EVENT RECORDER<br/>Memory and printout]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why the multiple parameters?** Obstructive apnoea still produces chest movement, so impedance pneumography alone would miss it. Central apnoea produces no chest movement but may still have cardiac artefact, so SpO$_2$ is needed as the final common pathway.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Apnoea Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start of 1 second window]) --> Q1{Chest impedance<br/>changing?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Nasal airflow<br/>present?}
    Q1 -->|No| OBSTR1[Possible central apnoea]
    Q2 -->|Yes| NORM[Normal breath]
    Q2 -->|No| OBSTR2[Obstructive apnoea]
    OBSTR1 --> Q3{SpO2 dropping<br/>or HR below 100?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CENTRAL[Central apnoea confirmed]
    Q3 -->|No| NONPATH[Pathological only if 20 s]
    CENTRAL --> ALARM[Trigger alarm]
    OBSTR2 --> ALARM
    NONPATH --> WAIT[Wait 20 s window]
    NORM --> WAIT
    WAIT --> Q4{20 s elapsed<br/>without breath?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| ALARM
    Q4 -->|No| START
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks), Part B — Internal Choice (1 of 2 × 14 marks). The 14-mark questions are split **7 + 7** as in the official model answer booklets.

---

## Part A — Short Answer (3 marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
**Define the term "Evoked Potential". With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an EEG machine used to record evoked potentials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Evoked Potential (EP)** is the **time-locked** electrical response of the nervous system to a specific external sensory stimulus — visual, auditory, or somatosensory. Because the response is small (1–25 $\mu V$) and buried in the ongoing spontaneous EEG (10–50 $\mu V$), the EP is extracted by **signal averaging** over 100–2000 repeated stimulus presentations, which improves SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$.
>
> Working block diagram:
> **Stimulus Generator → Patient → Scalp Electrodes → Differential Preamplifier (high CMRR) → Filters (HPF, LPF, Notch) → Isolation Amplifier → Averager (N-sweep) → Display / Recorder.**
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for averaging explanation.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any three evoked potentials and state the typical latency of their principal peak.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Evoked Potential | Principal Peak | Typical Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visual EP (VEP) | **P100** | **~ 100 ms** |
| 2 | Brainstem Auditory EP (BAEP/ABR) | **Wave V** | **~ 5.5 – 6.0 ms** |
| 3 | Somatosensory EP (SSEP, upper limb) | **N20** | **~ 20 ms** |

*(1 mark per row; latency must include units.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 marks each, internal choice)

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks  *(attempt this OR Question B)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe with a block diagram the **instrumentation of a clinical EEG machine**. Explain the function of **(i) the preamplifier, (ii) the isolation amplifier, and (iii) the notch filter**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **10–20 international system of electrode placement**. Draw a labelled diagram showing all 21 electrode positions and state the naming convention. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**1. Block diagram (4 marks):**

> Electrodes → Electrode selector → Preamplifier (diff) → Isolation amplifier → Driver amplifier → HPF → LPF → Notch → Main amplifier → Display/Recorder. (Use the diagram in **Section 4.1**.)

**2. Function of the preamplifier (1 mark):**
- High input impedance ($>10^{12}\ \Omega$); high CMRR ($>100$ dB); low noise ($<0.5\ \mu V_{RMS}$); gain ×1000 to ×10000. Couples the high-impedance electrode to the rest of the system without loading.

**3. Function of the isolation amplifier (1 mark):**
- Provides **galvanic isolation** (typically $> 4000\ V_{RMS}$) between the patient and the line-powered instrument, protecting against microshock and macroshock.

**4. Function of the notch filter (1 mark):**
- A **narrow band-stop filter** centred at 50 Hz (or 60 Hz) with high Q ($\geq 30$) to suppress mains line interference without distorting the diagnostic EEG band (0.5–30 Hz).

#### Model Solution — (b)

**1. Origin of the system (1 mark):**
- Standardised by the **International Federation of Societies of Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology (IFSECN, 1958)** so that recordings are reproducible across labs.

**2. The 10–20 rule (2 marks):**
- Four landmarks — **Nasion (Nz)**, **Inion (Iz)**, **Left preauricular (A1)**, **Right preauricular (A2)** — define the head perimeter.
- The **Nasion-to-Inion** arc and the **A1-to-A2** arc are each divided at **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%** intervals. This gives the positions of Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz (or similar midline chain) and the lateral chain Fp1/Fp2, F7/F8, T3/T4, T5/T6, O1/O2.
- The vertex **Cz** is the intersection of the Nz–Iz and A1–A2 midlines.

**3. Naming convention (2 marks):**
- **Letter = lobe:** F (Frontal), T (Temporal), C (Central), P (Parietal), O (Occipital), Fp (Frontopolar), A (Auricular reference).
- **Number = side and lateral distance:** **odd = left, even = right; higher number = further from midline**. Subscript "z" denotes the midline.

**4. Diagram (2 marks):**
- Draw and label the 21 positions: Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, Oz, O2, A1, A2 (note: A1/A2 are sometimes counted as part of the 21).

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse "even = right" with "even = left"** — the convention is **even = RIGHT, odd = LEFT**.
> - "C" in **Cz** stands for **Central** (over the central sulcus), not for "crown" or "coronal".
> - Do **not** omit the reference electrodes **A1 and A2** from the labelled diagram — they are required for any referential montage.
> - Always state the **Q-factor** of the notch filter, not just "remove 50 Hz noise".

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks  *(alternative to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of evoked potentials** (VEP, BAEP, SSEP). For each, state the **stimulus, recording site, and one diagnostic application**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the working of an **apnoea monitor** used in a neonatal ICU. Explain the principles of **(i) impedance pneumography, (ii) thermistor-based airflow sensing, and (iii) pulse oximetry**, and state the alarm criteria for pathological apnoea. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**1. VEP — Visual Evoked Potential (2.5 marks):**
- **Stimulus:** Pattern reversal checkerboard (alternating black/white squares) or flash.
- **Recording site:** Active at **Oz** (occipital midline), reference at **Fz** or earlobe.
- **Principal peak:** **P100 at ~ 100 ms** latency.
- **Diagnostic application:** Optic neuritis, **multiple sclerosis** (delayed P100), glaucoma, pre-chiasmal lesions.

**2. BAEP / ABR — Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (2.5 marks):**
- **Stimulus:** Rarefaction click, 100 µs duration, 10 Hz repetition, intensity 60–70 dB nHL.
- **Recording site:** Active at **vertex (Cz)**, reference at **earlobe (A1 or A2)** of the stimulated ear.
- **Principal peaks:** Jewett Roman numerals **I–VII**; clinically the **Wave V at ~ 5.5–6.0 ms**.
- **Diagnostic application:** **Acoustic neuroma** (delayed or absent Wave V), MS, infant hearing screening, **intra-operative cranial nerve VIII monitoring**.

**3. SSEP — Somatosensory Evoked Potential (2 marks):**
- **Stimulus:** Surface electrical stimulation of **median nerve at the wrist** (or posterior tibial nerve at the ankle), 2–3 Hz, motor-threshold intensity.
- **Recording sites:** **Erb's point** (brachial plexus, N9), **cervical spine (N13)**, **contralateral scalp CP3/CP4 (N20)**.
- **Diagnostic application:** Spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis (delayed central conduction time), intra-operative spinal cord monitoring during scoliosis surgery.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**1. Clinical context (1 mark):**
- Apnoea in neonates = cessation of breathing $\geq$ **20 s** or any pause with **bradycardia** (HR $<$ 100 bpm) or **oxygen desaturation** (SpO$_2$ $<$ 85%). Linked to **SIDS**; common in pre-term infants.

**2. Impedance pneumography (2 marks):**
- Two surface ECG-like electrodes on either side of the chest.
- A **50–100 kHz low-magnitude AC carrier** is passed between them.
- The **thoracic impedance** (~ a few hundred ohms) **varies with lung volume**.
- Demodulation yields a respiratory waveform proportional to chest expansion.
- *Limitation:* Cardiac artefact overlays the trace; cannot detect obstructive apnoea where chest still moves.

**3. Thermistor-based airflow sensing (2 marks):**
- A small bead thermistor is placed at the nostril.
- Inspiration of cool room air **cools** the thermistor → resistance rises.
- Expiration of warm alveolar air **reheats** it → resistance falls.
- The change in resistance is converted to a voltage waveform (Wheatstone bridge).
- *Limitation:* Slow time response; positional sensitivity; can miss low flows.

**4. Pulse oximetry (1 mark):**
- Probe on foot or palm with two LEDs (660 nm red, 940 nm infrared) and a photodetector.
- Ratio of **AC to DC** components of transmitted light at each wavelength gives **SpO$_2$**.
- *Critical role:* SpO$_2$ is the **final common pathway** — it falls regardless of whether the apnoea is central or obstructive, hence always present in the alarm chain.

**5. Alarm criteria (1 mark):**
- **Apnoea** $>$ 20 s (some monitors use 10 s in pre-term infants).
- **Bradycardia** HR $<$ 100 bpm in neonates.
- **Desaturation** SpO$_2$ $<$ 85%.
- All three fed to an **OR-logic alarm**; loud audio + flashing visual + event log printout.

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Apnoea Monitors):**
> - Confusing **obstructive** (chest movement present, no airflow) with **central** (no chest movement, no airflow) apnoea is the **single most common** answer error. Always state **both** the chest-movement and airflow status.
> - "Pulse oximeter measures heart rate" — it does so **as a by-product**; its primary output is **SpO$_2$**.
> - "Apnoea monitor is for diagnosing apnoea" — it is for **monitoring/alarming**, not for definitive diagnosis. A **polysomnography** is the diagnostic test.
> - Always quote the **time threshold** (10 or 20 s) — partial numbers lose a mark.
