| $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler Shift Equation | $\Delta f$ = frequency shift (Hz), $f_0$ = transmitted freq (Hz), $v$ = blood velocity (m/s), $\theta$ = beam-flow angle (deg), $c$ = 1540 m/s | Core formula — directly yields blood velocity from measurable $\Delta f$ |
| $v = \dfrac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Velocity Reconstruction | Solved for $v$ | Computes actual blood speed in m/s for clinical reporting |
| $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Range / Depth Formula (PW Doppler) | $d$ = sample volume depth (m), $t$ = time delay (s) | Determines depth of blood vessel in pulsed mode |
| $f_{PRF} \geq 2 f_{Doppler,max}$ | Nyquist Limit (PW Doppler) | $f_{PRF}$ = pulse repetition freq (Hz) | Avoids aliasing artifacts in pulsed systems |
| $v_{max} = \dfrac{c^2}{8 f_0 d \cos\theta}$ | Maximum Unambiguous Velocity | Limits PW Doppler | Determines the highest measurable flow speed at depth $d$ |
| $Z_{tissue} = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic Impedance | $\rho$ = tissue density (kg/m³) | Governs reflection at tissue interfaces — key for transducer design |
| $\text{Resolution}_{axial} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | Axial Resolution | $BW$ = transducer bandwidth | Determines shortest distinguishable echo time |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Never use the speed of sound in air (**343 m/s**) in Doppler equations — KTU examiners deduct marks for this. Always use **$c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$** for human soft tissue and blood.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The UDBFM principle is not limited to blood flow. The same physics drives:
- **Fetal heart rate monitors** in obstetrics.
- **Industrial flow meters** for slurries and chemicals (where electrodes fail).
- **Doppler radar** in meteorology and defense.
- **Doppler ultrasound echocardiography** for cardiac valve assessment.
- **Laser Doppler vibrometry** for non-contact vibration measurement of structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Doppler Shift Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Objective:** Starting from first principles, derive the governing equation that relates the Doppler frequency shift to the velocity of red blood cells. This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark question** in the KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Establish the coordinate system and assumptions.**
Let the transmitting transducer be fixed in space. The transmitted wave travels along the $+x$ direction through a medium with sound speed $c$. The blood vessel is oriented at an angle $\theta$ to the beam axis. RBCs move with velocity $v$ along the vessel. The component of RBC velocity *along the beam direction* is $v_x = v \cos\theta$.

**Step 2 — Frequency observed by the moving RBC (source stationary, observer moving).**
The standard Doppler formula for a moving observer and stationary source is:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v_x}{c} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

*Textual logic:* The moving observer (RBC) "intercepts" wavefronts faster because it advances towards the oncoming wave. The plus sign in the numerator accounts for the observer moving *toward* the source.

**Step 3 — Frequency of the reflected wave as received by the stationary transducer (source moving, observer stationary).**
The RBC now acts as a source moving *toward* the receiver. The received frequency is:

$$f_r = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v_x} \right) = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

*Textual logic:* A source moving toward the receiver compresses the wavefronts it emits, raising the received frequency. The denominator $c - v\cos\theta$ reflects that the source is *chasing* its own wavefronts at relative speed $c - v\cos\theta$.

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

Cancel the $c$ in the numerator of the second factor with the $c$ in the denominator of the first:

$$f_r = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-velocity approximation.**
Since blood flow velocity $v$ is on the order of 0.1 to 1.5 m/s, and $c = 1540$ m/s, we have $v/c \approx 10^{-4}$, justifying the use of the binomial series:

$$\frac{1}{1 - \left(\frac{v\cos\theta}{c}\right)} \approx 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} + \left(\frac{v\cos\theta}{c}\right)^2 + \cdots$$

Truncating after the linear term (higher orders are negligible at $\sim 10^{-8}$):

$$f_r = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Expand the product:**

$$f_r = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} \right)^2 = f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2v\cos\theta}{c} + \frac{v^2\cos^2\theta}{c^2} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Drop the second-order (squared) term.**
Since $(v/c)^2 \approx 10^{-8}$, this term is vanishingly small:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{2v\cos\theta}{c} \right) = f_0 + \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the Doppler frequency shift:**

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This is the **canonical, exam-ready, final Doppler equation**. ∎

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE 2019-Style Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Doppler blood flow meter transmits at $f_0 = 5 \text{ MHz}$ with the beam at angle $\theta = 30°$ to a blood vessel. The detected Doppler shift is $\Delta f = 1500 \text{ Hz}$. Compute the blood flow velocity. Given $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

Step 1 — Write the Doppler equation and isolate $v$:

$$v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:

$$v = \frac{1500 \times 1540}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times \cos 30°}$$

Step 3 — Evaluate $\cos 30°$:

$$\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$$

Step 4 — Compute the denominator:

$$2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 0.8660 = 8.66 \times 10^6$$

Step 5 — Compute the numerator:

$$1500 \times 1540 = 2{,}310{,}000 = 2.31 \times 10^6$$

Step 6 — Divide:

$$v = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{8.66 \times 10^6} \approx 0.2667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Result:** $v \approx 0.267 \text{ m/s}$ (a typical venous flow velocity). The answer is dimensionally correct (m/s) and physically reasonable.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs

| **Design Parameter** | **Increase Effect on $\Delta f$** | **Clinical Consequence** |
|---|---|---|
| Transmit frequency $f_0$ | $\Delta f$ increases linearly | Better sensitivity to slow flow, but **reduced tissue penetration** |
| Beam angle $\theta$ | $\Delta f$ decreases as $\cos\theta$ drops | At $\theta = 90°$, $\Delta f = 0$ (no measurement possible) |
| Blood velocity $v$ | $\Delta f$ increases linearly | Allows detection of stenosis (turbulent high-velocity jets) |
| Depth of vessel $d$ | No direct effect on $\Delta f$ | Limits $f_0$ choice — deeper vessels need lower $f_0$ |
| Medium speed $c$ | $\Delta f$ inversely proportional | Tissues with higher $c$ yield smaller shifts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a CW Doppler Blood Flow Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osc1[Oscillator f0 5 MHz] --> txamp[RF Power Amplifier]
    txamp --> txcr[TX Piezo Crystal]
    txcr -.Ultrasound Beam.-> vessel[Blood Vessel with RBCs]
    vessel -.Reflected Echo.-> rxcr[RX Piezo Crystal]
    rxcr --> rxamp[RF Low Noise Amplifier]
    rxamp --> mixer[RF Mixer Demodulator]
    osc1 --> mixer
    mixer --> bpf[Bandpass Filter 100 Hz to 15 kHz]
    bpf --> audioamp[Audio Amplifier]
    bpf --> fft[FFT Spectrum Analyzer]
    audioamp --> spk[Loudspeaker Output]
    fft --> disp[LCD Velocity Display]
    osc1 --> qdet[Quadrature Phase Detector]
    rxamp --> qdet
    qdet --> dir[Directional Discriminator Forward Reverse]
    dir --> disp
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Pulsed-Wave (Range-Gated) Doppler System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    clk[Master Clock 5 MHz] --> txgate[Transmit Gate Pulse Generator]
    txgate --> txcr[Transmit Receive Crystal]
    txcr -.Ultrasound Pulse.-> body[Patient Body Tissue]
    body -.Echo at Time t.-> txcr
    txcr --> rxgate[Range Gate Time Delay Control]
    rxgate --> sample[Sample Volume Selector]
    sample --> mixer[Phase Quadrature Mixer]
    clk --> mixer
    mixer --> i_ch[I Channel 0 deg]
    mixer --> q_ch[Q Channel 90 deg]
    i_ch --> mti[MTI Wall Filter]
    q_ch --> mti
    mti --> sad[Directional Spectrum Analyzer]
    sad --> lcd[LCD Display Velocity Profile vs Time]
    rxgate --> depthctrl[Depth Selector d ct over 2]
```

### 4.3 Beam–Vessel Geometry Diagram (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vessel1[Blood Vessel Wall]
        r1[RBC moving at velocity v]
        r2[RBC moving at velocity v]
        r3[RBC moving at velocity v]
    end
    beam1[Incident Ultrasound Beam f0] --> r1
    r1 -.Reflected Beam fr.-> rec1[Receiver Crystal]
    beam1 -.Angle theta.-> r2
    r2 -.Reflected Beam fr.-> rec1
    beam1 -.Angle theta.-> r3
    r3 -.Reflected Beam fr.-> rec1
    note1[Cosine Factor cos theta reduces effective velocity component] -.- beam1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The figure above illustrates the geometric relationship between the ultrasound beam, the blood vessel, and the RBC motion. The factor $\cos\theta$ projects the true RBC velocity onto the beam axis — only this projected component contributes to the Doppler shift. This is why the **angle of insonation $\theta$** must be kept small (ideally $< 60°$) during clinical scanning to obtain accurate velocity measurements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Doppler Effect as applied to ultrasonic blood flow measurement. State the governing equation and explain each term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Doppler Effect is the apparent change in frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source and the observer. In an ultrasonic blood flow meter, ultrasound of frequency $f_0$ is transmitted into the body. The moving red blood cells (RBCs) act as moving reflectors, producing a reflected wave of frequency $f_r$. The difference $\Delta f = f_r - f_0$ is called the Doppler shift.

The governing equation is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

where $f_0$ is the transmitted ultrasound frequency, $v$ is the blood velocity, $\theta$ is the angle between the beam and the flow direction, and $c$ is the speed of sound in tissue (1540 m/s). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the speed of sound in blood taken as 1540 m/s and not 343 m/s in Doppler shift calculations?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The speed of sound in a medium is given by $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$, where $K$ is the bulk modulus and $\rho$ is the density. Human soft tissue and blood have a much higher bulk modulus and density combination than air, yielding an average propagation speed of **1540 m/s**. The value **343 m/s** corresponds to the speed of sound *in air* at 20°C, which is irrelevant because the UDBFM operates exclusively within biological tissue. Using the wrong value of $c$ would introduce an error factor of approximately **1540/343 ≈ 4.5×**, rendering the velocity computation incorrect. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks Total]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the Doppler shift equation $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ for an ultrasonic blood flow meter, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CW Doppler flow meter operates at $f_0 = 4 \text{ MHz}$ with the beam at $\theta = 45°$ to a carotid artery. The observed beat frequency is $f_b = 1.2 \text{ kHz}$. Calculate: (i) the blood flow velocity, and (ii) the diameter of the artery if the volumetric flow rate is $Q = 5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$. Take $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps to write in the answer script are:

1. Define the geometry: stationary transmitter, RBC moving with velocity $v\cos\theta$ along the beam. **(1 mark)**
2. Apply moving-observer formula: $f' = f_0 \left( \dfrac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$. **(1 mark)**
3. Apply moving-source formula: $f_r = f' \left( \dfrac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$. **(1 mark)**
4. Substitute to get $f_r = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}$. **(1 mark)**
5. Apply binomial approximation for $v \ll c$. **(1 mark)**
6. Expand and drop $(v/c)^2$ term. **(1 mark)**
7. State the final result $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ and list the assumptions (RBCs are point scatterers, $v \ll c$, beam is monochromatic, attenuation is neglected, flow is laminar). **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Velocity calculation:**

Step 1 — Write the velocity reconstruction formula:

$$v = \frac{f_b \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

Step 2 — Substitute $f_b = 1200 \text{ Hz}$, $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$, $f_0 = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, $\theta = 45°$:

$$v = \frac{1200 \times 1540}{2 \times 4 \times 10^6 \times \cos 45°}$$

Step 3 — Evaluate $\cos 45° = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$:

$$v = \frac{1{,}848{,}000}{5.657 \times 10^6} = 0.3267 \text{ m/s}$$

**Result:** $v \approx 0.327 \text{ m/s}$ (typical of moderate arterial flow). **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Diameter calculation:**

Step 4 — Recall $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v$.

Step 5 — Solve for $d$:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}}$$

Step 6 — Convert $Q = 5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 0.3267}} = \sqrt{\frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0265}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{1.948 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $d \approx 4.41 \text{ mm}$ (clinically realistic carotid artery diameter). **(4 marks)**

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) [14 Marks Total]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler blood flow meter**. Compare it with the **Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler** system in terms of depth discrimination and maximum measurable velocity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PW Doppler system uses $f_0 = 5 \text{ MHz}$ pulses to interrogate a vessel at depth $d = 5 \text{ cm}$. The pulse repetition frequency is $f_{PRF} = 10 \text{ kHz}$. Calculate: (i) the maximum unambiguous Doppler shift, (ii) the maximum unambiguous blood velocity at $\theta = 0°$, and (iii) state whether aliasing will occur if a stenosis produces a true Doppler shift of $\Delta f = 6 \text{ kHz}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) CW vs. PW Doppler Explanation (7 marks):**

**CW Doppler Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — describe verbally in script.)

A CW system uses two crystals: a transmitter continuously driven at $f_0$ and a separate receiver that picks up the Doppler-shifted echo $f_r$. The two signals are mixed in a demodulator, producing a beat frequency $f_{beat} = f_r - f_0 = \Delta f$, which is audified and analyzed. CW Doppler has **no range gating**, so it detects flow anywhere along the beam. It can measure very high velocities (no Nyquist limit) but **cannot isolate a specific vessel depth**. **(3 marks)**

**PW Doppler System:** (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram.)

A PW system uses a single crystal pulsed at $f_{PRF}$. By electronically time-gating the received echo (delay $t = 2d/c$), the system isolates echoes from a chosen sample volume at depth $d$, providing **range resolution**. However, sampling imposes the **Nyquist limit** $f_{PRF} \geq 2 f_{Doppler,max}$, capping the maximum measurable velocity. **(2 marks)**

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| **Feature** | **CW Doppler** | **PW Doppler** |
|---|---|---|
| Crystals needed | 2 (TX + RX) | 1 (shared) |
| Depth discrimination | No (entire beam) | Yes (sample volume) |
| Maximum velocity | Very high (no limit) | Limited by Nyquist |
| Aliasing risk | None | Yes (if $f_{PRF} < 2\Delta f$) |
| Clinical use | Stenosis jets | Specific vessel mapping |

**(b) PW Doppler Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum unambiguous Doppler shift:**

Step 1 — Apply Nyquist criterion:

$$\Delta f_{max} = \frac{f_{PRF}}{2} = \frac{10{,}000}{2} = 5{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Result:** $\Delta f_{max} = 5 \text{ kHz}$. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Maximum unambiguous blood velocity at $\theta = 0°$:**

Step 2 — Use the Doppler equation solved for $v$:

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta f_{max} \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos 0°} = \frac{5000 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 1}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{7{,}700{,}000}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}$$

**Result:** $v_{max} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}$ at $\theta = 0°$. **(2.5 marks)**

**(iii) Aliasing check:**

Step 3 — Compare actual shift ($\Delta f = 6 \text{ kHz}$) to $\Delta f_{max} = 5 \text{ kHz}$.

Since $6 \text{ kHz} > 5 \text{ kHz}$, the Nyquist criterion is violated.

**Result: Yes, aliasing WILL occur.** The flow will appear reversed or wrap around on the display. The examiner may recommend increasing $f_{PRF}$ or shifting the baseline to resolve it. **(2.5 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Doppler Flow Meter Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong speed of sound (343 m/s for air):** Costs 1 mark immediately. Always use $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$.
> 2. **Missing the factor of 2 in the Doppler equation:** A frequent mistake — students write $\Delta f = f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the small-velocity approximation:** Marks are reserved for stating the assumption $v \ll c$ before simplifying.
> 4. **Confusing $f_{beat}$ with $\Delta f$:** In CW systems, $f_{beat} = f_r - f_0 = \Delta f$ — but students often write it incorrectly. Know your mixer topology.
> 5. **Not stating the angle of insonation assumption:** If the problem says "along the flow direction" or "head-on," $\theta = 0°$ and $\cos\theta = 1$ must be explicitly written.
> 6. **Skipping unit conversions in numerical problems:** $5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$ must be converted to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ before substituting — examiners check this carefully.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Ready):**
> - ⭐ The **canonical Doppler equation** is $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — memorize it verbatim.
> - ⭐ Always use **$c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$** (soft tissue), never 343 m/s (air).
> - ⭐ Two factors of 2: one for the round-trip (TX → RBC → RX), one for the moving-source, moving-observer combined effect.
> - ⭐ **CW Doppler** = 2 crystals, no depth info, no aliasing, high-velocity jets.
> - ⭐ **PW Doppler** = 1 crystal, depth-specific sample volume, **Nyquist-limited** ($f_{PRF} \geq 2\Delta f$).
> - ⭐ **Duplex Doppler** = B-mode imaging + PW Doppler — the clinical standard.
> - ⭐ Typical clinical $f_0$: 2–5 MHz (cardiac), 5–10 MHz (peripheral/vascular).
> - ⭐ Typical $\Delta f$ range: **100 Hz to 15 kHz** — falls in the audible band → can be heard on a loudspeaker.
> - ⭐ Keep $\theta < 60°$ in practice; $\theta = 90°$ gives $\Delta f = 0$ (no measurement).
> - ⭐ Aliasing in PW Doppler = flow appears reversed → fix by raising $f_{PRF}$ or using CW mode.
> - ⭐ Quadrature phase detection (I/Q channels) is the standard technique for **directional discrimination** (forward vs. reverse flow).
> - ⭐ Acoustic impedance mismatch at air-skin interface is the reason for the **ultrasound gel** (impedance matching).
> - ⭐ Piezoelectric material of choice: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)**.
> - ⭐ Axial resolution $\approx \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ — higher bandwidth = better resolution.
> - ⭐ The factor $\cos\theta$ projects blood velocity onto the beam — clinical instruments require accurate angle correction.

---

> *End of Module 4 Notes — Ultrasonic Doppler Blood Flow Meters | PCEBT403 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audiometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Audiometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Audiometer** is a precision electroacoustic instrument used to evaluate the sensitivity and acuity of the human auditory (hearing) system by delivering calibrated acoustic stimuli at precisely controlled frequencies and intensities. The device objectively maps the softest sound a subject can detect — known as the **Hearing Threshold** — across a standardized frequency spectrum, producing a diagnostic graph called an **Audiogram**.

In KTU terminology, an audiometer is classified as a **diagnostic transducer-based bioelectronic instrument** that converts electrical energy into calibrated acoustic energy and records the psychoacoustic response of the patient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Audiometers belong to the family of *audiological diagnostic instruments* that measure the hearing threshold level (HTL) of an individual. The standard clinical range of frequencies tested is **125 Hz to 8 kHz**, and the standard intensity range is **-10 dB HL to 120 dB HL**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a quiet room, and someone slowly turns up the volume of a tone until you can just barely hear it. The volume level at which you first hear it tells the doctor *how sensitive your ears are*. An audiometer is essentially an **"intelligent volume knob"** combined with a **"musical note selector"** — it can produce any pure tone (think of a single flute note) at any precise loudness level, and a chart is made of the lowest loudness you can hear for each note. This chart (audiogram) tells whether your hearing is normal, and if not, *where the problem lies* (e.g., problem with the inner ear nerve or the middle ear bones).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** Audiometer = "Audio-meter" → a device that *measures* how well you can *hear audio*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Test Frequencies | **125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k Hz** | Octave-spaced clinical frequencies |
| Extended High-Frequency Range | Up to **12 kHz – 16 kHz** | For research-grade audiometers |
| Reference Intensity (0 dB HL) | **7.5 µPa** (RMS) at 1 kHz | Threshold of normal hearing |
| Standard Sound Pressure Level | **20 µPa** at 1 kHz | Threshold of audibility in dB SPL |
| Bone Conduction Range | **250 Hz – 4 kHz** | Mechanical vibration testing |
| Clinical dB Range | **-10 dB HL to 120 dB HL** | Air conduction |
| Calibration Standard | **ISO 8253-1 / IEC 60645-1** | International calibration protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:** *dB HL (Hearing Level)* is **NOT** the same as *dB SPL (Sound Pressure Level)*. dB HL is referenced to the average normal hearing threshold at each frequency, while dB SPL is an absolute physical measurement.

### 1.4 Functional Role in the Audiometric Chain

$$
\text{Audiometer} \xrightarrow{\text{Electrical signal}} \text{Earphone/Bone Vibrator} \xrightarrow{\text{Acoustic stimulus}} \text{Human Ear} \xrightarrow{\text{Response}} \text{Threshold Audiogram}
$$

The instrument therefore acts as a **calibrated psychophysical measurement tool** — it does *not* measure hearing objectively (like ECG measures the heart), but rather maps the patient's *perceptual* threshold through controlled acoustic stimulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Wise Operating Principle

A clinical audiometer performs three sequential operations for every test tone presented:

1. **Frequency Selection** – An internal oscillator generates a pure sinusoidal tone at the selected frequency (e.g., 1 kHz).
2. **Intensity Setting** – A precision attenuator reduces the tone amplitude to a desired Hearing Level (in dB HL).
3. **Presentation** – The calibrated tone is routed through a switch to either the Left, Right, or both earphones (or to a bone vibrator placed on the mastoid bone).

The patient indicates by pressing a **response button** when the tone is just audible. The threshold is defined as the lowest intensity at which the patient correctly responds approximately **50% of the time** (using the standard Hughson-Westlake up-down bracketing method).

### 2.2 Fundamental Equations of Audiometry

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the mathematical foundation required for any numerical problem on audiometers in KTU exams. Memorize the dB relationships carefully.

#### 2.2.1 Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL)

The decibel is fundamentally a *logarithmic ratio* of acoustic pressures, referenced to the threshold of human hearing at **1 kHz**.

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{(dB SPL)}
$$

where:
- $p$ = measured RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \;\mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{Pa}$ — standard reference pressure
- $L_p$ = Sound Pressure Level in **dB SPL**

#### 2.2.2 Intensity Level (dB IL)

When expressed in terms of acoustic intensity $I$:

$$
L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{(dB IL)}
$$

where $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\;\text{W/m}^2$.

#### 2.2.3 Hearing Level (dB HL) — The Clinical Scale

$$
L_{\text{HL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{avg}}(f)}\right) \quad \text{(dB HL)}
$$

where $p_{\text{avg}}(f)$ is the average sound pressure corresponding to the normal hearing threshold *at that specific frequency* $f$. ISO 8253-1 publishes reference equivalent threshold sound pressure levels (RETSPLs) for each frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Equation (Frequently Asked):** $\text{dB SPL} = \text{dB HL} + \text{RETSPL}(f)$
> For example, at 1 kHz, RETSPL ≈ **7.5 dB**, so 0 dB HL ≡ 7.5 dB SPL.

#### 2.2.4 Sound Intensity vs Distance — Inverse Square Law

For a point source of sound in free field:

$$
I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}
$$

In decibel form, doubling the distance $r$ from the source reduces the intensity by **6 dB**:

$$
\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right) \quad \text{(dB change with distance)}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Quantity | Reference / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_{\text{ref}})$ | Sound Pressure Level | $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\;\mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{\text{ref}})$ | Intensity Level | $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\;\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | $L_{\text{HL}} = L_{\text{SPL}} - \text{RETSPL}(f)$ | Hearing Level conversion | Frequency-dependent |
| 4 | $n_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{2}/V_{1})$ | Voltage ratio → dB | Electronics section |
| 5 | $n_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{2}/P_{1})$ | Power ratio → dB | Electronics section |
| 6 | $\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}(r_{2}/r_{1})$ | Distance attenuation | Inverse square |
| 7 | $Z_{0} = \rho_{0}\, c$ | Acoustic impedance of air | $\rho_0 = 1.21\;\text{kg/m}^3,\;c = 343\;\text{m/s}$ |
| 8 | $f_{\text{octave}} = 2 \times f_{\text{prev}}$ | Octave relationship | Standard test frequencies |
| 9 | $S = 10\;\log_{10}(N)$ | Masking effectiveness | Broad-band noise |
| 10 | $\text{Threshold} = \text{mean of 3 ascents}$ | Hughson-Westlake rule | Clinical thresholding |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why These Equations Matter

- **dB SPL ↔ dB HL** conversion is essential when calibrating audiometers against a sound level meter.
- The **inverse square law** governs free-field audiometric booths (where sound-field audiometry is performed).
- The **Hughson-Westlake** bracketing rule (modified ascending method) is the universally accepted threshold-seeking algorithm in clinical audiometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Insight:** A hearing loss of more than **25 dB HL** at speech frequencies (500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz) is clinically classified as *clinically significant hearing impairment* by WHO standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Decibel Uses a Factor of 20 for Pressure

Human hearing spans an enormous dynamic range — from the faintest audible whisper (about $20\;\mu\text{Pa}$) to painful levels (about $200\;\text{Pa}$). This is a ratio of **10 million to 1** in pressure.

A linear scale would be unusable. The decibel solves this with a logarithm.

$$
\text{Start:}\quad \text{Ratio} = \frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}
$$

$$
\text{Take logarithm base 10:}\quad \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Multiply by 20 to set 0 dB SPL = 20 µPa:}\quad 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

This multiplication factor of **20 (not 10)** arises because intensity $\propto$ pressure², so:

$$
L_{I} = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}}) = 10 \log_{10}(p^2/p_{\text{ref}}^{2}) = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Square Law in Decibel Form

Consider a point source radiating sound uniformly in all directions. The acoustic power $P$ crossing any sphere around the source is constant:

$$
I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}
$$

Comparing two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$
\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} = \left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}
$$

Expressing this ratio in dB:

$$
\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Frequently Asked):**

Suppose the audiometer earphone produces 80 dB SPL at the eardrum (distance ≈ 0 m reference). What would the level be if measured at 2× the original distance?

$$
\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}(1/2) = 20 \times (-0.301) = -6.02\;\text{dB}
$$

Therefore: $80 + (-6.02) = 73.98 \approx 74\;\text{dB SPL}$ at twice the distance.

### 3.3 Derivation: Threshold of Hearing Curve and RETSPL

The **Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Level (RETSPL)** is defined as the SPL value at which a *normal-hearing otologically clean young adult* (the ISO 8253-1 reference population) just barely detects a pure tone.

The standard RETSPL values (ISO 8253-1) used in audiometer calibration:

| Frequency (Hz) | RETSPL (dB SPL) for TDH-39 earphone |
|---:|---:|
| 125 | 45.0 |
| 250 | 25.5 |
| 500 | 11.5 |
| 1000 | 7.0 |
| 2000 | 9.0 |
| 4000 | 9.5 |
| 8000 | 13.0 |

**Conversion Example:**

A 2 kHz tone is presented at **40 dB HL**. What is the equivalent SPL delivered by the earphone?

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = L_{\text{HL}} + \text{RETSPL}(2000) = 40 + 9.0 = 49\;\text{dB SPL}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Hughson-Westlake Threshold Seeking Algorithm

The clinical procedure for finding the hearing threshold follows a **modified method of limits**, with a 5-dB-down / 10-dB-up bracketing rule:

1. Start at a level clearly audible (e.g., 30 dB HL).
2. If heard → decrease by **10 dB**.
3. If not heard → increase by **5 dB**.
4. The threshold is defined as the lowest level at which the subject responds on **at least 2 out of 3 ascending trials**.

$$
\text{Threshold}_{\text{HW}} = \frac{T_{1} + T_{2} + T_{3}}{3}
$$

where $T_i$ are the ascending catch-points from three bracketing cycles.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Types of Audiometers

| Type | Function | Key Components | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screening Audiometer** | Quick pass/fail test | Single oscillator, basic attenuator | Schools, industry |
| **Diagnostic Audiometer** | Full clinical evaluation | Multi-frequency oscillator, dual channels | ENT clinics |
| **Clinical Audiometer** | Air + bone conduction | 2 channels, masking generator | Hospitals |
| **Bekesy Audiometer** | Self-recording thresholds | Motor-driven attenuator, automatic plot | Diagnostic research |
| **Speech Audiometer** | Measures speech intelligibility | CD/cassette input + pure tone | Speech therapy |
| **Impedance Audiometer** | Middle-ear measurements | Probe tone + pressure pump | Middle-ear diagnosis |
| **Industrial Audiometer** | Noise-exposure monitoring | Portable, automatic | Occupational health |
| **Computerized Audiometer** | Digital signal generation | DSP processor + USB output | Modern clinics |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Air Conduction vs Bone Conduction

| Parameter | Air Conduction | Bone Conduction |
|---|---|---|
| **Transducer** | Supra-aural earphone (TDH-39) | Bone vibrator (B-71) on mastoid |
| **Pathway** | Outer → Middle → Inner ear | Skull → Cochlea directly |
| **Frequency Range** | 125 Hz – 8 kHz | 250 Hz – 4 kHz |
| **Crosstalk** | Possible | Significant (>40 dB needed) |
| **Diagnostic Use** | Tests entire auditory pathway | Isolates cochlear/neural function |
| **Calibration** | RETSPL (coupler 6 cc) | RETFL (mechanical coupler) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Logic for KTU:** Air conduction tells you *whether* hearing loss exists. Bone conduction tells you *where* the loss is located (conductive vs sensorineural).

### 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (Board Exam Style)

**Question:** A pure-tone audiometer delivers a 1 kHz tone at 50 dB HL through a TDH-39 earphone. Calculate the equivalent SPL delivered to the eardrum and the acoustic pressure amplitude.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Look up RETSPL at 1 kHz (from Table above):
$$
\text{RETSPL}(1000) = 7.0\;\text{dB SPL}
$$

Step 2 — Compute equivalent SPL:
$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = L_{\text{HL}} + \text{RETSPL} = 50 + 7.0 = 57\;\text{dB SPL}
$$

Step 3 — Convert dB SPL to absolute pressure $p$:
$$
57 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{57}{20} = 2.85
$$

$$
p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{2.85} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 707.9 \approx 0.01416\;\text{Pa}
$$

Step 4 — Verify (alternate path):
$$
10^{57/20} = 10^{2.85} \approx 707.9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p \approx 0.0142\;\text{Pa} \approx 14.2\;\text{mPa}
$$

The acoustic pressure at the eardrum is **~14.2 mPa**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Clinical Audiometer (Functional Architecture)

This block diagram shows the major subsystems of a dual-channel clinical audiometer, illustrating how a pure-tone stimulus is generated, attenuated, routed, and presented to the patient.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph O1[OSCILLATOR 1 RIGHT EAR]
        oscA[Oscillator 250 to 8000 Hz]
        attA[Attenuator 0 to 120 dB HL]
        swA[Channel A Switch]
    end

    subgraph O2[OSCILLATOR 2 LEFT EAR]
        oscB[Oscillator 250 to 8000 Hz]
        attB[Attenuator 0 to 120 dB HL]
        swB[Channel B Switch]
    end

    subgraph MG[MASKING GENERATOR]
        noise[Wide Band Noise Source]
        attM[Masking Attenuator]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT TRANSDUCERS]
        earL[Left Earphone TDH 39]
        earR[Right Earphone TDH 39]
        bone[Bone Vibrator B 71]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL AND LOGIC]
        respBtn[Patient Response Button]
        display[Digital Display and Audiogram Plotter]
        mic[Clinician Microphone for Talk Over]
    end

    oscA --> attA --> swA
    oscB --> attB --> swB
    noise --> attM --> swA
    noise --> attM --> swB
    swA --> earR
    swB --> earL
    swA --> bone
    swB --> bone
    respBtn --> display
    mic --> earL
    mic --> earR
    attA --> display
    attB --> display
```

### 4.2 Operational Flowchart — Audiometric Threshold Testing (Hughson-Westlake)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Test]) --> selFreq[Select Test Frequency]
    selFreq --> setStart[Set Initial Level 30 dB HL]
    setStart --> present[Present Tone 1 to 2 sec]
    present --> resp{Patient Responds?}
    resp -- YES --> dec10[Decrease by 10 dB]
    dec10 --> present
    resp -- NO --> inc5[Increase by 5 dB]
    inc5 --> present
    present --> count{3 Ascents Completed?}
    count -- NO --> present
    count -- YES --> calc[Compute Threshold as Mean of 3 Ascents]
    calc --> nextFreq[Move to Next Frequency]
    nextFreq --> done{All Frequencies Tested?}
    done -- NO --> selFreq
    done -- YES --> plot[Plot Audiogram]
    plot([End Test])
```

### 4.3 Audiometer Test Setup Diagram (Booth Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOOTH[SINGLE WALL SOUND ISOLATED BOOTH]
        patient[Patient Seated Inside Booth]
        earL[Left Earphone]
        earR[Right Earphone]
        boneV[Bone Vibrator on Mastoid]
        resp[Patient Response Button]
    end

    subgraph OUTSIDE[CONTROL ROOM OUTSIDE BOOTH]
        audiometer[Clinical Audiometer Unit]
        pc[Computer with Audiometry Software]
        printer[Printer for Audiogram]
    end

    patient --- earL
    patient --- earR
    patient --- boneV
    patient --- resp

    earL -. cable .-> audiometer
    earR -. cable .-> audiometer
    boneV -. cable .-> audiometer
    resp -. cable .-> audiometer

    audiometer --- pc
    pc --- printer
```

### 4.4 Diagnostic Decision Tree — Type of Hearing Loss

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    test[Perform AC and BC Audiometry] --> q1{AC Threshold Elevated?}
    q1 -- NO --> normal[Normal Hearing]
    q1 -- YES --> q2{BC Threshold Also Elevated?}
    q2 -- NO --> conduct[Conductive Hearing Loss - Middle or Outer Ear Problem]
    q2 -- YES --> q3{Air Bone Gap Exists?}
    q3 -- YES --> mixed[Mixed Hearing Loss]
    q3 -- NO --> sensor[Sensorineural Hearing Loss - Cochlea or Auditory Nerve Problem]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **AC = Air Conduction** (earphone route)
> - **BC = Bone Conduction** (skull vibration route)
> - **Air-Bone Gap (ABG)** = AC threshold − BC threshold; if **ABG ≥ 15 dB**, a conductive component is present.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> Define an **audiometer**. List any **four** standard test frequencies used in clinical audiometry.

**Model Answer:**

An audiometer is an electroacoustic instrument used to measure the hearing sensitivity of an individual by determining the softest level (threshold) at which pure tones of various frequencies can be heard.

The four standard clinical test frequencies are:

$$
f = \{125,\; 250,\; 500,\; 1000,\; 2000,\; 4000,\; 8000\}\;\text{Hz}
$$

Any four may be quoted. *(Full marks: definition 2 marks + any four frequencies 1 mark)*

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> Differentiate between **dB SPL** and **dB HL**.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | dB SPL | dB HL |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Sound Pressure Level | Hearing Level |
| **Reference** | $20\;\mu\text{Pa}$ (absolute) | Average normal hearing threshold at that frequency |
| **Frequency Dependent?** | No (absolute physical quantity) | Yes (different reference at each frequency) |
| **Clinical Use** | Physical sound measurement | Direct hearing assessment |
| **Conversion** | — | $\text{dB HL} = \text{dB SPL} - \text{RETSPL}(f)$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **clinical pure-tone audiometer**. Discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **masking**? Why is it required during bone-conduction audiometry? Explain with an example. **(7 marks)**

##### Solution for (a):

A clinical pure-tone audiometer consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Oscillator (Tone Generator):** Generates pure sinusoidal tones over the clinical frequency range of **125 Hz – 8 kHz**, octave-spaced. A dual audiometer has two independent oscillators (Channel A and Channel B) so that different tones can be sent to each ear simultaneously.

2. **Attenuator:** A precision step-attenuator controlled by the clinician sets the output intensity in steps of **5 dB** (or **1 dB** for research) over the range **-10 dB HL to 120 dB HL**. The attenuator must have very fine resolution because clinical decisions hinge on small dB differences.

3. **Channel Selector Switch:** Routes the tone from either Channel A or Channel B to the appropriate output transducer (left ear, right ear, or bone vibrator).

4. **Output Transducers:**
   - **TDH-39 Supra-aural earphones** for air conduction (frequency 125 Hz – 8 kHz)
   - **B-71 Bone vibrator** for bone conduction (placed on the mastoid process; frequency 250 Hz – 4 kHz)

5. **Patient Response Button:** The patient presses a button the moment a tone is just barely heard. This signal is fed to a digital display that records the threshold level.

6. **Masking Generator:** A wide-band white noise source used to mask the non-test ear during bone conduction or when the interaural attenuation (~40 dB) is exceeded.

7. **Display / Audiogram Plotter:** A digital screen or paper plotter on which the audiogram (frequency vs. hearing threshold in dB HL) is automatically plotted.

The clinician follows the **Hughson-Westlake procedure**: starting at a clearly audible level (e.g., 30 dB HL), tone intensity is decreased in 10-dB steps and increased in 5-dB steps until the threshold is bracketed. The threshold is the lowest level heard on 2 of 3 ascending trials.

*(Block diagram to be drawn — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

##### Solution for (b):

**Masking** is the process of presenting a controlled noise (usually wide-band white noise) to the **non-test ear** during audiometry to prevent it from participating in the response, thereby ensuring that the threshold measured actually corresponds to the test ear only.

**Why masking is required in bone conduction:**

During bone conduction audiometry, the bone vibrator placed on the mastoid of the test ear delivers vibrations that travel *through the skull* and can be heard by the **contralateral (non-test) cochlea** as well, because the skull transmits vibration efficiently to both ears. This phenomenon is called **cross-hearing** or **crossover**.

Since the **interaural attenuation for bone conduction is only 0–10 dB** (compared to 40 dB for air conduction), virtually any bone-conducted tone presented at threshold levels can be heard by both ears. Without masking, the clinician cannot tell *which cochlea* is actually responding, leading to **false (shadow) thresholds**.

**Example:**

Suppose the patient's right ear has normal hearing (0 dB HL) and left ear has severe sensorineural loss (70 dB HL). During bone conduction testing of the left ear, the tone at 50 dB HL may actually be heard by the *right* cochlea through skull vibration. The clinician would then erroneously conclude that the left cochlea can hear at 50 dB HL — which is the "**shadow threshold**" of the right ear.

To prevent this, **narrow-band masking noise** is delivered to the right (non-test) ear at an appropriate level (e.g., 30–40 dB above its air-conduction threshold). This "busies" the right cochlea with noise, ensuring only the left cochlea responds to the test tone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - Students often forget that **interaural attenuation for air conduction is ~40 dB**, while for **bone conduction it is only 0–10 dB**. This is why masking is *almost always* required for bone conduction but only selectively for air conduction.
> - A common error is to over-mask. The masking noise itself can cross over and disturb the test ear. The safe masking rule: keep masking level at **test ear threshold + interaural attenuation**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **(a)** Explain the **Hughson-Westlake method** of threshold determination. Why is the threshold defined at 50% correct response? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An audiometer delivers a **2 kHz tone at 60 dB HL**. Calculate the equivalent **SPL delivered to the ear** and the **acoustic pressure amplitude**. Given RETSPL at 2 kHz = 9 dB SPL. **(7 marks)**

##### Solution for (a):

The **Hughson-Westlake procedure** (also called the *modified method of limits* or *ascending method*) is the internationally standardized psychophysical procedure for finding hearing thresholds, defined in **ISO 8253-1**.

**Procedure:**

1. **Familiarization Phase:** A tone is presented at a clearly audible level (e.g., 40 dB HL) so the subject understands the task.

2. **Initial Descent:** The level is decreased in **10-dB steps** until the subject no longer responds.

3. **Ascending Trials:** The level is then increased in **5-dB steps** until the subject responds again. This level is recorded as the **first ascending catch-point**.

4. **Bracketing:** Steps 2 and 3 are repeated to obtain at least **two more ascending catch-points** at the same frequency.

5. **Threshold Definition:** The threshold is defined as the **lowest level at which the subject responds on at least 2 out of 3 ascending trials**, or equivalently the **mean of three ascending catch-points**.

$$
T_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{T_{1} + T_{2} + T_{3}}{3}
$$

**Why 50% correct response?**

The hearing threshold is a *statistical/psychophysical* concept — a tone near threshold is heard *intermittently* because the underlying neural firing is probabilistic. By defining threshold as the level giving approximately **50% positive responses**, the procedure:

- Eliminates *lucky guesses* (which would falsely lower the threshold).
- Avoids *missed detections* due to inattention (which would falsely elevate it).
- Provides a **reproducible, statistically unbiased** measure of the subject's true perceptual limit.
- Aligns with classical psychophysical theory (the *threshold of detectability* in signal-detection theory).

The 5-dB step size gives sufficient resolution while keeping test time practical.

##### Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Frequency $f = 2\;\text{kHz}$
- Hearing Level $L_{\text{HL}} = 60\;\text{dB HL}$
- Reference Threshold: $\text{RETSPL}(2000) = 9\;\text{dB SPL}$

**Step 1 — Convert HL to SPL:**

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = L_{\text{HL}} + \text{RETSPL}(f)
$$

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = 60 + 9 = 69\;\text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert SPL to absolute pressure:**

From the dB SPL formula:

$$
L_{\text{SPL}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

$$
69 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{69}{20} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
3.45 = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{p}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{3.45} = 2818.4
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for pressure $p$:**

$$
p = 2818.4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 56368 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0564\;\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{p \approx 0.0564\;\text{Pa} = 56.4\;\text{mPa}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (alternate path):**

If $L_{\text{SPL}} = 69$, then power ratio $= 10^{6.9} \approx 7.94 \times 10^{6}$.

Intensity $I = 7.94 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-12} = 7.94 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{W/m}^2$.

Pressure $p = \sqrt{I \cdot Z_0} = \sqrt{7.94 \times 10^{-6} \times 415} \approx \sqrt{3.3 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0574\;\text{Pa}$ ✓ (matches within rounding).

**Final Answer:** Sound Pressure Level = **69 dB SPL**, Acoustic Pressure = **0.0564 Pa**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing dB HL with dB SPL** — many students add RETSPL incorrectly or forget the reference is frequency-dependent.
> 2. **Wrong factor (10 vs 20):** Using $10 \log_{10}$ instead of $20 \log_{10}$ for pressure gives an answer off by 100× — a classic mistake.
> 3. **Wrong reference pressure:** Some students use $0.0002$ dyn/cm² (cgs) or $1\;\mu\text{Pa}$ (underwater acoustics) instead of the audiometric standard $20\;\mu\text{Pa}$.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** Always write **Pa** or **mPa**, not just the number.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Audiometers**

- **Definition:** Audiometer = electroacoustic instrument measuring hearing threshold across standard frequencies.

- **Standard Test Frequencies (memorize):** 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k Hz (octave-spaced).

- **Two Key Scales:**
  - **dB SPL** = absolute pressure, reference $20\;\mu\text{Pa}$.
  - **dB HL** = clinical scale, frequency-dependent RETSPL reference.
  - Conversion: $\text{dB SPL} = \text{dB HL} + \text{RETSPL}(f)$.

- **Reference Pressure:** $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\;\mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 kHz for normal hearing threshold = **0 dB SPL**.

- **Major Blocks of an Audiometer:** Oscillator → Attenuator → Channel Switch → Output Transducer (earphone/bone vibrator) → Patient response button → Display/Audiogram.

- **Two Transducers:**
  - **TDH-39 earphone** (air conduction, 125 Hz – 8 kHz)
  - **B-71 bone vibrator** (bone conduction, 250 Hz – 4 kHz, placed on mastoid)

- **Hughson-Westlake Method:** 10-dB descent, 5-dB ascent, threshold = mean of 3 ascending catches; ≥ 2 of 3 positive responses.

- **Masking:** Required for bone conduction (interaural attenuation only ~0–10 dB) and for air conduction when level > 40 dB above non-test ear threshold.

- **Air-Bone Gap (ABG):** $\text{ABG} = \text{AC} - \text{BC}$ threshold. ABG ≥ 15 dB ⇒ conductive component present.

- **Three Types of Hearing Loss (Diagnostic Logic):**
  - **Conductive:** AC elevated, BC normal → outer/middle ear.
  - **Sensorineural:** AC and BC both equally elevated → cochlea/nerve.
  - **Mixed:** Both AC and BC elevated, but AC > BC.

- **Inverse Square Law:** Doubling the distance reduces SPL by **6 dB**.

- **Numerical Formula Anchor:**
  - $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_{\text{ref}})$
  - $L_{\text{SPL}} = L_{\text{HL}} + \text{RETSPL}(f)$

- **Calibration Standards:** ISO 8253-1, IEC 60645-1, ANSI S3.6.

- **One-line Memory Hook:** *An audiometer is to hearing what a blood pressure cuff is to the heart — a calibrated psycho-physical measurement tool.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pure Tone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pure Tone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Tone (PT):** A sound wave consisting of a **single sinusoidal frequency** with constant amplitude and phase, containing no harmonic content or overtones. In the context of **Analytical & Diagnostic Equipments**, a *Pure Tone Audiometer* is an electroacoustic instrument that generates calibrated pure-tone stimuli at specific frequencies (typically 125 Hz to 8 kHz) and variable intensities to determine the **hearing threshold** of a subject across the audible frequency spectrum.

In audiological diagnostics, a pure tone is mathematically represented as:

$$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

Where:
- $A$ = Peak amplitude (related to Sound Pressure Level)
- $f$ = Frequency in Hertz (Hz)
- $\phi$ = Phase angle in radians
- $t$ = Time in seconds

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The pure tone audiometer is the **gold standard** clinical instrument for quantitative assessment of hearing sensitivity. It is the foundational equipment studied under *Module 4: Audiological Diagnostic Equipment*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a piano. When you strike **only the middle C key**, you hear a single, clean musical note with no other notes mixed in — that is essentially a **pure tone**. It is "pure" because it contains energy at only **one frequency**, unlike everyday sounds (speech, traffic, music) which are a chaotic mixture of thousands of frequencies superimposed together.

Now, think of a doctor trying to find out *which specific frequencies* your ear can no longer hear well. A pure tone audiometer is like a **"frequency flashlight"** — it shines (plays) one single pitch at a time at varying loudness levels, and the patient signals when they can just barely hear it. By sweeping across all frequencies, the audiologist builds a complete map of the patient's hearing ability — called an **audiogram**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standards for Pure Tone Audiometry:**
> - **ISO 8253-1** — Acoustics: Audiometric test methods
> - **ANSI S3.21** — American National Standard Methods for Manual Pure-Tone Threshold Audiometry
> - **IEC 60645-1** — Pure-tone audiometers (equipment specification)

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following table lists the **standard physical metrics** used in pure tone audiometry that KTU expects students to memorize:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference Sound Pressure | $p_0$ | **20 µPa** | Pascal |
| Audible Frequency Range | $f$ | **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz** | Hertz |
| Clinical Audiometric Range | $f$ | **125 Hz – 8,000 Hz** | Hertz |
| Threshold of Hearing (SPL) | $L_{ref}$ | **0 dB SPL** | Decibel |
| Normal Hearing Level | HL | **0 dB HL** | Decibel |
| Standard Octave Frequencies | $f$ | **125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k** | Hz |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Decibel in Audiometry

Audiometry uses the **decibel (dB)** as its primary measurement unit because human hearing spans an enormous range of sound pressures (from 20 µPa to 20 Pa — a million-fold ratio). The decibel is a **logarithmic ratio** that compresses this range into manageable numbers.

### 2.1.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

The Sound Pressure Level expresses the ratio of measured sound pressure to the standard reference pressure of **20 µPa** (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz):

$$L_{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB}$$

Where:
- $p_{rms}$ = Root-mean-square sound pressure of the stimulus (Pa)
- $p_0$ = Reference sound pressure = **20 µPa** = $20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa
- $\log_{10}$ = Base-10 logarithm

### 2.1.2 Hearing Level (HL)

Since the human ear is **not equally sensitive** at all frequencies (it is most sensitive around 1–4 kHz), clinicians use the **Hearing Level (HL)** scale, which is **frequency-specific**. At each audiometric frequency, **0 dB HL** is defined as the **median threshold of hearing** for a large population of young, otologically normal adults (18–25 years).

The conversion between dB HL and dB SPL is:

$$L_{HL} = L_{SPL} - RETSPL$$

Where **RETSPL** = **Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Level** (a tabulated, frequency-specific value).

### 2.1.3 RETSPL Values (Critical for KTU Numericals)

| Frequency (Hz) | RETSPL — TDH-39 Headphone (dB SPL) |
|---|---|
| 125 | 45.0 |
| 250 | 25.5 |
| 500 | 11.5 |
| 1000 | 7.0 |
| 2000 | 9.0 |
| 4000 | 9.5 |
| 8000 | 13.0 |

---

## 2.2 Pure Tone Audiometer — Block Architecture & Operating Principle

A pure tone audiometer is built from the following functional blocks (each is a high-yield exam topic):

- **Oscillator (Signal Generator):** Produces a sinusoidal waveform at selectable audiometric frequencies (125 Hz, 250 Hz, 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz, 8 kHz). Modern digital audiometers use **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** chips.
- **Attenuator (Intensity Control):** A precision logarithmic attenuator (typically in 5 dB steps, with a 1 dB vernier) controls output intensity from -10 dB HL to +120 dB HL.
- **Output Selector (Transducer Switch):** Routes the signal to either the **air-conduction headphone (TDH-39 / ER-3A insert)**, **bone-conduction vibrator (B-71)**, or **sound-field speaker**.
- **Masking Noise Generator:** Produces **narrowband noise** centered on the test frequency for contralateral masking.
- **Patient Response Button (Hand Switch):** The patient presses this button the moment a tone is heard, allowing the audiologist to determine threshold via the **Hughson-Westlake procedure**.
- **Calibration Reference:** Internal calibration tables store RETSPL values for each transducer.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for KTU university exam questions on pure tone audiometry. Memorize these thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | Equation of a pure tone | Pa·s |
| 2 | $L_{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level (reference: 20 µPa) | dB SPL |
| 3 | $L_{HL} = L_{SPL} - RETSPL(f)$ | Hearing Level (frequency-corrected) | dB HL |
| 4 | $L_{SPL} = L_{HL} + RETSPL(f)$ | Inverse conversion (HL → SPL) | dB SPL |
| 5 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level (reference: $10^{-12}$ W/m²) | dB |
| 6 | $\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$ | Difference in level between two SPLs | dB |
| 7 | $f_2 = 2 f_1$ | Octave relationship (definition) | Hz |
| 8 | $HL_{avg} = \dfrac{\Sigma HL_i}{n}$ | Pure Tone Average (PTA) at 0.5, 1, 2, 4 kHz | dB HL |
| 9 | Threshold via **Hughson–Westlake (10-down, 5-up)** | 10 dB decrease on no response, 5 dB increase on response; threshold = lowest level with 2 of 3 responses | — |
| 10 | $f_{mask} = f_{test} \pm \dfrac{1}{2} \text{octave}$ | Narrowband mask center frequency | Hz |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Audiology:** Primary tool for diagnosing sensorineural, conductive, and mixed hearing loss.
- **Occupational Health:** Mandatory hearing-conservation screening for industrial workers (OSHA, Noise Pollution Rules India 2000).
- **Pediatric Screening:** Used in school-based and neonatal hearing-screening programs (with modifications like conditioned play audiometry).
- **ENT Practice:** Routine pre-operative baseline documentation.
- **Forensic Audiology:** Legal assessment of noise-induced hearing loss for compensation claims.
- **Cochlear Implant Mapping:** Pre-implant audiometric profiling is a prerequisite for implant candidacy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Sound Pressure to dB SPL

**Given:** A pure-tone stimulus produces an RMS sound pressure of $p_{rms} = 0.1$ Pa at the listener's eardrum.

**Find:** The corresponding Sound Pressure Level in dB SPL.

### Step 1 — Write the defining formula

$$L_{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB}$$

### Step 2 — Identify and substitute the reference pressure

The standard reference pressure for sound in air is $p_0 = 20 \text{ µPa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Pa}$. Substituting:

$$L_{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.1}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the ratio

$$L_{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.1}{0.00002}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(5000)$$

### Step 4 — Compute the logarithm

$$L_{SPL} = 20 \times 3.69897 = 73.98 \text{ dB SPL}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{L_{SPL} \approx 74 \text{ dB SPL}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always keep $p_0 = 20 \text{ µPa}$ in SI units. Forgetting this is the #1 reason students lose marks on dB conversion problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion of dB HL to dB SPL (KTU-Favorite Numerical)

**Given:** A pure tone of **1 kHz** is presented at an intensity of **40 dB HL** through a TDH-39 supra-aural headphone.

**Find:** The corresponding Sound Pressure Level in dB SPL.

### Step 1 — Recall the conversion formula

$$L_{SPL} = L_{HL} + RETSPL(f)$$

### Step 2 — Look up the RETSPL for 1 kHz (TDH-39)

From the standard RETSPL table: $RETSPL(1000 \text{ Hz}) = 7.0 \text{ dB SPL}$.

### Step 3 — Substitute the values

$$L_{SPL} = 40 + 7.0 = 47.0 \text{ dB SPL}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{L_{SPL} = 47 \text{ dB SPL}}$$

**Interpretation:** Although the audiometer dial reads "40", the actual physical sound pressure at the eardrum corresponds to 47 dB SPL — because the human ear is more sensitive at 1 kHz than at the 0 dB SPL reference threshold.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pure Tone Average (PTA) Calculation

**Given:** A patient's air-conduction thresholds are tabulated as follows (Right Ear):

| Frequency (Hz) | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold (dB HL) | 30 | 35 | 40 | 45 |

**Find:** The Pure Tone Average (PTA) — the standard clinical index for grading hearing loss.

### Step 1 — Apply the PTA formula

$$PTA = \frac{HL_{500} + HL_{1000} + HL_{2000} + HL_{4000}}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$PTA = \frac{30 + 35 + 40 + 45}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Sum the numerator

$$PTA = \frac{150}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Final division

$$\boxed{PTA = 37.5 \text{ dB HL}}$$

**Clinical Interpretation (using WHO grading):**

| PTA (dB HL) | Degree of Hearing Loss |
|---|---|
| ≤ 25 | Normal |
| 26 – 40 | Mild |
| 41 – 55 | Moderate |
| 56 – 70 | Moderately Severe |
| 71 – 90 | Severe |
| > 90 | Profound |

> This patient (PTA = 37.5) falls in the **Mild** category.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Determining SPL When Intensity Level Doubles

**Given:** A tone has an intensity level of $L_1 = 60$ dB. The intensity is **doubled** ($I_2 = 2 I_1$).

**Find:** The new intensity level $L_2$.

### Step 1 — Write the general difference formula

$$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $I_2 = 2 I_1$

$$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(2)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$\Delta L = 10 \times 0.30103 = 3.01 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4 — Add to the original level

$$L_2 = L_1 + \Delta L = 60 + 3.01 = 63.01 \text{ dB}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{L_2 \approx 63 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A 3 dB increase in level represents a **doubling of acoustic intensity (power)**, but a **doubling of perceived loudness** requires approximately a **10 dB** increase. This distinction is frequently tested.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Air Conduction vs Bone Conduction

This matrix is a **guaranteed question** in KTU university exams. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Air Conduction (AC) | Bone Conduction (BC) |
|---|---|---|
| Transducer | Supra-aural headphone (TDH-39) or insert earphone (ER-3A) | Bone vibrator (B-71) placed on mastoid or forehead |
| Pathway | Outer ear → Middle ear → Cochlea → Auditory nerve | Skull bone vibration → Cochlea directly (bypasses outer & middle ear) |
| Tests | Entire auditory system | Inner ear (cochlea) and auditory nerve only |
| Frequency Range | 125 Hz – 8 kHz | 250 Hz – 4 kHz (limited above 4 kHz due to vibrator artifacts) |
| Max Output | Up to 120 dB HL | Up to 70–80 dB HL (limited by vibrator power) |
| RETSPL | Freq-dependent, calibrated for TDH-39 | Freq-dependent, calibrated for B-71 (e.g., 0 dB HL at 1 kHz ≈ 50 dB SPL at mastoid) |
| Air–Bone Gap | AC threshold − BC threshold; **> 10 dB** indicates conductive component |
| Clinical Use | Screening & full diagnostic | Differentiates conductive vs sensorineural loss |
| Calibration Std | ISO 389-1 / ANSI S3.6 | ISO 389-3 / ANSI S3.6 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pure Tone Audiometer Signal Flow

The following **Mermaid functional block diagram** illustrates the complete signal chain inside a clinical pure tone audiometer, from oscillator to patient transducer. This is a frequently asked KTU drawing question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osc1[Oscillator DDS 125Hz to 8kHz]
    atten1[Attenuator -10 to 120 dB HL 5 dB step]
    out1[Output Selector]
    air[Air Conduction Headphone TDH39 or ER3A]
    bone[Bone Conduction Vibrator B71 placed on mastoid]
    spk[Free Field Loudspeaker]
    mask[Masking Noise Generator Narrowband]
    rsp[Patient Response Button Hand Switch]
    disp[HUD Display Threshold Storage]
    cal[Calibration EEPROM RETSPL Table]
    osc1 --> atten1
    atten1 --> out1
    cal -.-> atten1
    out1 --> air
    out1 --> bone
    out1 --> spk
    mask --> out1
    rsp --> disp
    atten1 --> disp
```

**Working Principle (mapped to the diagram above):**

1. The **Oscillator (DDS)** generates a precise sinusoidal voltage at one of the standard audiometric frequencies.
2. The **Attenuator** adjusts this voltage logarithmically to produce the desired hearing level (in 5 dB steps, with finer control possible).
3. The **Output Selector** routes the attenuated signal to one of the three transducers (headphone, bone vibrator, or loudspeaker).
4. The **Masking Noise Generator**, when activated, superimposes narrowband noise on the contralateral ear to prevent "cross-hearing."
5. The **Patient Response Button** captures the patient's behavioral response and feeds it to the **HUD Display**, which automatically stores and displays the threshold.
6. The **Calibration EEPROM** continuously supplies the RETSPL correction values to the attenuator, ensuring that the dial reading in dB HL matches the actual delivered SPL.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hughson–Westlake Threshold-Seeking Algorithm

The Hughson–Westlake (or **"10-down, 5-up"**) procedure is the **internationally standardized** psychophysical method for finding the hearing threshold. KTU questions often ask for its description or flowchart.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Test at Familiarization Level 30 dB HL])
    pres1[Present Tone for 1 to 2 seconds]
    q1{Patient Responds?}
    yes1[Decrease Level by 10 dB]
    no1[Increase Level by 5 dB]
    next1[Move to Next Frequency]
    thr{2 of 3 Responses at Same Level?}
    rec[Record Threshold]
    stop([Threshold Recorded])
    start --> pres1
    pres1 --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| yes1
    q1 -->|No| no1
    yes1 --> pres1
    no1 --> pres1
    pres1 -.-> next1
    next1 --> thr
    thr -->|Yes| rec
    rec --> stop
```

**Algorithm Logic Explanation:**

- **Start at 30 dB HL** as a familiarization stimulus (adjustable based on initial patient response).
- **If the patient hears it** → decrease the level by **10 dB** (move toward silence).
- **If the patient does NOT hear it** → increase the level by **5 dB** (move toward audibility).
- The **smaller ascent (5 dB) and larger descent (10 dB)** asymmetry biases the procedure toward finding the *lowest audible level*.
- **Threshold = the lowest level at which the patient responds in 2 out of 3 presentations.**
- This is then repeated for every test frequency and both ears.

---

## 4.3 Audiogram Interpretation Schematic

The audiogram is a **graphical record** of hearing thresholds. Below is a schematic of how the audiogram is plotted.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AUD[Audiogram Plot]
        yaxis[Vertical Axis: Hearing Level dB HL]
        xaxis[Horizontal Axis: Frequency Hz logarithmic 125 to 8000]
        xnote[Top of Chart = Better Hearing 0 dB HL]
        ynote[Bottom of Chart = Worse Hearing 120 dB HL]
    end
    subgraph SYM[Standard Symbols]
        s1[Right Ear Air Conduction: Red Circle O]
        s2[Left Ear Air Conduction: Blue Cross X]
        s3[Right Ear Bone Conduction: Red Bracket]
        s4[Left Ear Bone Conduction: Blue Bracket]
    end
    AUD --- SYM
```

**Reading the Audiogram:**

- The **X-axis (frequency)** is logarithmic, with standard octaves marked at 125, 250, 500, 1 k, 2 k, 4 k, 8 k Hz.
- The **Y-axis (intensity)** runs from **−10 dB HL** at the top to **120 dB HL** at the bottom.
- A line connecting the thresholds is the **audiogram curve**.
- **Air-bone gap (ABG)** = AC threshold − BC threshold at the same frequency.
  - **ABG ≤ 10 dB** → sensorineural loss (or normal)
  - **ABG > 10 dB** → conductive or mixed loss
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define pure tone. Why is it used as the stimulus in clinical audiometry?**

**Model Answer:**

A *pure tone* is a sound wave with energy concentrated at a **single sinusoidal frequency**, with no harmonic or overtones. Mathematically, it is described by $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$.

It is used as the standard audiometric stimulus because:

1. **Frequency-specific diagnosis:** A pure tone isolates one frequency, allowing the audiologist to map hearing sensitivity *independently* at each frequency.
2. **Mathematical simplicity:** As a single sinusoid, its physical parameters (frequency, amplitude) are unambiguous and easy to calibrate.
3. **Psychophysical clarity:** Patients give unambiguous yes/no responses, enabling the **Hughson–Westlake** threshold-seeking algorithm.
4. **International standardization:** RETSPL tables exist for pure tones, making cross-clinic, cross-country comparisons valid.

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 key reasons)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is RETSPL? Why is it frequency-dependent?**

**Model Answer:**

**RETSPL (Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Level)** is the SPL value (in dB SPL) that corresponds to **0 dB HL** at a given audiometric frequency, for a specified transducer (e.g., TDH-39 headphone).

It is **frequency-dependent** because the human ear is **not uniformly sensitive** across the audible spectrum. The ear is most sensitive in the **1–4 kHz** speech range, and less sensitive at low and very high frequencies. Therefore:

- At **1 kHz**, 0 dB HL ≈ 7 dB SPL (low RETSPL — ear is sensitive).
- At **125 Hz**, 0 dB HL ≈ 45 dB SPL (high RETSPL — ear needs more sound pressure to perceive the tone).

This frequency correction is essential for converting physical SPL into clinically meaningful *hearing level* values.

**(3 marks: 1 RETSPL definition + 1 frequency-dependence reason + 1 example)**

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Both are full-mark (14) questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a clinical pure tone audiometer. List the standard audiometric test frequencies. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (As drawn in Section 4.1 above)

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **Oscillator (Signal Generator):** Generates a pure sinusoidal voltage at one of the standard test frequencies — **125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, and 8000 Hz**. Modern audiometers use **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** for high frequency accuracy and stability.

2. **Attenuator:** A precision logarithmic attenuator that adjusts the output in **5 dB steps** over a range from **−10 dB HL to +120 dB HL**. A 1 dB or 2 dB vernier is often provided. The attenuator must be accurately calibrated against RETSPL values stored in firmware.

3. **Output Selector Switch:** Routes the tone to one of three transducers:
   - **Air-conduction headphone (TDH-39 / ER-3A insert)**
   - **Bone-conduction vibrator (B-71)**
   - **Loudspeaker** (for sound-field testing)

4. **Masking Noise Generator:** Produces **narrowband noise** centered on the test frequency, applied to the *non-test* (contralateral) ear to prevent cross-hearing during BC or high-level AC testing.

5. **Patient Response Switch (Hand Switch):** A two-button toggle the patient presses when they hear (and release when they don't) the tone. Used in **Hughson–Westlake** procedure.

6. **HUD/Display & Memory:** Displays the current frequency, level, and the recorded threshold for each ear.

**Standard Test Frequencies (ISO 8253-1):** 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000 Hz (with 3000 and 6000 Hz optional).

**(7 marks: 1 diagram + 4 for block description + 1 for frequencies + 1 for standard)**

---

**(b)** Define **Hearing Threshold**. Describe the **Hughson–Westlake** procedure with a flowchart. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Hearing Threshold:** The *minimum sound pressure level* (or hearing level) at which a specified pure tone is detected by an individual in **50% of presentations** (i.e., on at least half of the trials) under standardized test conditions.

**Hughson–Westlake Procedure ("10-down, 5-up" technique):**

1. **Familiarization:** Begin with a clearly audible tone (e.g., 30 dB HL) at the test frequency.
2. **Response-driven step size:**
   - If patient **responds** → reduce level by **10 dB** (descend toward silence).
   - If patient **does not respond** → increase level by **5 dB** (ascend toward audibility).
3. **Repeat the sequence** until the level stabilizes.
4. **Threshold Definition:** The threshold is the **lowest level at which the patient responds correctly in 2 out of 3 presentations** at the same intensity.
5. **Cross-check:** Threshold is re-confirmed by an ascending trial.
6. The procedure is repeated for **all test frequencies** in both ears.

**Significance:** The 10-down / 5-up asymmetric staircase converges on the **50% detection point** — the true psychophysical threshold — while minimizing patient fatigue and false-positive responses.

**Flowchart:** (As drawn in Section 4.2 above)

**(7 marks: 1 definition + 4 procedure steps + 1 significance + 1 flowchart)**

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **difference between dB SPL and dB HL**. Convert **30 dB HL at 2 kHz** to dB SPL using the TDH-39 RETSPL table. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Difference between dB SPL and dB HL:**

| Aspect | dB SPL | dB HL |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | $p_0 = 20$ µPa (physical, fixed) | Population median threshold (frequency-specific) |
| Dependence on frequency? | No (purely physical unit) | Yes (RET SPL subtracted) |
| Reflects | Actual physical sound pressure | **Perceived hearing level** relative to "normal" |
| Use | Engineering, acoustics, noise measurement | **Clinical audiology** |

**Conversion Calculation — 30 dB HL at 2 kHz:**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$L_{SPL} = L_{HL} + RETSPL(f)$$

**Step 2 — Look up RETSPL for 2 kHz (TDH-39):**

$$RETSPL(2000 \text{ Hz}) = 9.0 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve:**

$$L_{SPL} = 30 + 9.0 = 39.0 \text{ dB SPL}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_{SPL} = 39 \text{ dB SPL}}$

**Interpretation:** A tone set at 30 dB HL at 2 kHz on the audiometer dial produces a physical sound pressure level of 39 dB SPL at the eardrum. Because the human ear is *less* sensitive at 2 kHz than at 1 kHz (RETSPL is higher), the same HL reading corresponds to a higher SPL.

**(7 marks: 3 for dB SPL vs dB HL comparison + 4 for numerical conversion)**

---

**(b)** What is the **air-bone gap (ABG)**? A patient has the following thresholds in the right ear:

| Frequency (Hz) | AC (dB HL) | BC (dB HL) |
|---|---|---|
| 500 | 50 | 15 |
| 1000 | 55 | 20 |
| 2000 | 60 | 25 |

**Calculate the ABG at each frequency and identify the type of hearing loss.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **Air-Bone Gap (ABG)** is the difference (in dB) between the air-conduction (AC) threshold and the bone-conduction (BC) threshold at a given frequency:

$$ABG(f) = AC(f) - BC(f)$$

**Step-by-step Calculation:**

**At 500 Hz:**

$$ABG(500) = 50 - 15 = 35 \text{ dB}$$

**At 1000 Hz:**

$$ABG(1000) = 55 - 20 = 35 \text{ dB}$$

**At 2000 Hz:**

$$ABG(2000) = 60 - 25 = 35 \text{ dB}$$

**Pure Tone Average (PTA) of BC thresholds** (cochlear reserve):

$$BC_{PTA} = \frac{15 + 20 + 25}{3} = 20 \text{ dB HL} \quad \text{(mild cochlear loss)}$$

**PTA of AC thresholds:**

$$AC_{PTA} = \frac{50 + 55 + 60}{3} = 55 \text{ dB HL} \quad \text{(moderately severe)}$$

**Average ABG across frequencies:**

$$ABG_{avg} = \frac{35 + 35 + 35}{3} = 35 \text{ dB}$$

**Interpretation:**

- BC threshold of 20 dB HL → mild **sensorineural** (cochlear) involvement.
- ABG of **35 dB** (significantly > 10 dB) → indicates a substantial **conductive** component.
- A significant air-bone gap combined with a measurable sensorineural loss = **Mixed Hearing Loss**.

**Final Diagnosis:** $\boxed{\text{Right Ear — Mixed Hearing Loss (Moderately Severe Conductive + Mild Sensorineural)}}$

**Recommended Next Step:** Impedance audiometry + otoscopic examination to localize the conductive pathology (e.g., ossicular chain fixation, otosclerosis, middle-ear effusion).

**(7 marks: 1 ABG definition + 3 numerical calcs + 1 PTA calcs + 1 interpretation + 1 diagnosis)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the transducer** in RETSPL problems. RETSPL for TDH-39 is *different* from ER-3A inserts. Always state which transducer you are using.
> 2. **Confusing 0 dB HL with 0 dB SPL.** A common error. They are equal *only* at 1 kHz with TDH-39 (where RETSPL = 7 dB, not 0). Remember: **0 dB HL ≠ 0 dB SPL**.
> 3. **Sign errors in ABG calculation:** Always compute AC − BC, not the other way around. A negative ABG is a red flag — recheck your values.
> 4. **PTA frequency selection:** KTU expects PTA over **0.5, 1, 2, 4 kHz** (4-frequency PTA). Including 3 kHz or excluding 4 kHz will lose marks.
> 5. **Masking rule of thumb (not always asked, but critical):** Mask the contralateral ear whenever the difference between the test-ear AC and non-test-ear BC exceeds **40 dB** (the interaural attenuation for AC is ~40 dB).
> 6. **Threshold procedure protocol:** The Hughson–Westlake result must be 2 responses out of 3 at the same level — **not** a single response. Many students write "threshold = first audible level" — this is incorrect.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pure Tone Audiometry**

- ✅ A **pure tone** is a single-frequency sinusoid — no harmonics, no overtones.
- ✅ The **reference SPL** is always $p_0 = 20$ µPa (in air).
- ✅ **0 dB HL ≠ 0 dB SPL** (they differ by RETSPL, which is frequency-dependent).
- ✅ **Standard audiometric frequencies:** 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k Hz.
- ✅ **Attenuator step size:** 5 dB standard; threshold = lowest level with 2/3 responses.
- ✅ **Hughson–Westlake algorithm:** 10-down on response, 5-up on no-response → converges on 50% detection threshold.
- ✅ **RHS = 0 dB HL** = median threshold of otologically normal young adults (ISO 389).
- ✅ **Air-Bone Gap (ABG):** AC − BC at same frequency. **ABG > 10 dB** = conductive/mixed component.
- ✅ **Sensorineural loss:** AC ≈ BC (no gap); cochlea/nerve pathology.
- ✅ **Conductive loss:** AC elevated, BC normal → large ABG; outer/middle-ear pathology.
- ✅ **Mixed loss:** Both AC and BC elevated, with significant ABG.
- ✅ **Masking:** Required when AC test ear − BC non-test ear > 40 dB.
- ✅ **PTA (Pure Tone Average):** Mean of thresholds at 0.5, 1, 2, 4 kHz — used to grade hearing loss.
- ✅ **WHO grading (PTA in dB HL):** Normal ≤ 25, Mild 26–40, Moderate 41–55, Mod-Severe 56–70, Severe 71–90, Profound > 90.
- ✅ **Calibration standards:** ISO 8253-1 (test methods), ISO 389 (RETSPL), IEC 60645-1 (equipment), ANSI S3.6 (US equivalent).
- ✅ **3 dB increase** = doubling of intensity (power); **10 dB increase** = doubling of perceived loudness.
- ✅ **Air-conduction pathway** = outer + middle + inner ear; **Bone-conduction pathway** = inner ear only (bypasses outer and middle ear).
- ✅ **Bone vibrator placement:** Mastoid process (more common) or forehead (B-71 spec).
- ✅ **Interaural attenuation:** ~40 dB for AC (headphone) — basis for the masking rule.
- ✅ **Narrowband masking noise** is centered at the test frequency (bandwidth ≈ 1/2 to 1 octave).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
