topics.title: Speech Audiometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT403 - ANALYTICAL & DIAGNOSTIC EQUIPMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Speech Audiometers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Speech Audiometer** is a specialized electroacoustic instrument used in clinical audiology to assess an individual's ability to **hear, recognize, and understand spoken language** at various intensity levels and frequencies. Unlike a pure-tone audiometer that uses sine-wave signals, the speech audiometer delivers **calibrated speech stimuli** (live voice or recorded) to evaluate the auditory system's ability to process complex, real-world sounds. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBT403 – Module 4):** Speech audiometers are explicitly listed as a key diagnostic equipment topic. Students must master the **block diagram, speech test battery (SRT, SDT, MCL, UCL, Speech Discrimination), and the difference between clinical/diagnostic/screening speech audiometers.** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a patient sitting in a quiet, soundproof chamber. Now, picture a teacher standing outside speaking words into a microphone. The teacher can choose to **whisper softly**, speak at a **normal conversational level**, or **shout loudly**. A speech audiometer is essentially that microphone + control panel + speaker system, but precisely calibrated. - The **whisper** corresponds to testing the **Speech Reception Threshold (SRT)** — the softest level at which the patient can correctly repeat 50% of two-syllable (spondaic) words like *"baseball"* or *"airplane."* - The **normal conversation level** corresponds to the **Most Comfortable Level (MCL)** — the loudness at which the patient reports speech is most comfortable to listen to. - The **shout** corresponds to the **Uncomfortable Level (UCL)** — the level at which speech becomes uncomfortably loud. So, while a pure-tone audiometer asks *"Can you hear a beep?"*, a speech audiometer asks the more clinically relevant question: *"Can you understand what someone is saying?"* ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Values) | Metric | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | Frequency Range | **125 Hz – 8000 Hz** | Speech-relevant bandwidth | | Intensity Range | **–10 dB HL to +120 dB HL** | Modern diagnostic units | | Attenuator Step Size | **1 dB or 2 dB** | Continuous or discrete | | Reference Threshold | **ANSI S3.6 / IEC 60645** | Calibration standard | | Stimulus Type | **Recorded / Live Voice** | Live voice is auditor-biased | > [!NOTE] > The dB HL (Hearing Level) is referenced to **ANSI S3.6-2010** standard thresholds for each frequency, unlike dB SPL which is an absolute physical measure. Always specify which scale is in use during analysis. ### 1.4 Types of Speech Audiometers 1. **Diagnostic Speech Audiometer** – Full-feature, calibrated, with masking; used in ENT/audiology clinics. 2. **Screening Speech Audiometer** – Simplified; checks SRT only; used in schools, industrial screening. 3. **Clinical Research Speech Audiometer** – High-precision units used in cochlear implant programming and research. 4. **Portable/Handheld Speech Audiometer** – Compact, battery-operated, used at bedside or in field. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Architecture A speech audiometer consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **Speech Signal Source** - *Live voice microphone* (electret/condenser) - *Recorded tape / CD / digital memory* (preferred for standardization) 2. **Input Selector Switch** — Routes either live voice, recorded material, or auxiliary input (CD/aux). 3. **Preamplifier** — Low-noise amplification of the speech input. 4. **VU (Volume Unit) Meter** — Visual monitoring of input level to maintain a **0 VU reference** corresponding to the calibration tone. 5. **Calibration Tone Oscillator** — Generates a **1000 Hz reference tone** used to set the VU meter to 0 dB before testing. 6. **Attenuator Network** — Precision step attenuator (1 or 2 dB steps) to control intensity presented to the patient. 7. **Output Amplifier** — Power amplifier driving the transducer. 8. **Transducer** — Headphones (TDH-39/49, ER-3A insert), bone vibrator, or free-field loudspeaker. 9. **Masking Noise Generator** — Provides contralateral masking (typically narrowband or speech-spectrum noise). ### 2.2 Speech Test Battery — The "Why" and "How" #### (a) Speech Reception Threshold (SRT) - **Definition:** The lowest hearing level (in dB HL) at which a patient can correctly identify **50% of spondaic (two-syllable, equally stressed) words**. - **Stimulus:** Spondaic words — *"hotdog," "railroad," "stairway," "greyhound."* - **Method:** **Modified Hughson-Westlake (ascending-descending) bracketing** or the ASHA (1979) recommended method. - **Clinical Utility:** Cross-checks the **PTA (Pure Tone Average)**; discrepancy > 12 dB suggests pseudohypacusis (feigned hearing loss). #### (b) Speech Detection Threshold (SDT) / Speech Recognition Threshold (SRS) - **Definition:** The lowest level at which the patient can *just detect* the presence of speech (SDT), often 6–8 dB lower than SRT. - **Stimulus:** Continuous discourse ("the rain in Spain…") rather than discrete words. #### (c) Most Comfortable Level (MCL) - The intensity level at which the patient reports speech is most comfortable to listen to over an extended period. - **Typical range:** 40–60 dB HL for normal hearing; varies with pathology. #### (d) Uncomfortable Level (UCL) / Threshold of Discomfort (TD) - The minimum level at which the patient reports speech is **uncomfortably loud**. - **Clinical Utility:** Defines the **dynamic range of hearing**; critical for **hearing aid fitting** and determining recruitment (abnormal loudness growth in sensorineural loss). #### (e) Speech Discrimination Score (SDS) / Word Recognition Score (WRS) - **Definition:** The **percentage of phonetically balanced (PB) words** correctly identified when presented at a comfortable level (usually MCL + 35 dB or MCL + 40 dB). - **Stimulus:** PB-50 (50-word) or PB-100 (100-word) lists — Harvard/IAL lists. - **Interpretation:** - **90–100%:** Normal / mild conductive loss - **75–89%:** Sensorineural loss, mild–moderate - **60–74%:** Moderate sensorineural loss - **<50%:** Severe loss / retrocochlear pathology (e.g., acoustic neuroma) - **Why it matters:** Two patients with identical audiograms can have wildly different word recognition — speech audiometry measures the **functional, real-world hearing ability**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Pure Tone Average (PTA)** | $PTA = \dfrac{HL_{500} + HL_{1000} + HL_{2000}}{3}$ | dB HL | Used to cross-check SRT | | **Speech Discrimination Score** | $SDS = \dfrac{\text{Correct words}}{\text{Total words}} \times 100$ | % | At MCL + 35 or 40 dB | | **Dynamic Range** | $DR = UCL - SRT$ | dB | Hearing aid fitting parameter | | **Half-Intensity / Half-Level Rule** | $I_2 = I_1 \cdot 10^{-0.15} \approx 0.707\,I_1$ | relative | –3 dB SPL = half acoustic intensity | | **Articulation Index (AI)** | $AI = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot \dfrac{E_i - E_{\text{ambient}}}{E_{\text{reference}}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Weighted sum over critical bands; AI > 0.5 ⇒ intelligible speech | | **Speech Spectrum Average** | Long-term average ≈ **65 dB SPL** at 1 m | dB SPL | Reference for VU meter calibration | | **SRT–PTA Agreement** | $\vert SRT - PTA \vert \leq 6$ dB | dB | Standard clinical agreement rule | | **Conversion: dB SPL → dB HL** | $dB\,HL = dB\,SPL - RETSPL$ | dB | RETSPL = Reference Equivalent Threshold SPL per ANSI S3.6 | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Point:** The **RETSPL** values (Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Levels) are frequency-specific constants defined in **ANSI S3.6-2010**. For example, RETSPL for TDH-39 supra-aural headphones at 1000 Hz is **7.5 dB SPL** — meaning a 0 dB HL tone requires 7.5 dB SPL output. ### 2.4 Calibration Methodology 1. Set the VU meter to read **0 dB** using a **1000 Hz calibration tone** recorded at the beginning of every test tape. 2. This 0 dB reference corresponds to a peak speech level of approximately **65 dB SPL** at the listener's eardrum when played through the audiometer. 3. **Biological calibration** is then performed on a group of normal-hearing listeners (10–20 otologically normal subjects, ages 18–30) to verify that the recorded material yields expected SRTs (within ±5 dB of ANSI standard). 4. Daily **exhaustive biological calibration** and weekly **acoustic calibration** are KTU-standard protocols. ### 2.5 Engineering Significance - Speech audiometry bridges the gap between the **objective audiogram (pure tones)** and the **subjective real-world hearing experience**. - Essential for **hearing aid prescription** (NAL-NL2, DSL-5 targets use SRT, MCL, UCL). - Critical for **cochlear implant candidacy evaluation**. - Used in **occupational hearing conservation** programs under OSHA regulations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why "Half-Intensity" Corresponds to a –3 dB Change The relationship between sound intensity $I$ and sound pressure level in dB SPL is: $$L = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ where $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$. **Step 1:** Suppose we have an initial intensity $I_1$. Compute its level $L_1$: $$L_1 = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_1}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Reduce the intensity to half: $I_2 = \dfrac{I_1}{2}$. Compute $L_2$: $$L_2 = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_2}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_1}{2 \cdot I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Apply logarithm property $\log(ab) = \log(a) + \log(b)$: $$L_2 = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_1}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$$ $$L_2 = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(0.5)$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the numerical term: $$10 \log_{10}(0.5) = 10 \times (-0.30103) = -3.0103\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore, halving the intensity corresponds to: $$\Delta L = L_2 - L_1 \approx -3\,\text{dB SPL}$$ This is the **–3 dB = half-intensity** rule. Engineering insight: hearing loss is often reported in dB, so a 3 dB increase in hearing loss represents a **doubling of acoustic intensity** required to reach threshold. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Speech Discrimination Score Calculation **Given data from a clinical test:** - 50-word PB-50 list presented at MCL + 35 dB - Patient correctly repeats 42 words - PTA at 500, 1000, 2000 Hz is 32 dB HL - MCL = 50 dB HL - UCL = 90 dB HL **Step 1: Compute SDS using the standard percentage formula:** $$SDS = \frac{\text{Correct words}}{\text{Total words}} \times 100 = \frac{42}{50} \times 100$$ $$SDS = 84\%$$ **Step 2: Compute Dynamic Range (DR):** Since SRT is not given, we first estimate it as PTA (per clinical rule SRT ≈ PTA for sensorineural loss): $$SRT \approx PTA = 32\,\text{dB HL}$$ $$DR = UCL - SRT = 90 - 32 = 58\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 3: Compute the Presentation Level (PL) used for the discrimination test:** $$PL = MCL + 35 = 50 + 35 = 85\,\text{dB HL}$$ **Step 4: Check whether PL is below UCL to ensure no discomfort:** $$PL = 85\,\text{dB HL} \quad < \quad UCL = 90\,\text{dB HL} \quad \text{✓ (acceptable)}$$ **Step 5: Clinical interpretation:** SDS of **84%** with a PTA of **32 dB HL** is consistent with a **mild-to-moderate sensorineural hearing loss** with good word recognition — a good hearing aid candidate. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Articulation Index (AI) Simplified Calculation The **Articulation Index** predicts the intelligibility of speech from the S/N ratio across critical frequency bands. A simplified 1-band form: $$AI = \frac{E - E_{\text{ambient}}}{30\,\text{dB}} \quad \text{(clipped to } 0 \le AI \le 1)$$ where: - $E$ is the speech level in dB at the listener's ear - $E_{\text{ambient}}$ is the ambient noise level in dB **Step 1:** A patient has a PTA of 40 dB HL. Speech is presented at MCL + 40 = 90 dB SPL. Ambient room noise is 40 dB SPL. $$AI = \frac{90 - 40}{30} = \frac{50}{30} = 1.67$$ **Step 2:** Clip to maximum value: $$AI = \min(1.67,\, 1.0) = 1.0$$ **Step 3:** Interpretation: With AI = 1.0, the speech is expected to be **fully intelligible** (≈ 100% PB-word recognition). This confirms that simply amplifying speech above noise yields full intelligibility if the dynamic range permits. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Test Selection Guide | Patient Complaint | Recommended Test Battery | Expected Finding | |---|---|---| | Can't hear soft speech | SRT | SRT > 25 dB HL | | Hearing but not understanding | SDS at MCL + 40 | Reduced SDS | | "Everything is too loud" (recruitment) | UCL | UCL ≤ 70 dB HL, dynamic range < 30 dB | | Suspected pseudohypacusis | SRT vs PTA | SRT–PTA > 12 dB discrepancy | | Cochlear implant candidacy | Full SRT, MCL, UCL, SDS | SDS < 50%, DR < 30 dB | | Hearing aid verification | Real-ear measurement + SRT, MCL, UCL | Match NAL-NL2 targets | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Define Speech Reception Threshold (SRT). Mention the type of stimulus words used.** **Model Answer:** Speech Reception Threshold (SRT) is defined as the **lowest hearing level (in dB HL)** at which a patient can correctly identify **50% of a list of spondaic words** presented to the test ear. Spondaic words are two-syllable words with **equal stress on both syllables** (e.g., *"baseball," "airplane," "hotdog," "railroad"*). The SRT is clinically cross-checked with the **Pure Tone Average (PTA at 500, 1000, 2000 Hz)**; the two values should agree within **± 6 dB** for a valid test. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for stimulus type, 1 for clinical significance)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** **What is Speech Discrimination Score (SDS)? How is it clinically interpreted?** **Model Answer:** Speech Discrimination Score (SDS), also called Word Recognition Score (WRS), is the **percentage of phonetically balanced (PB) words** correctly identified by a patient when presented at a comfortable level (typically **MCL + 35 dB**). The score is computed as: $$SDS = \frac{\text{Correct words}}{\text{Total words}} \times 100\,\%$$ **Clinical interpretation:** - **90–100%** → Normal / conductive loss - **75–89%** → Mild–moderate sensorineural loss - **60–74%** → Moderate sensorineural loss - **< 60%** → Severe loss / retrocochlear pathology SDS of < 50% in a patient with mild audiometric loss raises suspicion of **retrocochlear lesion (e.g., acoustic neuroma)**. **(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for stimulus, 1 for clinical interpretation)** --- ### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]** **(a)** Draw the **block diagram of a speech audiometer** and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **SRT and Speech Discrimination Score** tests in detail. Describe the procedure, stimulus material, and clinical significance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram — 7 marks** *(Draw the block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 above.)* Function of each block: 1. **Speech Source:** Provides the test signal — either a live voice microphone (electret condenser) or a pre-recorded standardized tape/CD/digital file. Recorded material is preferred because live voice is auditor-dependent and not standardized. 2. **Calibration Tone Oscillator:** Generates a 1000 Hz reference tone used to set the VU meter to 0 dB before each test session. 3. **Input Selector Switch:** Routes either the live voice, recorded speech, or calibration tone to the preamplifier. 4. **Preamplifier:** Low-noise voltage amplifier providing initial gain control for the speech signal. 5. **VU (Volume Unit) Meter:** Visual indicator; the operator adjusts the input so the VU meter reads **0 dB during the calibration tone**, ensuring consistent peak speech level of ~65 dB SPL. 6. **Attenuator Network:** Precision step attenuator providing **1 dB or 2 dB steps** (range typically –10 to +120 dB HL) to vary the intensity presented to the patient. 7. **Output Power Amplifier:** Boosts the attenuated signal to drive the transducer. 8. **Transducer Selector:** Routes the output to the appropriate transducer — TDH-39/49 supra-aural headphones, ER-3A insert earphones, bone vibrator, or free-field speaker. 9. **Masking Noise Generator:** Provides contralateral masking noise (narrow-band or speech-spectrum) when needed. 10. **Patient Response Microphone / Panel:** Captures or accepts the patient's verbal response for the audiologist's scoring. **(b) SRT & SDS — 7 marks** **SRT (Speech Reception Threshold):** - **Stimulus:** Spondaic word lists (e.g., CID W-1, CID W-2 lists). - **Procedure:** The Modified Hughson–Westlake bracketing method is used. Starting from a clearly audible level, the intensity is decreased in 10 dB steps until the patient misses, then increased in 5 dB steps. The lowest level at which the patient correctly identifies **≥ 50% of words (≥ 3 out of 6)** is recorded as the SRT. - **Clinical Significance:** - Cross-checks the audiogram (SRT–PTA ≤ 6 dB is normal agreement). - Discrepancy > 12 dB suggests **pseudohypacusis** or **functional hearing loss**. - Provides a baseline for the speech discrimination test (presented at MCL + 35 dB). **SDS (Speech Discrimination Score):** - **Stimulus:** PB-50 or PB-100 word lists (Harvard, NU-6, IAL). - **Procedure:** Words are presented at **MCL + 35 dB** (or MCL + 40 dB in some clinics) in quiet. The patient repeats each word; the audiologist scores correct responses. The score is expressed as a percentage. - **Formula:** $SDS = \dfrac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Total}} \times 100\,\%$ - **Clinical Significance:** - Measures **functional, real-world hearing ability**, not just detection. - Reduced SDS with mild audiogram suggests **retrocochlear pathology**. - Used for **hearing aid candidacy assessment**. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain the concepts of **MCL (Most Comfortable Level)** and **UCL (Uncomfortable Level)**. How is the dynamic range of hearing determined, and what is its clinical importance in **hearing aid fitting**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **calibration procedure** of a speech audiometer. Explain the significance of ANSI S3.6 and RETSPL. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) MCL & UCL — 7 marks** **Most Comfortable Level (MCL):** MCL is the hearing level (in dB HL) at which the patient reports speech is **most comfortable to listen to** for an extended period without strain. The audiologist presents running speech at varying levels; the patient indicates (by pointing to a scale or by report) the level they find most pleasant. For normal-hearing listeners, MCL is typically **40–55 dB HL**. In **recruitment-positive** patients (e.g., with cochlear pathology), MCL may be abnormally low. **Uncomfortable Level (UCL) / Threshold of Discomfort (TD):** UCL is the **minimum hearing level** at which the patient reports speech becomes uncomfortably loud. The audiologist ascends slowly, asking the patient to signal at the first sign of discomfort. UCL is typically **85–95 dB HL** in normal hearing but may be **≤ 70 dB HL** in patients with recruitment (sensorineural loss), indicating a **collapsed dynamic range**. **Dynamic Range (DR):** $$DR = UCL - SRT \quad \text{[in dB]}$$ For example: $DR = 95 - 20 = 75$ dB (normal) vs. $DR = 75 - 20 = 55$ dB (mild SNHL) vs. $DR = 80 - 50 = 30$ dB (severe SNHL with recruitment). **Clinical Importance in Hearing Aid Fitting:** 1. **Output Limiting:** The hearing aid's maximum output (OSPL90) must be set **below UCL** to avoid discomfort and further damage. 2. **Gain Calculation:** The required gain equals **SRT (target) – patient's SRT**, with the entire output range fitting within MCL to UCL. 3. **Prescription Formulas:** **NAL-NL2** and **DSL-5** use SRT, MCL, and UCL as primary inputs to compute target gain and output. 4. **Compression:** Patients with narrow dynamic range (< 30 dB) require **wide dynamic range compression (WDRC)** circuits. **(b) Calibration Procedure — 7 marks** **Calibration Procedure (Sketch — see Section 4.1 Block Diagram):** 1. **Daily Acoustic Calibration:** - Set the audiometer on a reference subject (or a 2-cc coupler for electroacoustic calibration). - Switch input to the **calibration tone oscillator** (1000 Hz). - Adjust the input gain so the VU meter reads **0 dB** at the start of the recorded word list's carrier level. 2. **Biological Calibration:** - Test **10–20 otologically normal listeners** (18–30 years, thresholds ≤ 15 dB HL). - Present the recorded word list; compute mean SRT. - The mean SRT should be within **± 5 dB of 0 dB HL** (i.e., 0 dB HL corresponds to 19–20 dB SPL for TDH-39, but the audiometer's dB HL scale normalizes this). 3. **Exhaustive Calibration (ANSI S3.6 / IEC 60645):** - Output SPL is measured in a 6-cc coupler (supra-aural) or 2-cc coupler (insert) using a sound level meter. - The audiometer's dB HL dial setting is compared to the actual SPL output; deviation must be **< ± 3 dB** at all frequencies. 4. **Periodic Calibration:** Weekly acoustic, daily biological, annual exhaustive. **Significance of ANSI S3.6 & RETSPL:** - **ANSI S3.6-2010 (American National Standards Institute)** defines the **Reference Equivalent Threshold Sound Pressure Levels (RETSPLs)** for audiometric transducers at each frequency. - **RETSPL** is the SPL (in dB) at the eardrum/coupler that corresponds to **0 dB HL** for a normal-hearing young adult. - For TDH-39 supra-aural headphones: - 250 Hz: **25.5 dB SPL** - 1000 Hz: **7.5 dB SPL** - 4000 Hz: **9.5 dB SPL** - **Conversion:** $dB\,HL = dB\,SPL - RETSPL$ This standardization ensures that a "0 dB HL" reading represents the same threshold reference across all clinics worldwide. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing dB HL with dB SPL** — A 0 dB HL tone is NOT 0 dB SPL. Always subtract the RETSPL. > 2. **Forgetting the calibration step** — In block diagram questions, the calibration tone oscillator and VU meter are *commonly missed* by students (lose 2 marks). > 3. **Using "spondaic" vs "phonetically balanced" interchangeably** — SRT uses **spondaic**; SDS uses **PB words**. Mixing them up is a frequent error. > 4. **Forgetting to specify the formula and units** in SDS calculations — Always write $SDS = \frac{\text{correct}}{\text{total}} \times 100\%$. > 5. **Missing MCL and UCL** in long-answer questions about speech audiometry — Most students cover SRT and SDS but forget the **dynamic range**, which is the KTU-favorite test concept. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Speech Audiometer** = instrument for measuring hearing of speech signals (not pure tones). - **Core speech tests** = **SRT, SDT, MCL, UCL, SDS** (PB word score). - **SRT** = spondaic words, 50% correct, lowest level; should match PTA within **± 6 dB**. - **SDS** = PB words at MCL + 35 dB; expressed as a **percentage**. - **Dynamic Range = UCL – SRT**; critical for hearing aid fitting. - **Calibration** uses a **1000 Hz reference tone** to set VU meter to 0 dB (corresponds to ~65 dB SPL long-term average speech). - **ANSI S3.6 / IEC 60645** are the calibration standards; **RETSPL** converts dB SPL ↔ dB HL. - **MCL** (typical 40–55 dB HL) and **UCL** (typical 85–95 dB HL) bracket the comfortable listening range. - **SDS < 50%** with a mild audiogram → suspect **retrocochlear lesion**. - **Block Diagram Components (10 blocks):** Speech source, Cal tone osc., Selector, Preamplifier, VU meter, Attenuator, Power amplifier, Transducer selector, Masking generator, Patient response. - **Live voice** is auditor-biased; **recorded material (CID, NU-6, PB-50)** is the KTU-preferred standard. - **Hearing aid fitting formulas (NAL-NL2, DSL-5)** all use **SRT, MCL, UCL** as primary inputs.
